security.auth_design: CLI reads JWT from MIGRATION_TOKEN environment variable injected by Azure DevOps pipeline at runtime. Token validated against internal IdP JWKS endpoint on startup. Role extracted from JWT claims and stored in CLI context for downstream authorization checks.
security.secrets_management: AS/400 ODBC connection string and ERP API key fetched from Azure Key Vault via DefaultAzureCredential at CLI startup. Connection strings never written to logs; structured log fields use masked placeholders. Staging PostgreSQL password rotated after each migration project via Key Vault secret rotation policy.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: audit_entries table in staging PostgreSQL: id BIGSERIAL PK, run_id FK, operator_jwt_sub, action ENUM extract/validate/stage/promote/rollback/approve_duplicate, record_id, before_value_hash, after_value_hash, timestamp. Appended atomically within each operation transaction. Exported to Azure Blob Storage in NDJSON format for 7-year SOX retention.

data.entities: source_purchase_orders (run_id FK, po_number VARCHAR AS400, vendor_id VARCHAR AS400, line_items JSONB, po_date, amount_decimal, extracted_at). supplier_master_records (run_id FK, vendor_id VARCHAR AS400, vendor_name TEXT, tax_id VARCHAR, address_json JSONB, payment_terms VARCHAR, extracted_at). field_mappings (id UUID PK, source_field, target_erp_field, transform_rule TEXT, active BOOL). migration_runs (id UUID PK, run_type ENUM dry_run/production, status ENUM pending/extracting/staging/awaiting_approval/promoting/complete/failed, last_checkpoint_id BIGINT, started_at, completed_at). validation_errors (id BIGSERIAL PK, run_id FK, record_type ENUM po/supplier, source_id VARCHAR, error_code VARCHAR, error_detail TEXT, created_at). staging_batches (id UUID PK, run_id FK, batch_sequence INT, record_count INT, promoted BOOL, rolled_back BOOL, promoted_at). rollback_checkpoints (id BIGSERIAL PK, run_id FK, batch_id FK, erp_ids_json JSONB, created_at).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server as staging database. All staging tables in a dedicated migration schema, isolated from any shared application databases. Azure Blob Storage for HTML summary reports and SOX audit export files.
data.migration_notes: source_purchase_orders and supplier_master_records are write-once staging tables; after promotion they are marked with promoted_at and never modified. rollback_checkpoints store ERP-assigned IDs of promoted records to enable compensating DELETE calls on rollback. Field mapping rules versioned by field_mappings.active flag; only active rules applied per run.

devops.deployment_topology: Azure Container Instances with a migration Docker image built from Python 3.12-slim. Each migration phase runs as a separate ACI container task invoked by Azure DevOps pipeline stage. Staging PostgreSQL on Azure Flexible Server (Burstable B2ms for staging validation, General Purpose D4s for production run). Azure Blob Storage for reports and audit exports.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps multi-stage pipeline: build and push Docker image to ACR, run unit tests with 85% coverage gate (pytest + coverage), stage per migration phase gated by manual approval. Production promotion stage locked to change control board sign-off in Azure DevOps environment approval gate.
devops.backup_and_recovery: PostgreSQL automated backups every 6 hours with 7-day point-in-time recovery. Pre-promotion snapshot of staging database taken as Azure Flexible Server on-demand backup before each production promotion run. Rollback_checkpoints provide application-level recovery without needing DB restore.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a SOX-compliant one-time migration automation tool with Python Click CLI and PostgreSQL staging database on Azure. The key architectural decisions are: chunked ODBC extraction with checkpoint persistence for resumability across the 5-hour AS/400 window, all-or-nothing batch promotion with compensating ERP API calls on rollback, and an append-only audit_entries table satisfying SOX segregation-of-duties requirements. No frontend is needed; HTML reports serve the data steward review workflow.
target_output.title: AS/400 to Cloud ERP Migration Automation Tool Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 Click CLI tool running on Azure Container Instances that extracts purchase order and supplier master data from on-premise AS/400 systems via ODBC, applies configurable field transformation rules, performs fuzzy duplicate detection on supplier records, and loads data into a cloud ERP via REST API with all-or-nothing batch promotion and automatic rollback-to-checkpoint on failure. PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server serves as the staging database and audit log. The tool enforces SOX segregation of duties by requiring a data steward approval JWT before production promotion, with a 7-year-retained append-only audit trail.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-binary Python CLI package with Click command groups mirroring the five migration phases: extract (AS/400 ODBC to PostgreSQL staging), validate (schema and field-level ERP constraint checks), stage (apply field_mappings transformation rules, write staging_batches), detect-duplicates (fuzzy matching on supplier tax IDs and names, emit HTML report), and promote (ERP REST API load with token-bucket rate limiter and compensating rollback). A shared CLI context object carries the operator JWT, run_id, and staging DB connection across all commands. Azure Container Instances provide on-demand containerized execution for each phase without persistent cluster overhead. Azure Key Vault supplies AS/400 ODBC credentials and ERP API keys at runtime via DefaultAzureCredential. The append-only audit_entries table in PostgreSQL captures every record-level transformation and promotion decision with JWT subject for SOX traceability.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, Click CLI framework, pyodbc for AS/400 ODBC connectivity, psycopg2 for PostgreSQL staging DB, httpx async client for ERP REST API with token-bucket rate limiter. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server for staging and audit storage. Infrastructure: Azure Container Instances for migration run execution, Azure Container Registry, Azure Key Vault for secrets, Azure Blob Storage for HTML reports and SOX audit NDJSON exports. CI/CD: Azure DevOps multi-stage pipelines with manual approval gates. Observability: Grafana plus Prometheus for run metrics, structured JSON logging per record transformation.
target_output.functional_feature_map: migrate extract: AS/400 ODBC cursor-paginated extraction of PO_HEADER/PO_LINE and VENDOR_MASTER tables in configurable chunks of 10,000 records, writing to source_purchase_orders and supplier_master_records staging tables with checkpoint writes after each chunk. migrate validate: schema validation of every staging record against ERP field constraints, writing per-record failure reasons to validation_errors table, emitting pass/fail count to operator stdout. migrate stage: applies active field_mappings transformation rules to convert AS/400 field formats to ERP schema, groups records into staging_batches of 5,000, writes rollback_checkpoints capturing source IDs per batch. migrate detect-duplicates: fuzzy matching of incoming supplier_master_records against existing ERP vendor list using name, tax_id, and address similarity scores, flagging matches above threshold for data steward review in HTML report. migrate promote: sequential batch promotion via ERP REST API at 100 records/second, requiring data steward approval JWT claim before execution, rolling back current batch and emitting structured error report on any ERP API failure. migrate rollback: restores staging tables to last rollback_checkpoint and issues compensating DELETE calls to ERP for any committed records in the failed batch.
target_output.system_components: Migration CLI Container (Python 3.12 Click, ACI, one container per phase invocation). PostgreSQL Staging DB (Azure Flexible Server, migration schema with source tables, validation_errors, staging_batches, rollback_checkpoints, audit_entries). AS/400 ODBC Source (pyodbc connection over TLS, read-only service account, off-peak window midnight to 5am). Cloud ERP REST API Target (httpx async client, token-bucket rate limiter at 100 rec/sec, bulk endpoint for batch promotion). Azure Key Vault (AS/400 connection string, ERP API key, operator JWT signing key). Azure Blob Storage (HTML duplicate detection reports, SOX audit NDJSON exports). Azure DevOps Pipeline (phase-gated execution with manual approval stages).
target_output.workflows: Extraction and Checkpoint: Azure DevOps pipeline triggers ACI container with migrate extract command. CLI fetches AS/400 credentials from Key Vault, opens ODBC cursor on PO_HEADER joined with PO_LINE and VENDOR_MASTER, paginates in 10,000-record chunks. After each chunk, inserts rows to source_purchase_orders or supplier_master_records and writes migration_runs.last_checkpoint_id. If the 5am extraction window closes mid-run, subsequent invocation resumes from last_checkpoint_id without re-extracting committed chunks. Duplicate Detection and Approval: After staging, migrate detect-duplicates compares incoming supplier_master_records against ERP vendor list using rapidfuzz token-sort ratio on name and exact match on tax_id. Matches above 85% similarity threshold are written to a suspects table and rendered into an HTML report uploaded to Azure Blob Storage. Data steward reviews the report and invokes migrate promote with a signed approval JWT embedding the run_id and steward identity; the CLI validates the JWT claim before allowing promotion to proceed. Rollback on Promotion Failure: If any ERP API call in a staging_batch returns a 4xx or 5xx response, the promotion command halts the batch, issues compensating DELETE requests to the ERP for all records in rollback_checkpoints.erp_ids_json that were committed in this batch, reverses staging_batches.promoted flag to false, writes a ROLLBACK action row to audit_entries with the failure detail, and exits with a non-zero status code causing the Azure DevOps pipeline to fail the stage and alert operators.
target_output.data_model: source_purchase_orders (id BIGSERIAL PK, run_id UUID FK, po_number VARCHAR(20), vendor_id VARCHAR(10), po_date DATE, amount_decimal NUMERIC(14,2), line_items JSONB, extracted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, promoted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). supplier_master_records (id BIGSERIAL PK, run_id UUID FK, vendor_id VARCHAR(10), vendor_name TEXT, tax_id VARCHAR(20), address_json JSONB, payment_terms VARCHAR(30), extracted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, promoted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, duplicate_flag BOOL DEFAULT FALSE). field_mappings (id UUID PK, source_field VARCHAR(50), target_erp_field VARCHAR(50), transform_rule TEXT, active BOOL, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). migration_runs (id UUID PK, run_type ENUM dry_run/production, status ENUM pending/extracting/staging/awaiting_approval/promoting/complete/failed, last_checkpoint_id BIGINT, operator_jwt_sub TEXT, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). validation_errors (id BIGSERIAL PK, run_id UUID FK, record_type ENUM po/supplier, source_id VARCHAR(20), error_code VARCHAR(30), error_detail TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). staging_batches (id UUID PK, run_id UUID FK, batch_sequence INT, record_count INT, promoted BOOL DEFAULT FALSE, rolled_back BOOL DEFAULT FALSE, promoted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). rollback_checkpoints (id BIGSERIAL PK, run_id UUID FK, batch_id UUID FK, erp_ids_json JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_entries (id BIGSERIAL PK, run_id UUID FK, operator_jwt_sub TEXT, action ENUM extract/validate/stage/promote/rollback/approve_duplicate, record_id BIGINT, before_value_hash CHAR(64), after_value_hash CHAR(64), timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API exposed by this tool. External integration points: AS/400 ODBC source (pyodbc, SELECT-only service account on PO_HEADER, PO_LINE, VENDOR_MASTER tables, TLS required). ERP REST API target (POST /api/v1/vendors bulk endpoint accepting up to 500 records per request, POST /api/v1/purchase-orders bulk endpoint accepting up to 500 records per request, DELETE /api/v1/vendors/:erp_id for rollback compensation, DELETE /api/v1/purchase-orders/:erp_id for rollback compensation). Azure Key Vault REST (DefaultAzureCredential managed identity, GET secrets/as400-odbc-connection-string and secrets/erp-api-key). Azure Blob Storage REST (PUT HTML reports to migration-reports container, PUT NDJSON audit exports to sox-audit container).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Secrets: AS/400 ODBC connection string and ERP API key fetched from Azure Key Vault via managed identity at container startup; never written to logs or stdout. Auth: Operator JWT validated against internal IdP JWKS on CLI startup; role claim checked before each phase command. Segregation of duties: migration_admin role permits extract/validate/stage/rollback; data_steward role required for duplicate approval JWT claim; promote command rejects invocation if JWT role is not data_steward. AS/400 ODBC access via read-only service account; no write operations on source system. Audit trail: audit_entries table is INSERT-only (no UPDATE or DELETE); exported to Azure Blob Storage sox-audit container in NDJSON format after each run; retained 7 years per SOX mandate. SOX compliance: change control board approval required in Azure DevOps environment gate before production promote stage executes. TLS enforced on all ODBC and HTTPS connections.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure Container Instances: one ACI container per migration phase, launched by Azure DevOps pipeline stage. Docker image built from Python 3.12-slim, pushed to Azure Container Registry. ACI environment variables injected by pipeline: MIGRATION_TOKEN (operator JWT), KEY_VAULT_URI, STAGING_DB_HOST. Azure DevOps multi-stage pipeline: build (pytest 85% coverage gate, flake8 lint, docker build and ACR push), extract (ACI task, midnight schedule), validate (ACI task, auto-trigger after extract success), stage (ACI task, auto-trigger after validate), detect-duplicates (ACI task, auto-trigger after stage, uploads HTML report), promote (ACI task, manual approval gate requiring data steward environment approval in Azure DevOps). Pre-production environment: staging-validation namespace in Azure using reduced record count samples from AS/400 test partition. Rollback stage always available as manual trigger from pipeline run history.
target_output.observability: Grafana dashboard connected to Prometheus scraping ACI container metrics. Custom metrics exposed via prometheus-client: migration_records_extracted_total (counter by record_type), migration_validation_errors_total (counter by error_code), migration_promotion_rate_per_second (gauge), migration_rollback_events_total (counter). Structured JSON logs per record transformation written to stdout and forwarded to Azure Monitor Logs. HTML summary report generated after detect-duplicates and promote phases: total records extracted, validation error count by type, duplicate suspects flagged, promoted count, failed count, rollback events. Azure DevOps pipeline email notifications on stage failure.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $180 total for single migration project: ACI compute for 5 phase runs at $0.0000025/vCore-second ($30 total), Azure Flexible Server B2ms for staging ($45/month for 2-month project), Azure Blob Storage ($5 for reports and audit exports), Azure Container Registry ($10), Azure DevOps included in enterprise license. For subsequent delta sync projects, ACI cost remains under $50 per incremental run due to smaller record volumes. No persistent cluster costs after migration project completes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Dry-Run Validation 4 weeks: implement extract command with AS/400 ODBC pagination and checkpoint writes, validate command with ERP schema constraint checks, stage command with field_mappings transformation rules, rollback command with compensating ERP API calls, PostgreSQL staging schema, Azure DevOps pipeline with approval gates, structured logging and HTML report generation. Execute dry run against full AS/400 dataset with ERP sandbox environment, validate error rates and duplicate detection accuracy. Phase 2 Production Migration 4 weeks: data steward review of duplicate detection HTML report and mapping rule adjustments based on dry-run findings, change control board approval, execute production promote run against live ERP, verify record counts and spot-check transformed records, archive staging data and export SOX audit NDJSON to Azure Blob Storage. Phase 3 Post-Migration 2 weeks: monitor ERP data quality with business users, address any field mapping corrections via re-promotion of specific record subsets, deliver final migration audit report to compliance team.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python: ruff linter and mypy strict type checking enforced in CI. 85% test coverage via pytest with unit tests for every field transformation rule and rollback compensation logic. Integration tests against dockerized PostgreSQL and ERP API mock (respx for httpx). All connection strings and API keys must use Key Vault lookup functions; any direct string literal containing password or key is a CI lint failure. Click commands must emit JSON-structured log lines for every record-level action. PR requires review by one senior engineer and sign-off by security team on any changes to audit_entries write path.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: AS/400 ODBC extraction exceeds the 5-hour off-peak window for large datasets above 1.8M records. Mitigation: chunk-level checkpointing enables multi-night extraction across consecutive windows; migration_runs.last_checkpoint_id tracks progress across invocations. Risk: ERP API returns partial success on bulk endpoint (some records accepted, some rejected within one request), making rollback compensation ambiguous. Mitigation: use single-record ERP endpoint for promotion batches above the error rate threshold detected in dry run; accept lower throughput for higher atomicity confidence. Tradeoff: storing rollback_checkpoints.erp_ids_json as JSONB rather than a normalized table trades referential integrity for simpler batch-level compensation logic. Tradeoff: HTML report for duplicate detection rather than an interactive review UI reduces build cost but requires data steward to work from a static document, increasing the risk of approval without thorough review.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose Azure Container Instances over AKS given one-time execution pattern with no persistent workload requiring cluster management. Confirmed chunked ODBC pagination over full-table ODBC dump to stay within AS/400 off-peak window. Selected rapidfuzz for supplier duplicate detection over exact-match only after analysis of AS/400 data showed 12% of duplicate vendors had name variations. SOX audit trail implemented in append-only PostgreSQL table rather than Azure Monitor Logs to ensure tamper evidence within the migration database.

metadata.notes: SOX-compliant AS/400-to-ERP migration automation tool with Python Click CLI, PostgreSQL staging, and Azure Container Instances execution.

profile.domain: Supply Chain AI & Demand Forecasting

project_goal.value: Demand forecasting AI system ingesting historical sales, seasonal signals, and promotional calendars to produce SKU-level replenishment recommendations at scale across thousands of warehouse locations
project_goal.rationale: Core product capability enabling data-driven procurement decisions for consumer goods distributors

target_users.value: Supply chain planners reviewing replenishment recommendations via dashboard; downstream procurement systems consuming recommendations via REST API
target_users.rationale: Two distinct consumers: human planners and machine-to-machine procurement integration

project_class.rationale: Core product value is ML forecasting pipeline with model versioning and drift detection, not a generic CRUD app

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, ai_llm, analytics, batch_jobs, public_api
capabilities.rationale: Planner dashboard, FastAPI backend, ClickHouse analytics, ML forecasting, nightly batch retraining, and public procurement API all required

complexity_level.rationale: Thousands of SKU-location pairs across hundreds of warehouses with nightly retraining and horizontal compute partitioning

risk_level.rationale: Incorrect forecasts cause inventory imbalance but no safety or compliance risk; model drift monitoring mitigates impact

data_sensitivity.rationale: Distributor sales and inventory data is proprietary but no PII or financial transaction data

external_exposure.rationale: Dashboard and API require Auth0 JWT authentication; no public unauthenticated access

access_model.value: Auth0 JWT with RBAC: planner role (read recommendations, submit overrides, approve batches), analyst role (read analytics, export data), system role for procurement API integration
access_model.rationale: Planners need write access for overrides; procurement systems need API-only access

feature_scope.value: Nightly SKU-level demand forecasting, replenishment recommendation generation, planner dashboard with override capability, batch approval workflow, procurement API, model versioning and drift monitoring, promotional calendar integration
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for production deployment

mvp_scope.value: Nightly forecast batch for top-500 SKUs across all warehouse locations, replenishment recommendation dashboard with override, procurement REST API for approved batches
mvp_scope.rationale: Reduced SKU scope for pilot validation before full catalog rollout

security_baseline.value: Auth0 JWT RS256, HTTPS only, API rate limiting 500 req/min per system client, model serving endpoint authenticated
security_baseline.rationale: Internal system with private_authenticated exposure requires JWT but not MFA

frontend_stack.rationale: SSR for initial dashboard load; React Query for recommendation data polling

backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI async performance for recommendation API; Python ML ecosystem for forecasting pipeline

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for time-series analytics and hot recommendation queries; Redis 7 for hot recommendation cache; GCS for model artifacts and historical data lake
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse columnar storage delivers sub-second aggregation across millions of SKU-location-date rows

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot in us-central1 for all services and batch workers
hosting_target.rationale: Autopilot handles horizontal pod scaling for batch compute partitioning without cluster management overhead

privacy_retention_policy.value: Historical sales data retained indefinitely for model training; forecast_runs and replenishment_recommendations retained 2 years; model_versions retained with lineage for 5 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Long sales history improves forecast accuracy; model lineage required for audit and reproducibility

future_scope.value: Causal inference for promotion lift measurement, external market signal integration (weather, economic indicators), multi-echelon inventory optimization
future_scope.rationale: Advanced forecasting capabilities planned after pilot validation of base model accuracy

constraints.value: Nightly batch must complete within 4 hours; procurement API SLA 200ms p99; ClickHouse analytics queries must return within 3 seconds
constraints.rationale: Planners access dashboard before morning shift; procurement systems have strict latency SLA

observability_baseline.value: Grafana plus Prometheus on GKE, model drift metrics via custom Prometheus exporter, Cloud Build pipeline alerts on training job failures
observability_baseline.rationale: ML system requires both infrastructure and model-quality observability

execution_preference.value: ML-oriented iterative delivery: base model in production before expanding feature engineering or SKU scope
execution_preference.rationale: Validate forecast accuracy on reduced scope before scaling to full catalog

llm_integration.value: LLM-assisted anomaly explanation: GPT-4o via Azure OpenAI generates natural-language explanations for forecast anomalies flagged by drift detector, displayed as tooltip annotations in planner dashboard
llm_integration.rationale: Planners requested human-readable explanations for unexpected forecast spikes or drops to accelerate override decision-making

project.goal: SKU-level demand forecasting and replenishment recommendation system
project.target_users: Supply chain planners and procurement systems
project.timeline: Pilot with top-500 SKUs in Q2, full catalog in Q3

frontend.state: React Query for recommendation polling, Zustand for dashboard filter state

backend.framework: FastAPI with async SQLAlchemy for ClickHouse reads, httpx for LLM calls

security.auth: Auth0 RS256 JWT
security.transport: HTTPS only, GKE Ingress TLS termination

data.lake: GCS Parquet for historical sales data lake

reasoner_reviews.product: The planner override workflow is a critical trust-building feature; planners who cannot explain or override AI recommendations will not adopt the system. The LLM anomaly explanation feature should be surfaced prominently in the dashboard as a key differentiator. Batch approval before procurement API exposure is the right gate to prevent unreviewed recommendations from reaching procurement.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: ClickHouse materialized views for pre-aggregated recommendation summaries are essential to hit 3-second dashboard query SLAs across millions of SKU-location rows. The SKU-location partitioning strategy for batch workers needs to account for hot SKUs having disproportionate compute time; a weighted partition assignment based on historical SKU complexity scores will reduce tail worker latency. Redis hot cache for top-1000 SKUs by query frequency eliminates most ClickHouse read pressure on the procurement API path.
reasoner_reviews.security: The LLM integration via Azure OpenAI must not send raw sales data in prompts; only aggregated anomaly delta values and SKU category labels should be included in the anomaly explanation prompt to prevent proprietary distributor data from leaving the GCP environment in uncontrolled form.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 4-hour batch window requires validating partition counts. With 50,000 SKUs across 200 warehouses yielding 10M SKU-location pairs, 50 worker pods each processing 200,000 pairs in 4 hours is feasible using LightGBM inference at approximately 50ms per pair including I/O.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 2 plan addressed the ClickHouse materialized view concern but did not specify the LLM prompt data minimization controls. The LLM explanation feature remains a risk without explicit data sanitization before Azure OpenAI API calls.

backend.service_design: Three Python FastAPI services: forecast-api (recommendation queries, override submission, batch approval, procurement API), training-orchestrator (nightly batch job scheduling, worker pod coordination, drift metric computation), anomaly-explainer (LLM proxy service handling prompt construction with data minimization and Azure OpenAI calls). Shared Pydantic models package for SKU, recommendation, and forecast domain types.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful at /api/v1 with Auth0 JWT middleware. Procurement public API at /v1/recommendations/:warehouse_id with system role JWT. Planner override at PATCH /v1/recommendations/:id/override with planner role check. Batch approval at POST /v1/forecast-runs/:id/approve. LLM explanation at GET /v1/recommendations/:id/explain (async, returns explanation or 'computing' status).
backend.llm_integration_design: anomaly-explainer service receives anomaly context (sku_category, forecast_delta_pct, week_over_week_comparison, promotional_event_flag) from forecast-api. Constructs prompt with only aggregated deltas and category labels, never raw sales figures. Calls Azure OpenAI GPT-4o via httpx with 10s timeout. Caches explanation in Redis for 24 hours keyed by recommendation_id. Falls back to template explanation if LLM call fails.
backend.scaling_notes: Batch worker pods partitioned by sku_id hash modulo worker_count. GKE Autopilot scales workers from 0 to 50 pods at batch start, scales back to 0 after completion to minimize cost. ClickHouse horizontal sharding by warehouse_location_id distributes write load during batch result ingestion.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 App Router with route groups: /(planner) for recommendation dashboard and override workflow, /(analytics) for ClickHouse-backed trend charts, /(admin) for model version management and drift alerts.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Recommendation Dashboard: filterable grid of replenishment_recommendations by warehouse and SKU category, sortable by urgency score, with inline override form and LLM explanation tooltip. Forecast Timeline: Recharts line chart overlaying historical_sales, forecast_runs prediction line, and seasonal_adjustments. Batch Approval: paginated list of pending forecast_runs with summary statistics for planner sign-off before procurement API exposure.
frontend.state_management: React Query polling /api/v1/recommendations every 60 seconds during active planner session. Zustand stores active warehouse filter, SKU category filter, and override modal state. Next.js server-side data fetch for initial recommendation grid on page load to avoid flash of empty state.

security.auth_design: Auth0 tenant with three application clients: planner-dashboard (SPA, PKCE), procurement-api-client (client credentials, system role), analyst-dashboard (SPA, PKCE, read-only scopes). JWT RS256 verified by FastAPI dependency using python-jose JWKS endpoint cache.
security.authorization_model: FastAPI route decorators enforce role claims: planner role required for POST /overrides and POST /approve, system role required for /v1/recommendations procurement endpoint, analyst role for GET /analytics. ClickHouse row-level warehouse_id filtering applied from JWT claims for multi-tenant planners managing subsets of warehouses.
security.privacy_controls: LLM prompt construction in anomaly-explainer must strip warehouse_id, distributor_name, and raw sales_volume fields. Only sku_category_label, forecast_delta_pct rounded to nearest 5%, and promotional_event_flag BOOL included in prompt. Output cached in Redis to minimize API calls and exposure frequency.

data.entities: sku_catalog (sku_id, category, description, unit_of_measure, active). warehouse_locations (location_id, region, capacity_units). historical_sales (sku_id, location_id, sale_date, units_sold, promo_flag) -- ClickHouse partitioned by toYYYYMM(sale_date). forecast_runs (id, model_version_id FK, run_date, status ENUM pending/running/complete/failed, worker_count, completed_at). replenishment_recommendations (id, forecast_run_id FK, sku_id FK, location_id FK, recommended_units, urgency_score, planner_override_units, override_justification, approved BOOL, approved_at). promotional_events (id, sku_id FK, location_id FK, event_type, lift_factor, start_date, end_date). seasonal_adjustments (sku_id, week_of_year, adjustment_factor). model_versions (id, algorithm ENUM prophet/lgbm_ensemble, training_run_id, mape_score, rmse_score, created_at, active BOOL).
data.storage_design: ClickHouse cluster (3 shards, 2 replicas each) on GKE: historical_sales and replenishment_recommendations tables; materialized view mv_recommendation_summary pre-aggregating by warehouse and urgency band. Redis 7 cluster for hot recommendation cache keyed by warehouse_id:sku_id, LLM explanation cache. GCS Parquet lake for historical_sales training snapshots and model artifacts.
data.analytics_events: Planner events tracked to ClickHouse analytics_events table: override_submitted (sku_id, location_id, original_units, override_units), batch_approved (forecast_run_id, planner_id, latency_since_run), explanation_viewed (recommendation_id, explanation_source lllm/template).

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster: forecast-api Deployment (3 replicas, HPA on CPU 60%), training-orchestrator CronJob (nightly 2am UTC), batch-worker Job (0 to 50 pods, scaled by training-orchestrator), anomaly-explainer Deployment (2 replicas). GKE Ingress with Google-managed TLS cert. Cloud Memorystore Redis for hot cache. ClickHouse Operator managing 3-shard cluster on GKE.
devops.ci_cd_design: Cloud Build triggers on main branch merge: pytest 90% coverage gate, ruff lint, mypy strict, docker build, Artifact Registry push, GKE rolling update via kubectl apply. Separate Cloud Build trigger for model training pipeline runs validation holdout MAPE check before promoting new model_version to active.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping FastAPI /metrics, ClickHouse /metrics, and custom batch job metrics. Grafana dashboards: forecast accuracy (MAPE trend per model_version), batch run duration, API latency p50/p99, recommendation approval rate, LLM call success rate and latency. Drift detector emits feature_drift_score metric per SKU category; Grafana alert when drift_score exceeds 0.15.

ISS-002.title: ClickHouse query timeout risk on unindexed recommendation filters
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan lacked materialized view specification for dashboard recommendation queries, risking 3-second SLA breach on full-table scans across millions of rows

history.note: Identified by architect_reasoner
mv_recommendation_summary materialized view specified in data subplan

ISS-003.title: Batch worker partition skew from hot SKUs
ISS-003.detail: Simple hash-modulo partitioning assigns equal record counts per worker but ignores SKU-level compute complexity differences; high-velocity SKUs with long training histories create tail worker latency that may breach the 4-hour batch window

history.note: Identified by architect_reasoner in round 2 review

focus_issues.title: Batch worker partition skew from hot SKUs
focus_issues.detail: Simple hash-modulo partitioning creates tail worker latency from high-velocity SKUs; needs weighted partition assignment based on historical SKU complexity scores

latest_recommendations: Add weighted partition assignment for batch workers based on SKU complexity scores to prevent tail worker latency breaching the 4-hour window
