previous_plan.title: Demand Forecasting AI System Architecture v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: GKE Autopilot-hosted demand forecasting system with ClickHouse analytics, LightGBM and Prophet ensemble forecasting, and Next.js planner dashboard. Round 2 added anomaly-explainer data minimization for LLM prompts and mv_recommendation_summary materialized view for dashboard query SLA compliance.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Three FastAPI services on GKE Autopilot: forecast-api, training-orchestrator CronJob, anomaly-explainer LLM proxy. ClickHouse 3-shard cluster with mv_recommendation_summary materialized view. Redis hot cache for top-SKU recommendations. Batch workers scaled 0 to 50 pods by training-orchestrator using hash-modulo SKU partitioning.
previous_plan.data_model: sku_catalog, warehouse_locations, historical_sales (ClickHouse, partitioned by toYYYYMM(sale_date)), forecast_runs, replenishment_recommendations, promotional_events, seasonal_adjustments, model_versions. Round 2 added analytics_events table for planner interaction tracking.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Auth0 RS256 JWT, RBAC with planner/analyst/system roles. anomaly-explainer data minimization: only sku_category_label, forecast_delta_pct rounded to nearest 5%, promotional_event_flag in LLM prompts. Redis explanation cache with 24-hour TTL.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 focuses on resolving ISS-003: batch worker partition skew from high-velocity SKUs. The fix is a weighted bin-packing partition assignment where the training-orchestrator pre-computes sku_complexity_scores from the previous training run's actual per-SKU inference time, then assigns SKUs to worker pods using a greedy bin-packing algorithm to equalize expected worker duration. This replaces hash-modulo assignment without changing the underlying worker pod architecture. All other plan sections from round 2 carry forward with minor refinements.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced hash-modulo SKU partitioning in training-orchestrator with weighted bin-packing assignment. training-orchestrator pre-computes sku_complexity_score (rolling 7-day average of per-SKU inference time from previous forecast_runs metrics) and assigns SKUs to worker pods using a greedy decreasing bin-pack, targeting equal expected duration per worker pod. sku_complexity_scores stored in Redis sorted set for fast lookup at batch start.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Tail worker latency reduced from estimated 5.5 hours (exceeding 4-hour window) to approximately 3.8 hours under peak SKU count, providing 12-minute safety margin. ISS-003 resolved.

target_output.title: Demand Forecasting AI System Architecture v3
target_output.executive_summary: A high-scale GKE Autopilot-hosted demand forecasting AI system for consumer goods distributors, producing SKU-level replenishment recommendations across thousands of warehouse locations using a LightGBM and Prophet ensemble nightly batch. ClickHouse 3-shard cluster with materialized views delivers sub-3-second planner dashboard queries across millions of SKU-location-date rows. GPT-4o via Azure OpenAI provides natural-language anomaly explanations with enforced data minimization. Round 3 resolves batch worker partition skew by replacing hash-modulo SKU assignment with weighted bin-packing based on sku_complexity_scores, ensuring the nightly batch completes within the 4-hour window under full 50,000-SKU catalog load.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three Python FastAPI services on GKE Autopilot in us-central1: forecast-api serving recommendation queries, override submissions, batch approvals, and the procurement REST API; training-orchestrator CronJob managing nightly batch execution with weighted bin-packing worker pod assignment; and anomaly-explainer proxying GPT-4o calls with enforced data minimization. Batch workers (0 to 50 GKE Job pods) process assigned SKU partitions against ClickHouse historical_sales, writing replenishment_recommendations results back to ClickHouse. Redis 7 via Cloud Memorystore caches hot recommendation sets and LLM anomaly explanations. ClickHouse materialized view mv_recommendation_summary pre-aggregates recommendations by warehouse and urgency band for sub-3-second dashboard queries. Auth0 RS256 JWT enforces RBAC across planner, analyst, and system roles.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, LightGBM 4.3, Prophet 1.1, Pydantic v2 for domain models, httpx async for Azure OpenAI and ERP integration. Frontend: Next.js 14, TypeScript, React Query, Recharts for forecast timeline charts. Database: ClickHouse cluster (3 shards, 2 replicas each) for time-series analytics and recommendations; Redis 7 Cloud Memorystore for hot cache and LLM cache. Storage: GCS Parquet for training data lake and model artifacts. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot, Cloud Build, Artifact Registry, Cloud Memorystore. Auth: Auth0 RS256 JWT with PKCE for SPA clients, client credentials for procurement system. Observability: Prometheus, Grafana, custom drift exporter.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Nightly Forecast Batch: training-orchestrator CronJob launches batch-worker Job pods with weighted bin-packed SKU assignments, each pod fetching assigned SKU historical_sales from ClickHouse, computing LightGBM and Prophet ensemble forecasts, and writing replenishment_recommendations with urgency_scores. Planner Dashboard: filterable recommendation grid with inline override form, LLM anomaly explanation tooltip via anomaly-explainer, and Recharts forecast timeline. Batch Approval Workflow: planner reviews pending forecast_run summary statistics and approves batch, gating procurement API exposure. Procurement API: GET /v1/recommendations/:warehouse_id returns approved recommendations as structured JSON for downstream procurement system consumption. Model Versioning: training-orchestrator computes MAPE and RMSE on holdout validation set after each training run and promotes model_version to active only if MAPE improves or stays within 2% of previous active version. Drift Monitoring: custom Prometheus exporter computing feature_drift_score per SKU category using PSI (population stability index) against training distribution baseline.
target_output.system_components: forecast-api (Python FastAPI, GKE Deployment 3 replicas, HPA CPU 60%, handles recommendation queries, overrides, approvals, and procurement API). training-orchestrator (Python CronJob 2am UTC, pre-computes sku_complexity_scores from Redis sorted set, runs greedy bin-packing to assign SKUs to worker pods, launches batch-worker GKE Job with env-var partition manifests). batch-worker (Python Job pods 0-50, each receives sku_id list via env or ConfigMap, fetches training data from ClickHouse, runs LGBMRegressor and Prophet ensemble, writes forecast_runs rows and replenishment_recommendations to ClickHouse). anomaly-explainer (Python FastAPI, GKE Deployment 2 replicas, receives anomaly context, constructs minimized prompt, calls Azure OpenAI GPT-4o, caches in Redis 24h). ClickHouse Operator cluster (3 shards, 2 replicas, historical_sales and replenishment_recommendations tables, mv_recommendation_summary materialized view). Cloud Memorystore Redis (hot recommendation cache, LLM explanation cache, sku_complexity_scores sorted set). GCS (Parquet training data lake, model artifact store). Next.js 14 dashboard on GKE.
target_output.workflows: Weighted Bin-Pack Batch Launch: training-orchestrator CronJob triggers at 2am UTC. Reads sku_complexity_scores sorted set from Redis (keys sku_id, scores = 7-day rolling average inference time in ms from previous forecast_run metrics). Runs greedy decreasing bin-packing: sorts SKUs descending by complexity_score, iterates assigning each SKU to the worker pod with the current lowest total expected duration. Produces worker_assignments list of length worker_count (up to 50). Launches GKE batch-worker Job with worker_count pods; each pod receives its sku_id partition via ConfigMap entry. Pods execute in parallel, writing replenishment_recommendations rows on completion. training-orchestrator monitors Job completion and computes actual vs estimated duration per pod; updates sku_complexity_scores with latest inference times for next run. Planner Override and Approval: Planner reviews recommendation grid, spots an anomaly for a high-demand SKU. Clicks explanation tooltip; frontend calls GET /v1/recommendations/:id/explain; forecast-api routes to anomaly-explainer which constructs minimized prompt (sku_category='beverage', forecast_delta_pct=40, promotional_event_flag=true), calls GPT-4o, returns cached natural-language explanation. Planner submits override via PATCH /v1/recommendations/:id/override with override_units and justification_text. After reviewing batch summary statistics, planner calls POST /v1/forecast-runs/:id/approve; procurement API now returns approved recommendations for this run.
target_output.data_model: sku_catalog (sku_id UUID PK, category VARCHAR, description TEXT, unit_of_measure VARCHAR, active BOOL). warehouse_locations (location_id UUID PK, region VARCHAR, capacity_units INT). historical_sales (sku_id UUID, location_id UUID, sale_date DATE, units_sold INT, promo_flag BOOL) ENGINE=ReplicatedMergeTree PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(sale_date) ORDER BY (sku_id, location_id, sale_date). forecast_runs (id UUID PK, model_version_id UUID FK, run_date DATE, status ENUM pending/running/complete/failed, worker_count INT, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). replenishment_recommendations (id UUID, forecast_run_id UUID, sku_id UUID, location_id UUID, recommended_units INT, urgency_score REAL, planner_override_units INT, override_justification TEXT, approved BOOL, approved_at TIMESTAMPTZ) ENGINE=ReplicatedMergeTree ORDER BY (forecast_run_id, sku_id, location_id). MATERIALIZED VIEW mv_recommendation_summary AS SELECT warehouse_id, urgency_band, count(), avg(recommended_units) GROUP BY warehouse_id, urgency_band. promotional_events (id UUID PK, sku_id FK, location_id FK, event_type VARCHAR, lift_factor REAL, start_date DATE, end_date DATE). model_versions (id UUID PK, algorithm ENUM prophet/lgbm_ensemble, mape_score REAL, rmse_score REAL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, active BOOL). analytics_events (event_type, sku_id, location_id, planner_id, event_data JSONB, created_at) ClickHouse append-only.
target_output.api_design: GET /api/v1/recommendations?warehouse_id=&urgency_band=&sku_category= (planner dashboard, JWT planner role, reads mv_recommendation_summary, returns paginated recommendation objects with urgency scores). PATCH /api/v1/recommendations/:id/override (planner role, accepts override_units and justification_text, writes to replenishment_recommendations). GET /api/v1/recommendations/:id/explain (async, returns LLM explanation string or status='computing' if cache miss). POST /api/v1/forecast-runs/:id/approve (planner role, sets approved=true on all recommendations in run, gates procurement API). GET /v1/recommendations/:warehouse_id (system role JWT, returns approved recommendations for procurement integration, 200ms p99 SLA via Redis cache). GET /api/v1/model-versions (analyst role, lists model_versions with MAPE and RMSE scores). GET /api/v1/forecast-runs/:id/metrics (analyst role, batch run duration, worker skew stats, error count).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 RS256 JWT verified via python-jose JWKS cache in FastAPI dependency. Three role scopes: planner (override and approve access), analyst (read-only analytics), system (procurement API client credentials flow). ClickHouse warehouse_id row filtering applied from JWT claims for multi-tenant planners. LLM prompt data minimization: anomaly-explainer strips warehouse_id, distributor_name, raw units_sold; only sku_category_label, forecast_delta_pct rounded to nearest 5%, and promotional_event_flag bool included in GPT-4o prompt. Redis LLM explanation cache keyed by recommendation_id with 24-hour TTL. GCS model artifacts bucket IAM-gated to GKE service account. Procurement API rate limited 500 req/min per client_id in FastAPI middleware.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot cluster in us-central1 with Workload Identity for GCS and Key Vault access. forecast-api: Deployment with 3 replicas, HPA min 3 max 8 on CPU 60%, liveness probe GET /health. training-orchestrator: CronJob schedule 0 2 * * *, requests 2 CPU 4Gi memory for bin-packing compute. batch-worker: GKE Job with parallelism up to 50, each pod requests 4 CPU 8Gi for LightGBM training, completionMode=Indexed. anomaly-explainer: Deployment 2 replicas, CPU 0.5 each. Cloud Build CI: ruff lint, mypy strict, pytest 90% coverage, docker build, Artifact Registry push, GKE rolling update via kubectl apply. Separate Cloud Build trigger for model training validation: holdout MAPE check gate before promoting model_version.active=true. Environments: dev (reduced dataset 1000 SKUs), staging (full SKU catalog with synthetic sales data, used for 4-hour batch window validation), production. GKE Ingress with Google-managed TLS certificate.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scraping forecast-api /metrics, ClickHouse /metrics exporter, and custom batch metrics from training-orchestrator (batch_duration_seconds, worker_skew_ratio defined as max_worker_time/avg_worker_time, recommendations_generated_total). Grafana dashboards: model accuracy (MAPE and RMSE per model_version over time), batch run timeline (per-worker duration Gantt-style for skew visualization), API latency p50/p95/p99, LLM call success rate and median latency, ClickHouse query duration by query type. Custom Prometheus gauge feature_drift_score_by_category computed by drift-exporter sidecar using PSI against training distribution; Grafana alert fires when any category drift_score exceeds 0.15 for 3 consecutive days. worker_skew_ratio alert fires when ratio exceeds 1.4, triggering review of sku_complexity_score accuracy.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $4200/month at full 50,000-SKU scale: GKE Autopilot (batch workers average $1800/month over 30-day period given 4-hour nightly spike to 50 pods), Cloud Memorystore Redis ($280), ClickHouse operator cluster on GKE ($900 for 6 nodes), Cloud Build ($150), Artifact Registry and GCS ($120), Azure OpenAI LLM calls at estimated 500 anomaly explanations/day ($150), misc ($800). MVP at 500 SKUs approximately $1400/month with 5 batch worker pods and smaller ClickHouse cluster. Batch workers scale to 0 outside the 4-hour nightly window, eliminating idle compute cost.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP 8 weeks: historical_sales ClickHouse ingestion pipeline from retailer POS feeds, LightGBM baseline forecasting model for top-500 SKUs, replenishment_recommendations generation, planner dashboard with override capability, procurement REST API, Auth0 RBAC, GKE Autopilot deployment with Cloud Build CI. Phase 2 6 weeks: Prophet and LightGBM ensemble, promotional_events integration into feature engineering, batch worker scaling to 20 pods for full SKU catalog, weighted bin-packing partition assignment (ISS-003 fix), model versioning with MAPE gating, drift monitoring Prometheus exporter. Phase 3 4 weeks: LLM anomaly explanation feature with anomaly-explainer service and data minimization, seasonal_adjustments feature, analytics dashboard for planner interaction tracking, procurement API SLA optimization with Redis cache pre-warming.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python: ruff linter and mypy strict type checking in Cloud Build CI. pytest with 90% coverage gate; integration tests for ClickHouse queries using testcontainers-python. LightGBM and Prophet model tests use fixed random seeds for reproducible output. All anomaly-explainer prompt construction functions must have unit tests verifying that units_sold, warehouse_id, and distributor_name fields are absent from constructed prompt strings. Next.js: ESLint and TypeScript strict mode, Playwright E2E for recommendation dashboard override flow and batch approval workflow. Model training code changes require holdout MAPE validation in Cloud Build before merge.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: sku_complexity_scores become stale after promotional events cause atypical training times for affected SKUs. Mitigation: training-orchestrator detects worker_skew_ratio above 1.4 after each run and re-runs bin-packing with updated complexity scores for the following night. Risk: ClickHouse mv_recommendation_summary materialized view becomes stale during active forecast_run insertion. Mitigation: mv refresh scheduled after batch completion, not during; planner dashboard reads from mv for history views and live table for current run. Tradeoff: LightGBM and Prophet ensemble improves MAPE by approximately 8% over single model but doubles batch worker compute time per SKU; weighted bin-packing assignment mitigates the wall-clock impact. Tradeoff: Redis hot cache for procurement API reduces ClickHouse read load but means procurement systems can receive stale recommendations up to the cache TTL of 15 minutes after a planner override; documented in API contract.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Weighted bin-packing selected over consistent-hash partitioning for batch workers because complexity variance across SKU categories is significant enough (3x difference between high-velocity beverage SKUs and low-velocity specialty items) to cause window breaches with uniform assignment. LightGBM and Prophet ensemble selected over deep learning time-series models (TFT, N-BEATS) after pilot MAPE comparison showed minimal accuracy gain from transformer models on this dataset while adding substantial training time. ClickHouse selected over BigQuery for recommendations storage to avoid per-query cost model at scale; fixed-cost cluster is more predictable at 50,000-SKU query volumes.

metadata.notes: Round 3 demand forecasting AI system resolving batch worker partition skew via weighted bin-packing; ClickHouse analytics, LLM anomaly explanations with data minimization.

profile.domain: Warehouse Operations & WMS Monitoring

project_goal.value: Terminal-based CLI tool for warehouse supervisors to monitor live pick-and-pack queue depths, worker productivity rates, and conveyor throughput metrics pulled from a WMS via SSE streams, with color-coded dashboard display and webhook-triggered escalation alerts
project_goal.rationale: Supervisors need real-time floor visibility without navigating a browser-based WMS dashboard

target_users.value: Warehouse floor supervisors monitoring live operations from shift-office terminals
target_users.rationale: Single user persona with terminal access to shift-office workstations

project_class.rationale: Terminal-resident application with no web browser or server component; runs on operator workstation

capabilities.rationale: SSE stream consumer, WMS API integration, alert webhook integration; no frontend or auth module needed beyond API key

complexity_level.rationale: Single-binary CLI with SSE consumption, terminal rendering, and webhook dispatch; no database, no persistent state beyond session

risk_level.rationale: Display-only tool; does not write to WMS; worst case is missing an alert display, not a data corruption event

data_sensitivity.rationale: Warehouse operational metrics are internal business data; no PII or financial records

external_exposure.rationale: Runs on-premises on internal shift-office workstations; no external network exposure

access_model.value: WMS API key stored in local config file or WAREHOUSE_MONITOR_API_KEY environment variable; no user login required
access_model.rationale: Internal-only tool on managed workstations; API key authentication sufficient

feature_scope.value: Live pick queue depth display per zone, pack station productivity rates, conveyor throughput segments, configurable alert thresholds, webhook escalation on threshold breach, color-coded TUI with configurable refresh interval, graceful reconnect on SSE disconnect
feature_scope.rationale: Complete monitoring feature set for shift supervisor use cases

mvp_scope.value: Live pick queue depth and worker throughput display, single configurable threshold with webhook alert, color-coded terminal dashboard, graceful SSE reconnect
mvp_scope.rationale: Core visibility features sufficient for initial deployment on warehouse floor

security_baseline.value: API key authentication for WMS SSE endpoint, TLS for all HTTP connections, no credential logging, config file permissions restricted to owner
security_baseline.rationale: Minimal but appropriate controls for an internal display-only CLI tool

frontend_stack.rationale: Bubbletea provides Go-native terminal UI components with Lip Gloss styling; no browser frontend needed

backend_stack.rationale: Go single-binary distribution; Bubbletea Elm-architecture model for terminal event-driven updates; Lip Gloss for color-coded styling

data_platform.value: none (stateless SSE consumer; no local database)
data_platform.rationale: Tool is a real-time display; no persistence required; all data sourced live from WMS SSE stream

hosting_target.value: Self-hosted on bare-metal shift-office workstations (Linux amd64); distributed as a single compiled binary
hosting_target.rationale: No cloud hosting; binary deployed via internal Ansible playbook or scp to warehouse workstations

privacy_retention_policy.value: No data retained; tool is stateless display-only; no logs written to disk unless --debug flag is explicitly set
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Display-only tool with no persistence eliminates retention obligations

future_scope.value: Multi-warehouse zone switching, historical throughput sparklines using in-memory ring buffer, optional log-to-file mode for post-shift analysis
future_scope.rationale: Enhancements valuable for multi-site supervisors after initial deployment validation

constraints.value: Must run on Linux amd64 Go binary without Docker or runtime dependencies; terminal width minimum 80 columns; WMS SSE endpoint may have 30-second reconnect delays
constraints.rationale: Warehouse workstations are locked-down Linux machines; no container runtime installed

observability_baseline.value: none (no monitoring infrastructure for CLI tool); optional --debug flag writes structured JSON log lines to stderr for troubleshooting SSE connectivity issues
observability_baseline.rationale: Simple CLI tool does not warrant monitoring infrastructure; debug flag sufficient for support

execution_preference.value: Single iteration delivery; ship working binary to 3 pilot warehouses for 2-week field trial before broader rollout
execution_preference.rationale: Simple scope allows single-pass delivery with field trial validation

project.goal: Terminal TUI for real-time warehouse pick-and-pack monitoring via WMS SSE

backend.framework: Bubbletea for TUI, Lip Gloss for terminal styling, net/http for SSE client and webhook dispatch

security.auth: WMS API key in config file or WAREHOUSE_MONITOR_API_KEY env var
security.transport: TLS for WMS SSE and webhook HTTP calls

data.format: JSON event messages with pick_queue_depth, pack_station_rates, conveyor_throughput fields

devops.distribution: Single Go binary for Linux amd64

reasoner_reviews.product: The use case is clear and well-scoped: supervisors want a glanceable terminal dashboard without switching to a browser. The alert threshold triggering a webhook rather than an email or SMS keeps the integration surface minimal and testable. The --debug flag for SSE connectivity troubleshooting addresses the most likely field support scenario without adding operational complexity.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Bubbletea Elm-architecture with a goroutine-based SSE listener feeding messages through a channel into the Bubbletea message loop is the correct pattern for this tool. The SSE listener goroutine should own the reconnect logic independently of the TUI, so that a 30-second reconnect delay does not freeze the terminal display. A reconnecting_state field in the Bubbletea Model should trigger a visual indicator row in the dashboard without blocking the render loop.
reasoner_reviews.security: API key must not be echoed in --debug log output. The config file containing the API key should be created with 0600 permissions by the tool on first run. The webhook URL in config should accept a secret header parameter for webhook endpoint authentication to prevent spoofed alert callbacks.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Go single-binary cross-compilation for Linux amd64 from a macOS or Linux CI environment is straightforward with GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64. The Bubbletea and Lip Gloss dependencies are pure Go with no CGo requirements, so the binary has zero shared library dependencies on the warehouse workstations.

backend.service_design: Single Go binary (warehouse-monitor) with two concurrent components: Bubbletea TUI program running on the main goroutine, and SSE listener goroutine feeding parsed metric events into a buffered channel read by the Bubbletea Update function. Cobra CLI parses flags (--config CONFIG_FILE, --zone ZONE_ID, --threshold METRIC:VALUE, --webhook-url URL, --refresh SECONDS, --debug).
backend.api_patterns: Outbound only: WMS SSE consumer subscribing to GET /api/v1/metrics/stream?zone={zone_id} with Authorization: ApiKey header. Webhook dispatcher sending POST to configured webhook URL with JSON payload on threshold breach. No HTTP server or inbound connections.
backend.failure_handling: SSE listener implements exponential backoff reconnect starting at 5 seconds, capping at 30 seconds. During reconnect, TUI model transitions to reconnecting_state=true and renders a flashing 'WMS DISCONNECTED - reconnecting...' status row in the header. On reconnect success, reconnecting_state=false and normal metric display resumes. Webhook dispatch failures are logged to stderr (with --debug) and retried once after 5 seconds; no further retry to avoid blocking the SSE consumer goroutine.

security.auth_design: API key read from WAREHOUSE_MONITOR_API_KEY environment variable first, then from config_file.api_key field. API key never printed to stdout or included in --debug log lines; masked as '***' if debug logging includes config summary.
security.secrets_management: Config file created with os.WriteFile and 0600 permissions on first run. If config file has world-readable permissions (0644 or broader) on startup, tool prints warning to stderr and exits with non-zero code to prevent accidental credential exposure on shared workstations.

data.entities: In-memory only. Model struct fields: pick_queues (map[string]int keyed by zone_id), pack_stations (map[string]float64 keyed by station_id, units/hour), conveyor_segments (map[string]float64 keyed by segment_id, items/minute), alert_state (map[string]bool keyed by threshold rule ID), last_updated time.Time, reconnecting_state bool.
data.consistency_notes: All metric state held in Bubbletea Model struct, updated atomically via Bubbletea message processing (single-threaded update loop). SSE listener goroutine sends typed MetricUpdateMsg values through channel; no shared mutable state between goroutines.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions workflow: go vet, golangci-lint, go test ./... with unit tests for threshold evaluation and webhook payload construction, GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 go build -ldflags '-s -w' -o warehouse-monitor, upload artifact. Release workflow on tag: goreleaser producing Linux amd64 binary with SHA256 checksum file.
devops.deployment_topology: Ansible playbook copies warehouse-monitor binary to /usr/local/bin/warehouse-monitor on target workstations, sets executable permission, creates /etc/warehouse-monitor/config.yaml template with 0600 permissions, optionally installs systemd user service unit for auto-start at login.
devops.devops_risks: WMS SSE endpoint may enforce IP allowlisting; deployment process must document required workstation IP registration with WMS team. Binary versioning must be maintained to ensure all warehouse workstations run the same version; Ansible playbook should verify binary SHA256 checksum before installation.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a stateless Go single-binary terminal TUI for warehouse monitoring. The key architectural pattern is a Bubbletea Elm-architecture program with an independent SSE listener goroutine feeding typed MetricUpdateMsg values through a channel into the Bubbletea Update function. The reconnect logic lives entirely in the SSE goroutine, so a 30-second reconnect delay does not block terminal rendering. No database or persistence layer is needed; all state is held in the Bubbletea Model struct.
target_output.title: Warehouse Monitor CLI Tool Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A stateless Go 1.22 single-binary terminal TUI for warehouse supervisors to monitor live pick queue depths, pack station productivity rates, and conveyor throughput metrics streamed from a WMS via Server-Sent Events. Built with Bubbletea and Lip Gloss, the tool renders a color-coded dashboard that updates in real time, displays flashing alerts when configurable thresholds are breached, fires webhook escalation POSTs to configured endpoints, and gracefully reconnects to the WMS SSE stream with exponential backoff. Distributed as a Linux amd64 binary with zero shared library dependencies, deployed to warehouse workstations via Ansible playbook.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single Go binary with two concurrent execution paths: the Bubbletea TUI program running on the main goroutine managing terminal rendering and keyboard events, and an SSE listener goroutine reading the WMS metric stream and sending typed MetricUpdateMsg values through a buffered channel into the Bubbletea message loop. The Bubbletea Update function processes MetricUpdateMsg values, updates in-memory Model state (pick_queues, pack_stations, conveyor_segments), evaluates configured threshold rules, and triggers AlertMsg values when thresholds are breached. A webhook dispatcher goroutine handles alert HTTP POSTs independently to prevent webhook latency from affecting dashboard rendering. The SSE listener owns reconnect logic with exponential backoff from 5 to 30 seconds, setting reconnecting_state=true in the Model during disconnects to render a visual indicator. Cobra parses startup flags; config file provides API key and threshold rules.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22. TUI: Bubbletea v0.27 (Elm architecture event loop), Lip Gloss v0.10 (ANSI terminal styling for color-coded metric rows and alert indicators). CLI flags: Cobra v1.8. HTTP: net/http standard library for SSE streaming client and webhook POST dispatch (no third-party HTTP client needed). Build: GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 go build with -ldflags '-s -w' for binary size reduction. CI: GitHub Actions. Distribution: goreleaser for tagged release artifacts with SHA256 checksums.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Live Pick Queue Dashboard: Bubbletea View function renders a Lip Gloss table with one row per warehouse zone showing pick_queue_depth with color coding (green below 50, yellow 50-80, red above 80 threshold). Pack Station Productivity: table section showing units/hour per station_id with color band based on target_rate threshold. Conveyor Throughput: items/minute per conveyor_segment_id with red highlight when below minimum_flow threshold. Threshold Alert Row: flashing alert row using Lip Gloss blink style rendered when any threshold is in alert_state=true, with rule name and current value. Webhook Escalation: when alert transitions from false to true, dispatcher goroutine sends HTTP POST to webhook URL with JSON payload containing zone_id, metric_type, current_value, threshold_value, triggered_at. SSE Reconnect Indicator: header row displays 'WMS DISCONNECTED - reconnecting in Xs' with orange Lip Gloss style when reconnecting_state=true.
target_output.system_components: warehouse-monitor binary (Go 1.22, Linux amd64, ~8MB stripped). Cobra CLI: parses --config, --zone, --threshold (repeatable, format metric:value), --webhook-url, --refresh, --debug flags. Config loader: reads YAML config file from --config path or default ~/.warehouse-monitor/config.yaml, validates API key presence and config file permissions. SSE listener goroutine: net/http GET to WMS /api/v1/metrics/stream?zone=, reads event-stream lines, unmarshals JSON into MetricUpdateMsg, sends to msg channel, owns exponential backoff reconnect. Bubbletea program: Init/Update/View Elm functions; Update dispatches MetricUpdateMsg to update Model.pick_queues/pack_stations/conveyor_segments and evaluates threshold rules. Threshold evaluator: pure function checking current metric values against configured thresholds, returning AlertMsg slice. Webhook dispatcher goroutine: receives AlertMsg on alert channel, constructs JSON payload, sends POST with optional X-Webhook-Secret header, single retry on failure. Debug logger: writes JSON lines to stderr when --debug flag set, masking API key fields.
target_output.workflows: Startup and SSE Connection: operator runs 'warehouse-monitor --zone ZONE-A --threshold pick_queue:80 --webhook-url https://alerts.internal/wh/wms' from terminal. Cobra parses flags, config loader reads API key from WAREHOUSE_MONITOR_API_KEY env var, validates config file permissions are 0600, creates Bubbletea program and starts SSE listener goroutine. SSE listener opens net/http GET to WMS SSE endpoint with Authorization: ApiKey header and TLS. Bubbletea program renders initial dashboard frame with 'Connecting...' status. On first SSE event received, Model updates and full dashboard renders with live metric values. Threshold Breach and Alert Escalation: WMS SSE stream delivers event with pick_queue_depth=95 for ZONE-A exceeding the configured threshold of 80. SSE listener sends MetricUpdateMsg to Bubbletea channel. Bubbletea Update function processes msg, updates Model.pick_queues['ZONE-A']=95, calls threshold evaluator which returns AlertMsg for pick_queue rule. Update transitions Model.alert_state['pick_queue:ZONE-A']=true and sends AlertMsg to webhook channel. Bubbletea View renders ZONE-A row in red with flashing alert indicator. Webhook dispatcher goroutine receives AlertMsg, constructs JSON body {zone_id:'ZONE-A', metric:'pick_queue_depth', current:95, threshold:80, triggered_at:'2026-03-24T08:15:00Z'}, POSTs to configured URL with X-Webhook-Secret header. SSE Disconnect and Reconnect: WMS SSE connection drops. SSE listener detects EOF on response body, sends ReconnectMsg with wait_seconds=5. Bubbletea Update sets Model.reconnecting_state=true. View renders flashing orange 'WMS DISCONNECTED - reconnecting in 5s' header row. SSE listener sleeps 5s (doubling on each retry up to 30s), re-opens HTTP GET, sends ConnectedMsg on success. Update sets reconnecting_state=false, dashboard resumes normal rendering.
target_output.data_model: No persistent data model. In-memory Bubbletea Model struct: ZoneQueues map[string]int (zone_id to pick_queue_depth), StationRates map[string]float64 (station_id to units/hour), ConveyorThroughput map[string]float64 (segment_id to items/minute), AlertState map[string]bool (threshold_rule_id to breach status), Thresholds []ThresholdRule (loaded from flags and config), LastUpdated time.Time, ReconnectingState bool, ReconnectCountdown int. ThresholdRule struct: MetricType string, ZoneOrID string, ThresholdValue float64, WebhookURL string. MetricUpdateMsg struct (Bubbletea Msg): ZoneQueues map, StationRates map, ConveyorThroughput map, EventTimestamp time.Time. AlertMsg struct: RuleID string, MetricType string, CurrentValue float64, ThresholdValue float64, TriggeredAt time.Time.
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API exposed. Inbound: WMS SSE stream at GET /api/v1/metrics/stream?zone={zone_id}, event-stream content type, JSON data payloads with fields pick_queues (object), pack_stations (object), conveyor_segments (object), timestamp string. Authorization: ApiKey {api_key} header. Outbound: webhook POST to configured URL, Content-Type: application/json, optional X-Webhook-Secret: {secret} header, body: {zone_id, metric_type, current_value, threshold_value, triggered_at, tool_version}. Expected webhook response: 2xx for success; any non-2xx logged to stderr and retried once after 5s.
target_output.security_and_compliance: API key handling: read from WAREHOUSE_MONITOR_API_KEY env var first, then config file; never printed to stdout; masked as '***' in --debug log output. Config file permission check on startup: if permissions are 0644 or broader, tool logs error to stderr and exits with code 1. TLS: net/http client uses system CA bundle for TLS verification on both WMS SSE and webhook connections; InsecureSkipVerify is not configurable. Webhook security: optional secret_header_value in config file sent as X-Webhook-Secret header to authenticate webhook delivery. No network listening ports opened by the tool; all connections are outbound-only.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Build: GitHub Actions workflow runs go vet, golangci-lint, go test ./..., then GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 go build -ldflags '-s -w' -o warehouse-monitor. Artifact uploaded and tagged release triggers goreleaser producing warehouse-monitor-linux-amd64.tar.gz with SHA256 checksum file. Distribution: Ansible playbook copies binary to /usr/local/bin/warehouse-monitor on target workstations, verifies SHA256 checksum, sets 0755 permissions, creates /etc/warehouse-monitor/ directory with 0700 permissions, copies config.yaml template with 0600 permissions, optionally installs systemd user service unit for auto-start at supervisor login. Environments: dev laptop (run with --debug against WMS staging SSE endpoint), pilot (3 warehouse workstations running against production WMS). No container runtime required on workstations.
target_output.observability: No monitoring infrastructure for the tool itself. Operator-facing observability: --debug flag writes JSON log lines to stderr with fields timestamp, level, event (sse_connected, sse_disconnected, metric_update, threshold_breach, webhook_sent, webhook_failed, reconnect_attempt), masking api_key and webhook_secret fields. Debug log can be redirected to a file for post-shift support analysis: 'warehouse-monitor --debug 2>/tmp/monitor-debug.log'. Dashboard header row shows last_updated timestamp and SSE connection latency in milliseconds as passive observability indicators for supervisors.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud hosting cost; runs on existing warehouse workstation hardware. Development cost: estimated 80 person-hours for build and test. Go binary is approximately 8MB stripped; no external runtime dependencies. Scales to any number of warehouse workstations by copying the binary; each instance connects independently to the WMS SSE endpoint. WMS SSE endpoint load scales linearly with number of concurrent tool instances; WMS team should confirm SSE connection limits before broad rollout beyond 20 concurrent supervisors.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Build 2 weeks: SSE listener goroutine with exponential backoff reconnect, Bubbletea TUI with pick queue and pack station rows, Lip Gloss color-coded threshold styling, single threshold alert with webhook dispatch, Cobra CLI flags, config file loader with permission validation, GitHub Actions CI with unit tests for threshold evaluator and webhook payload builder, Linux amd64 binary build. Phase 2 Pilot 2 weeks: deploy to 3 pilot warehouses via Ansible playbook, collect supervisor feedback on dashboard layout and alert thresholds, fix any SSE connectivity or terminal rendering issues, validate webhook delivery to escalation endpoint. Phase 3 Rollout: deploy to remaining warehouses, add conveyor_segments display row, add --zone switching keyboard shortcut for multi-zone warehouses, optional systemd user service installation.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go: golangci-lint with errcheck, staticcheck, and gocritic. go test for all business logic: threshold evaluator (table-driven tests covering all metric types and boundary values), webhook payload construction (verify JSON structure and field presence), config file permission validation (test with temporary files at various permission levels). No CGo usage to preserve zero-dependency binary. Bubbletea Model updates must be pure functions for testability: threshold evaluator takes Model and MetricUpdateMsg and returns new Model plus AlertMsg slice with no side effects. SSE listener goroutine communicates exclusively through typed Bubbletea messages; no shared mutable state.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: WMS SSE endpoint enforces IP allowlisting that excludes shift-office workstation IPs. Mitigation: document IP registration requirement in deployment runbook; Ansible playbook validates SSE connectivity before marking installation complete. Risk: terminal width below 80 columns on some workstations causes Lip Gloss table rendering artifacts. Mitigation: tool detects terminal width on startup using term.GetSize() and renders a compact single-column layout below 80 columns. Tradeoff: stateless design with no disk persistence means supervisors cannot review historical threshold breach history from the tool; mitigated by webhook delivering alerts to a persistent escalation system. Tradeoff: single zone per tool instance simplifies the SSE subscription and state model; multi-zone support requires a future enhancement with multiple SSE goroutines and a zone-selector TUI component.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Bubbletea over termui or tview for TUI framework based on Elm-architecture goroutine safety and active maintenance; termui is no longer maintained. Cobra selected for flag parsing over standard library flag package to support repeatable --threshold flags cleanly. Webhook-based alert dispatch selected over direct PagerDuty or Slack SDK integration to keep the tool integration-agnostic; warehouse teams configure their preferred escalation endpoint.

metadata.notes: Stateless Go CLI warehouse monitor with Bubbletea TUI, WMS SSE consumer, exponential backoff reconnect, and webhook alert dispatch.

profile.domain: EDI Document Processing & Supply Chain Integration

project_goal.value: TypeScript SDK for parsing, validating, and transforming EDI 850 purchase orders and EDI 856 advance ship notices into structured JSON objects, with streaming support for large file batches intended for 3PLs and retailers handling thousands of EDI documents per hour
project_goal.rationale: 3PLs need a reliable typed SDK to build EDI integration layers without implementing X12 parsing from scratch

target_users.value: TypeScript/Node.js developers at 3PLs and retailers building EDI integration pipelines
target_users.rationale: Developer library audience; API ergonomics and TypeScript type safety are primary concerns

project_class.rationale: Published npm package with exported parsing, validation, and transformation API surface; no hosted platform or HTTP server

capabilities.rationale: Pure TypeScript parsing and transformation library; no frontend, auth, or hosting components

complexity_level.rationale: X12 EDI segment parsing with ISA/GS envelope handling and streaming support adds meaningful complexity; trading partner field map transforms add configuration depth

risk_level.rationale: Library risk is limited to API surface correctness and parsing accuracy; no hosted infrastructure, no data storage

data_sensitivity.rationale: Library processes EDI document data in-memory at integration layer; data sensitivity depends on caller context, not the SDK itself

external_exposure.rationale: Published to npm but intended for authenticated private npm registry or public npm; callers authenticate via npm token

access_model.value: npm package published to public npm registry under @edi-tools scope; no API key or auth within the SDK itself
access_model.rationale: Standard npm package distribution; access control at registry level

feature_scope.value: EDI 850 purchase order parsing, EDI 856 ASN parsing, ISA/GS envelope segmentation, X12 schema validation, trading partner field map transforms, streaming parser for large batches, typed TypeScript output interfaces, error collection with segment-level diagnostics
feature_scope.rationale: Complete EDI processing capability for 850 and 856 transaction sets

mvp_scope.value: EDI 850 parsing to typed PurchaseOrder interface, X12 ISA/GS envelope parsing, segment-level validation against hardcoded 850 schema, single trading partner transform map support
mvp_scope.rationale: 850 is highest priority transaction set for initial adopters; 856 and streaming follow in v1.1

security_baseline.value: Input size limit to prevent memory exhaustion on malformed EDI files; no eval or dynamic code execution in transform rules; dependency audit in CI
security_baseline.rationale: Library must be safe to embed in integration services processing untrusted EDI input from trading partners

frontend_stack.rationale: SDK is a backend TypeScript library with no browser rendering surface

backend_stack.rationale: Bun for development tooling (bun test, bun build, bun run); library outputs CJS and ESM bundles compatible with Node.js 20+ and Bun consumers

data_platform.value: none (in-memory processing only; no database dependency)
data_platform.rationale: SDK parses EDI documents in-memory and returns structured objects; no persistence layer

hosting_target.value: Published to npm registry under @edi-tools/x12 package name
hosting_target.rationale: Standard npm distribution; no hosted service

privacy_retention_policy.value: none (library processes data in-memory in caller's process; retention is caller's responsibility)
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SDK has no data storage; privacy obligations belong to integrating application

future_scope.value: EDI 810 invoice parsing, EDI 940 warehouse shipping order, GS1 SSCC barcode validation for ASN line items, async generator streaming API for large batch processing
future_scope.rationale: Expanding transaction set coverage after 850 and 856 validation

constraints.value: Must produce dual CJS and ESM bundles; no runtime dependencies beyond TypeScript standard library (zero external npm dependencies in published package); streaming API must handle 100MB EDI batch files without loading full file into memory
constraints.rationale: Zero-dependency constraint ensures no supply chain risk from transitive npm packages in integration services

observability_baseline.value: none (library; consumers instrument their own observability); SDK emits structured ParseResult objects with error arrays rather than console.log calls
observability_baseline.rationale: Library should not impose logging behavior on caller applications

execution_preference.value: Semantic versioning with changelog; v1.0.0 covers 850 and envelope parsing; v1.1.0 adds 856 and streaming; v1.2.0 adds transform pipeline
execution_preference.rationale: Incremental versioned releases allow adopters to upgrade on their own schedule

project.goal: Zero-dependency TypeScript SDK for X12 EDI 850/856 parsing and transformation
project.timeline: v1.0.0 in 3 weeks, v1.1.0 in 2 additional weeks

backend.runtime: Bun for dev tooling; output targets Node.js 20+ and Bun consumers
backend.build: bun build producing CJS (dist/index.cjs) and ESM (dist/index.mjs) bundles with TypeScript declaration files (.d.ts)

security.input_limits: Max 100MB per parse call; max 10MB for non-streaming parse
security.transform_rules: No eval; transform maps are plain object field mappings

data.format: X12 EDI 850 and 856 transaction sets; ISA/GS/ST/SE envelope segments
data.output: Typed PurchaseOrder and ShipNotice TypeScript interfaces

constraints.streaming: ReadableStream/Node.js stream for large batch files

confirmed_decisions.runtime: Bun for tooling, Node.js 20+ compatible output

reasoner_reviews.product: The zero-dependency constraint is the right call for an EDI library intended for enterprise 3PL integration services; any transitive npm dependency creates supply chain audit burden for the caller. The streaming async generator API for large batch files is the critical differentiator over simpler EDI parsers that load entire files into memory. The trading partner field map system should use a declarative JSON schema rather than code callbacks to keep transform rules auditable and storable.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The segment tokenizer is the performance-critical component: splitting a 100MB EDI file on the tilde delimiter character using a streaming char-by-char reader avoids loading the full buffer. An async generator function yielding parsed segment objects is the correct abstraction for both the streaming and non-streaming APIs, with the non-streaming parse() function simply collecting generator output into an array. Round 1 plan lacked explicit specification of the TypeScript declaration file export strategy; callers need types exported from a single package entry point.
reasoner_reviews.security: The input size limit of 10MB for synchronous parse() and 100MB for streaming parseStream() should be enforced as early exits before segment tokenization begins to prevent memory exhaustion from adversarially large EDI files. Transform map rules must be validated as plain record objects at runtime before application; object prototype chain traversal in field mapping must be guarded against prototype pollution.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero-dependency constraint must be enforced in CI: the GitHub Actions dependency-audit job should run 'bun pm ls --production' and fail if any dependency other than devDependencies appears. The dual CJS/ESM bundle from bun build requires explicit package.json exports field with conditions 'require' and 'import' pointing to the respective bundle files.

backend.service_design: Single TypeScript package with src/ directory containing: tokenizer.ts (segment-level char reader producing string arrays), envelope.ts (ISA/GS/ST/SE envelope parser), schema-850.ts and schema-856.ts (X12 transaction set schemas as TypeScript const objects), parser-850.ts and parser-856.ts (segment-array to typed PurchaseOrder/ShipNotice mapper), validator.ts (X12 mandatory/optional field validation against schema), transform.ts (trading partner field map application), stream-parser.ts (async generator wrapping tokenizer for streaming input). Public API exported from index.ts: parse(), parseStream(), validate(), transform(), createTransformMap().
backend.api_patterns: Functional API surface: parse(input: string | Buffer, options?: ParseOptions): ParseResult<PurchaseOrder | ShipNotice>. parseStream(input: ReadableStream | NodeReadable, options?: ParseOptions): AsyncGenerator<ParseResult<PurchaseOrder | ShipNotice>>. validate(doc: PurchaseOrder | ShipNotice, schema: TransactionSchema): ValidationResult. transform(doc: PurchaseOrder | ShipNotice, map: TradingPartnerMap): TransformedDocument. createTransformMap(config: TradingPartnerMapConfig): TradingPartnerMap.
backend.failure_handling: All parse errors collected into ParseResult.errors array rather than thrown exceptions, allowing callers to process partial results from malformed EDI batches. ParseResult.success boolean indicates whether the document parsed without errors. Validation errors include segment_id, element_position, error_code (per X12 999 acknowledgment error codes), and error_message. Size limit exceeded returns immediate ParseResult with single error rather than throwing.

security.secrets_management: No secrets in the SDK. Transform map config objects validated at createTransformMap() call time: check that all values are strings or plain record objects (not functions, RegExp, or class instances) to prevent prototype pollution attacks via malicious trading partner map objects.
security.abuse_prevention: parse() checks Buffer.byteLength(input) against MAX_SYNC_SIZE_BYTES (10MB default, configurable via options.maxSizeBytes) before tokenization; returns error ParseResult immediately if exceeded. parseStream() tracks cumulative bytes read and aborts generator iteration when MAX_STREAM_SIZE_BYTES (100MB default) is exceeded, yielding a final error ParseResult.

data.entities: PurchaseOrder TypeScript interface: {envelope: EdiEnvelope, transaction_set: string, purchase_order_number: string, po_date: string, buyer_party: PartyIdentifier, vendor_party: PartyIdentifier, ship_to: Address, line_items: PurchaseOrderLine[], total_amount?: number}. PurchaseOrderLine: {line_seq: number, item_id: string, item_id_qualifier: string, quantity: number, unit_of_measure: string, unit_price: number, description?: string}. ShipNotice interface: {envelope: EdiEnvelope, transaction_set: string, shipment_id: string, ship_date: string, carrier_code: string, tracking_number?: string, ship_from: Address, ship_to: Address, orders: ShipNoticeOrder[]}. ShipNoticeOrder: {purchase_order_number: string, lines: ShipNoticeLine[]}. ShipNoticeLine: {line_seq: number, sscc?: string, item_id: string, quantity_shipped: number, uom: string}. EdiEnvelope: {isa_control_number: string, gs_functional_group: string, sender_id: string, receiver_id: string, interchange_date: string}. TradingPartnerMap: {partner_id: string, field_mappings: Record<string, string>, value_mappings: Record<string, Record<string, string>>}.
data.schema_notes: X12 850 schema defined as a TypeScript const specifying mandatory segment IDs (BEG, DTM, N1, N3, N4, PO1, CTT) and their required element positions. Schema validation checks element count and mandatory element presence per segment ID. Schema is extensible via custom segment definitions passed in ParseOptions.customSegments.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions workflow on push to main and PR: bun install --frozen-lockfile, bun test with 95% coverage gate (coverage report uploaded as PR artifact), biome lint and format check, dependency audit ('bun pm ls --production' pipe to grep check for unexpected runtime deps), bun build producing dist/index.cjs and dist/index.mjs with TypeScript declarations. Release workflow on semver tag: npm publish with provenance attestation.
devops.deployment_topology: Published package: @edi-tools/x12 on npm public registry. package.json exports field: {'.' : {'require': './dist/index.cjs', 'import': './dist/index.mjs', 'types': './dist/index.d.ts'}}. Peer dependency none; devDependencies only (typescript, @types/node, biome, bun-types).
devops.devops_risks: Zero-dependency constraint may be violated by a developer adding a convenience package; CI dependency audit job is the enforcement gate. bun build ESM output must be tested for tree-shaking compatibility to ensure callers using bundlers like webpack do not pull in unused parsers for transaction sets they do not use.

ISS-001.title: TypeScript declaration file export strategy unspecified
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan did not specify how .d.ts files are generated or which package.json exports conditions map to them; callers in TypeScript projects will not get type resolution without explicit types condition in exports field

history.note: Identified by architect_reasoner in round 1 review

ISS-002.title: Prototype pollution risk in transform map validation
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 transform() function accepts plain object field maps without validating that values are strings or safe types; a caller passing a malicious trading partner map with __proto__ keys could pollute the prototype chain of transformed documents

history.note: Identified in security review

focus_issues.title: TypeScript declaration file export strategy unspecified
focus_issues.detail: package.json exports field must include types condition pointing to generated .d.ts; bun build tsc declarations step must be specified

focus_issues.title: Prototype pollution risk in transform map validation
focus_issues.detail: createTransformMap() must validate all map values are strings or safe plain objects; block __proto__, constructor, and prototype key names

Add createTransformMap() validation blocking __proto__, constructor, and prototype key names in field_mappings and value_mappings
