previous_plan.title: EDI X12 TypeScript SDK Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Zero-dependency TypeScript SDK for parsing and transforming EDI 850 and 856 documents. Bun tooling for development and testing; dual CJS/ESM bundle output. parse() and parseStream() async generator API for streaming large EDI batches.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Single TypeScript package with tokenizer, envelope parser, schema-850/856, parser-850/856, validator, and transform modules. Public API exported from index.ts. Functional parse(), parseStream(), validate(), transform(), createTransformMap() functions.
previous_plan.data_model: PurchaseOrder and ShipNotice TypeScript interfaces with EdiEnvelope, PartyIdentifier, Address, PurchaseOrderLine, ShipNoticeLine nested types. TradingPartnerMap interface for field and value mapping configuration.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Input size limits enforced before tokenization. Zero runtime npm dependencies enforced by CI audit. Transform map validation was identified as incomplete in audit.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 addresses ISS-001 by specifying the complete TypeScript declaration file generation process: tsc --emitDeclarationOnly generates dist/index.d.ts, and package.json exports field adds the types condition. ISS-002 is resolved by adding explicit key sanitization in createTransformMap() blocking __proto__, constructor, and prototype key names. Both fixes are targeted changes to the build configuration and transform module without affecting the parsing architecture.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit TypeScript declaration file generation to build pipeline: bun build produces JS bundles; separate tsc --emitDeclarationOnly --declaration --outDir dist step generates dist/index.d.ts and dist/index.d.mts. package.json exports field now includes 'types' condition: {'types': './dist/index.d.ts', 'require': './dist/index.cjs', 'import': './dist/index.mjs'}. Also added 'typesVersions' fallback for older TypeScript compiler versions.
fix_report.expected_outcome: TypeScript callers get full type inference for PurchaseOrder, ShipNotice, ParseResult, TradingPartnerMap, and all nested interfaces. ISS-001 resolved.

fix_report.action_taken: Added prototype pollution guard in createTransformMap(): iterates all keys in field_mappings and value_mappings (including nested value map objects), throws TypeError if any key string matches __proto__, constructor, or prototype. Guard applied before any object property assignments. Additionally, transform() constructs output using Object.create(null) as base object to ensure no prototype chain on transformed document output.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Malicious trading partner map configs with prototype-polluting keys are rejected at createTransformMap() call time with a descriptive TypeError before any document transformation executes. ISS-002 resolved.

target_output.title: EDI X12 TypeScript SDK Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A zero-dependency TypeScript SDK published to npm as @edi-tools/x12 for parsing EDI 850 purchase orders and EDI 856 advance ship notices into strongly typed JSON objects, with an async generator streaming API for large batch files. Built with Bun tooling for development and testing, producing dual CJS and ESM bundles with generated TypeScript declaration files for full caller type inference. Round 2 resolves missing .d.ts export strategy (ISS-001) and adds prototype pollution guard in the trading partner transform map validation (ISS-002).
target_output.architecture_overview: Single TypeScript package with a functional module composition: a char-by-char segment tokenizer feeds a segment array into transaction-set-specific parsers (parser-850.ts, parser-856.ts) that map X12 segments to typed PurchaseOrder and ShipNotice objects. A streaming async generator in stream-parser.ts wraps the tokenizer for large file consumption without full-buffer loading. The transform pipeline applies TradingPartnerMap field and value mappings to produce canonical internal document representations. All modules export pure functions with no side effects; no global state. package.json exports field provides types, require, and import conditions for full TypeScript and bundler compatibility. Zero runtime npm dependencies enforced by CI production dependency audit.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: TypeScript 5.4 with strict mode. Runtime targets: Node.js 20+ and Bun 1.x (CJS and ESM compatible output). Dev tooling: Bun (bun test, bun build, bun run). TypeScript compiler: tsc for --emitDeclarationOnly declaration generation. Linter/formatter: Biome. Build output: dist/index.cjs (CommonJS), dist/index.mjs (ESM), dist/index.d.ts (TypeScript declarations). Published as @edi-tools/x12 on npm public registry. CI: GitHub Actions with bun test, biome, tsc type check, production dependency audit, bun build, and npm publish on semver tag.
target_output.functional_feature_map: parse(input, options): synchronous parse of EDI string or Buffer up to 10MB limit; returns ParseResult containing PurchaseOrder or ShipNotice with errors array. parseStream(input, options): async generator yielding ParseResult objects from ReadableStream or Node.js Readable for EDI files up to 100MB; yields one result per EDI transaction set (ST/SE envelope). validate(doc, schema): validates a parsed document against an X12 transaction set schema, returning ValidationResult with per-segment error array using X12 999 acknowledgment error codes. transform(doc, map): applies TradingPartnerMap field_mappings and value_mappings to produce a TransformedDocument with canonical field names. createTransformMap(config): validates and compiles a TradingPartnerMapConfig into a sealed TradingPartnerMap, blocking prototype-polluting key names. Schema registry: built-in schema-850 and schema-856 exported as constants; consumer-extensible via options.customSegments.
target_output.system_components: tokenizer.ts: char-by-char segment reader splitting on ISA-detected element separator and segment terminator characters; yields string[] segments without loading full file buffer. envelope.ts: parses ISA (interchange), GS (functional group), ST (transaction set), and SE/GE/IEA (trailer) segments into EdiEnvelope object. schema-850.ts: X12 850 segment schema as TypeScript const (mandatory segments, element counts, mandatory element positions). schema-856.ts: X12 856 ASN schema. parser-850.ts: maps BEG, DTM, N1, N3, N4, PO1, PID, CTT segment arrays to PurchaseOrder interface. parser-856.ts: maps BSN, DTM, HL, LIN, SN1, PRF, REF segment arrays to ShipNotice interface. validator.ts: pure function taking segment array and TransactionSchema returning ValidationResult. transform.ts: applies TradingPartnerMap to document; uses Object.create(null) base for output to prevent prototype chain on result. createTransformMap(): validates config, blocks __proto__/constructor/prototype keys, returns sealed TradingPartnerMap. stream-parser.ts: async generator wrapping tokenizer for ReadableStream and Node.js Readable inputs with byte-count tracking for size limit enforcement. index.ts: re-exports all public API functions and TypeScript interfaces.
target_output.workflows: Synchronous Parse of EDI 850: caller invokes parse(ediBuffer, {transactionSet: '850'}). Function checks byteLength against 10MB limit; returns error ParseResult if exceeded. Tokenizer splits buffer on detected segment terminator (default tilde). envelope.ts extracts ISA/GS/ST segments into EdiEnvelope. parser-850.ts iterates segment array matching BEG segment for po_date and purchase_order_number, N1 loops for buyer_party and vendor_party, PO1 loops for line_items. validator.ts checks mandatory segment presence and element counts against schema-850 const. Returns ParseResult<PurchaseOrder> with errors array (empty on clean parse). Trading Partner Transform: caller constructs config {partner_id: 'ACME', field_mappings: {item_id: 'vendor_part_number'}, value_mappings: {unit_of_measure: {EA: 'EACH', CS: 'CASE'}}}. createTransformMap(config) iterates all keys in field_mappings and value_mappings entries, throws TypeError if key === '__proto__' or === 'constructor' or === 'prototype'. Returns sealed TradingPartnerMap. Caller invokes transform(purchaseOrder, map); transform() creates output = Object.create(null), copies all PurchaseOrder fields, then applies field_mappings renaming and value_mappings substitution. Returns TransformedDocument with renamed fields. Streaming Batch Parse of EDI 856: caller opens Node.js fs.createReadStream('shipments.edi') and passes to parseStream(stream, {transactionSet: '856'}). Async generator tokenizes chunks as they arrive, accumulating segments until SE trailer detected, then yields ParseResult<ShipNotice>. Caller iterates: for await (const result of parseStream(stream)) { if (result.success) processShipment(result.document); else logErrors(result.errors); }.
target_output.data_model: PurchaseOrder interface: {envelope: EdiEnvelope, transaction_set: '850', purchase_order_number: string, po_type_code: string, po_date: string, buyer_party: PartyIdentifier, vendor_party: PartyIdentifier, ship_to: Address, bill_to?: Address, line_items: PurchaseOrderLine[], total_line_items: number, total_amount?: number}. PurchaseOrderLine: {line_seq: number, item_id: string, item_id_qualifier: string, quantity: number, unit_of_measure: string, unit_price: number, description?: string, product_service_id?: string}. ShipNotice interface: {envelope: EdiEnvelope, transaction_set: '856', shipment_id: string, ship_date: string, carrier_code: string, carrier_routing?: string, tracking_number?: string, ship_from: Address, ship_to: Address, orders: ShipNoticeOrder[]}. ShipNoticeOrder: {purchase_order_number: string, lines: ShipNoticeLine[]}. ShipNoticeLine: {line_seq: number, sscc?: string, item_id: string, item_id_qualifier: string, quantity_shipped: number, uom: string}. EdiEnvelope: {isa_control_number: string, gs_functional_group: string, sender_id: string, receiver_id: string, interchange_date: string, interchange_time: string}. ParseResult<T>: {success: boolean, document?: T, errors: ParseError[], raw_segment_count: number}. ParseError: {segment_id: string, segment_position: number, element_position?: number, error_code: string, error_message: string}. TradingPartnerMap: {partner_id: string, field_mappings: Readonly<Record<string, string>>, value_mappings: Readonly<Record<string, Record<string, string>>>}. TransactionSchema: {transaction_set: string, mandatory_segments: string[], segment_specs: Record<string, SegmentSpec>}.
target_output.api_design: Public API exported from @edi-tools/x12 package entry point. parse(input: string | Buffer, options?: ParseOptions): ParseResult<PurchaseOrder | ShipNotice> -- synchronous, up to 10MB, auto-detects transaction set from ST02 element unless options.transactionSet specified. parseStream(input: ReadableStream | import('stream').Readable, options?: ParseOptions): AsyncGenerator<ParseResult<PurchaseOrder | ShipNotice>> -- async generator, up to 100MB default, yields one result per ST-SE transaction set envelope. validate(document: PurchaseOrder | ShipNotice, schema?: TransactionSchema): ValidationResult -- validates against built-in schema (auto-selected by transaction_set field) or provided custom schema; ValidationResult: {valid: boolean, errors: ValidationError[]}. transform(document: PurchaseOrder | ShipNotice, map: TradingPartnerMap): TransformedDocument -- returns plain object (Object.create(null) base) with remapped field names and substituted values. createTransformMap(config: TradingPartnerMapConfig): TradingPartnerMap -- validates config, blocks prototype-polluting keys, returns Object.freeze() sealed map. Exported schemas: EDI_850_SCHEMA, EDI_856_SCHEMA as TransactionSchema constants for direct use or extension. Exported types: PurchaseOrder, ShipNotice, PurchaseOrderLine, ShipNoticeLine, EdiEnvelope, ParseResult, ParseError, ValidationResult, TradingPartnerMap, TradingPartnerMapConfig.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Input size limits: parse() throws if byteLength > maxSizeBytes (default 10MB); parseStream() tracks cumulative bytes and yields error ParseResult then stops iteration at maxStreamSizeBytes (default 100MB). Prototype pollution guard in createTransformMap(): iterates all key strings in field_mappings and value_mappings recursively; throws TypeError('Dangerous key name in TradingPartnerMap config') if key matches __proto__, constructor, or prototype. transform() uses Object.create(null) as base for output object to ensure no inherited prototype methods on result. Zero runtime npm dependencies enforced by CI job running bun pm ls --production and failing on unexpected packages; eliminates supply chain attack surface from transitive dependencies. No eval, new Function, or dynamic code execution in any transform rule application. npm package published with provenance attestation via GitHub Actions for supply chain transparency.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Build pipeline: bun build --entrypoints src/index.ts --outdir dist --format cjs produces dist/index.cjs; bun build --entrypoints src/index.ts --outdir dist --format esm produces dist/index.mjs; tsc --emitDeclarationOnly --declaration --declarationMap --outDir dist generates dist/index.d.ts and dist/index.d.mts. package.json exports: {'.': {'types': './dist/index.d.ts', 'require': './dist/index.cjs', 'import': './dist/index.mjs'}}, typesVersions: {'*': {'*': ['./dist/index.d.ts']}} for TypeScript <4.7 fallback. GitHub Actions CI: install (bun install --frozen-lockfile), lint (bun run biome check), type-check (tsc --noEmit), test (bun test --coverage with 95% gate), dep-audit (bun pm ls --production grep check), build (bun build steps plus tsc declaration step), publish (npm publish --provenance on semver tag). Package files array in package.json limits published artifact to dist/ directory only; src/ excluded from npm tarball.
target_output.observability: No runtime observability instrumentation within the SDK; caller's application is responsible for logging and tracing. SDK design for caller observability: ParseResult.errors array with X12 999-standard error codes enables structured error categorization by callers. ParseResult.raw_segment_count field provides parsing throughput metric to callers. parseStream() generator yields per-transaction-set results enabling caller to instrument per-document processing latency. No console.log or process.stderr calls in production SDK code paths; all errors are return-value-based. The --debug equivalent for SDK consumers is to inspect ParseResult.errors after each parse call.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost; npm package with no runtime infrastructure. Development cost: estimated 120 person-hours for v1.0.0 covering 850 parser, streaming tokenizer, validation, and transform pipeline. v1.1.0 adds 856 parser for an additional 40 hours. The streaming async generator design enables callers to process EDI files of arbitrary size without memory constraints proportional to file size; a 100MB EDI batch containing 50,000 transaction sets can be processed with memory usage bounded by a single transaction set buffer at any point in time.
target_output.phased_implementation: v1.0.0 3 weeks: tokenizer with ISA separator detection, envelope parser, schema-850 const, parser-850 mapping BEG/DTM/N1/N3/N4/PO1/CTT segments to PurchaseOrder, validator with X12 error codes, createTransformMap() with prototype pollution guard, transform() with Object.create(null) output, CJS and ESM bundle build with tsc declaration generation, package.json exports with types condition, GitHub Actions CI with 95% coverage gate and dependency audit, npm publish workflow with provenance. v1.1.0 2 additional weeks: schema-856 const, parser-856 mapping BSN/DTM/HL/LIN/SN1 to ShipNotice, parseStream() async generator with ReadableStream and Node.js Readable support, streaming size limit enforcement, additional unit tests for streaming teardown on size breach. v1.2.0 future: EDI 810 invoice parser, GS1 SSCC validation for ASN line items, async generator streaming API performance benchmarks.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode (strict: true, noUncheckedIndexedAccess: true, exactOptionalPropertyTypes: true) in tsconfig.json. Biome for linting and formatting with no-explicit-any rule enforced. bun test with 95% coverage gate; tests for: tokenizer segment splitting on all valid EDI separator characters, envelope parsing with ISA/GS/ST/SE round-trip, parser-850 mapping of all mandatory segment types with table-driven test cases using real X12 850 sample documents, validator error code assignment for missing mandatory elements, createTransformMap() prototype key guard throwing on __proto__/constructor/prototype, transform() output object prototype chain verification (Object.getPrototypeOf(result) === null). Every exported function must have JSDoc comments describing parameters, return type, and thrown errors. Zero console.log in src/; biome no-console rule enforced. CHANGELOG.md updated for every version tag.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ISA separator character detection assumes standard tilde delimiter; EDI files using non-standard separators (asterisk element separator is standard but segment terminator varies) may be misparse. Mitigation: tokenizer reads ISA segment first 3 bytes to detect element separator and segment terminator characters before beginning full parse; documents this behavior in API reference with override option options.segmentTerminator. Risk: callers using bundlers may pull in all transaction set parsers even when only using parse() for 850 documents. Mitigation: export separate entry points options in v1.2.0 (dist/850.mjs, dist/856.mjs) for tree-shaking; note in documentation that the combined entry point includes all parsers. Tradeoff: pure functional API with no class instances simplifies testing and tree-shaking but means callers cannot subclass or override parsing behavior via inheritance; extension point is options.customSegments callback which covers the primary customization need without class inheritance.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-001 resolved: tsc --emitDeclarationOnly declaration generation step added to build pipeline; package.json exports types condition points to dist/index.d.ts; typesVersions fallback added for TypeScript versions before 4.7 conditional exports support. ISS-002 resolved: createTransformMap() validates all field_mappings and value_mappings keys against a blocklist of __proto__, constructor, and prototype before any property assignment; transform() uses Object.create(null) base to eliminate prototype chain on output. Bun build selected over tsup or rollup for bundle generation given the project's Bun-first tooling philosophy; output validated for Node.js 20+ CJS and ESM compatibility in CI.

metadata.notes: Round 2 EDI X12 TypeScript SDK resolving declaration file export strategy and prototype pollution guard in transform map validation.

profile.domain: High-Throughput Parcel Carrier Event Streaming Infrastructure

project_goal.value: Design and provision a high-throughput Kafka event streaming infrastructure ingesting barcode scan events from thousands of sortation hubs at over 500,000 events per second with sub-200ms end-to-end latency
project_goal.rationale: Core operational requirement for global parcel carrier visibility

target_users.value: Internal engineering teams and Kafka Streams applications consuming scan events for parcel tracking and analytics
target_users.rationale: No external consumers; all downstream systems are internal services

project_class.rationale: Platform/environment architecture using Terraform, MSK, and Kafka Streams; no application UI

capabilities.rationale: Pure infrastructure and stream processing; no frontend or auth surfaces

complexity_level.rationale: 500K events/second sustained throughput with sub-200ms latency across geo-distributed hubs

risk_level.rationale: Core carrier operational dependency; outage directly impacts parcel tracking SLAs

data_sensitivity.rationale: Scan events contain internal operational data without PII or financial data

external_exposure.rationale: MSK cluster is VPC-private; no public endpoint exposure

access_model.value: mTLS mutual authentication for all producer and consumer connections; IAM-based MSK access policies
access_model.rationale: Zero-trust networking between hub scan devices and MSK brokers

feature_scope.value: MSK cluster provisioning, topic partitioning strategy, Kafka Streams enrichment application, ClickHouse sink, Redis tracking cache, dead-letter queue handling, Terraform automation
feature_scope.rationale: Full infrastructure stack for scan event pipeline

mvp_scope.value: MSK cluster with 3 brokers, scan-events topic with 256 partitions, basic Kafka Streams enrichment job writing to ClickHouse, Terraform modules for provisioning
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for initial hub rollout covering 200 sortation sites

security_baseline.value: mTLS for all broker connections, MSK encryption at rest, VPC-private cluster, IAM MSK policies, no public broker endpoints
security_baseline.rationale: Operational data must not traverse public networks

frontend_stack.value: none; infrastructure project with no UI
frontend_stack.rationale: No user-facing frontend; Kafka tooling accessed via CLI and Datadog dashboards

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Kafka Streams for stream processing applications
backend_stack.rationale: Kafka Streams on Java provides native Kafka integration and stateful processing without external runtime

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for analytics hot data; Redis 7 for low-latency tracking API reads
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse handles columnar scan-event analytics; Redis serves sub-10ms parcel state reads

hosting_target.value: AWS MSK (Amazon Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka) in us-east-1 with multi-AZ brokers
hosting_target.rationale: Managed Kafka removes broker operations burden; multi-AZ ensures HA

privacy_retention_policy.value: Scan events retained on Kafka for 7 days; ClickHouse hot data 90 days; cold archival to S3 via Iceberg after 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Operational audit trail requirements; regulatory customs data retention

future_scope.value: Expand to 1M events/second by adding broker nodes; add Flink-based complex event processing for SLA breach prediction
future_scope.rationale: Next phase carrier network expansion to European hubs

constraints.value: Budget cap $45,000/month AWS spend; must use MSK not self-managed Kafka; Terraform-only infrastructure changes
constraints.rationale: Managed Kafka operational requirement; IaC mandate for audit compliance

observability_baseline.value: Datadog with Kafka consumer lag dashboards, broker throughput metrics, Kafka Streams processing latency histograms, PagerDuty for consumer lag exceeding 5-second threshold
observability_baseline.rationale: Real-time consumer lag is the primary SLA indicator for this pipeline

execution_preference.value: Infrastructure-as-code first with Terraform; all cluster changes via PR-gated Buildkite pipelines
execution_preference.rationale: Audit compliance requires all MSK configuration changes to be code-reviewed

compliance_context.value: Internal audit trail for infrastructure changes; SOC 2 Type II controls for infrastructure provisioning pipelines
compliance_context.rationale: Risk level high triggers compliance_context requirement; carrier operational systems under SOC 2 scope

project.goal: Provision and tune Kafka streaming infrastructure handling 500K parcel scan events per second with sub-200ms latency
project.target_users: Internal Kafka Streams consumers, analytics teams, tracking API

backend.stream_processing: stateful enrichment topology with RocksDB state stores

security.auth: mTLS for MSK connections
security.encryption: MSK encryption at rest and in transit
security.network: VPC-private MSK cluster, no public endpoints

open_questions.dlq_strategy: Dead-letter queue retention and reprocessing workflow under review

reasoner_reviews.product: The 500K events/second target at sub-200ms end-to-end is a genuine high-scale constraint that drives partitioning depth. The 256-partition scan-events topic keyed by tracking number ensures ordering per parcel while distributing producer load across hubs. The dead-letter queue design for malformed scan events is critical to prevent a single bad hub from blocking the main consumer topology.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Kafka Streams is the right choice here over Flink for the enrichment layer given the team owns Java and the stateful topology is bounded to hub metadata lookups. The RocksDB state store for hub metadata must be pre-warmed on startup to avoid cold-start enrichment delays. The ClickHouse sink should use the Kafka Connect ClickHouse connector with batching to avoid per-message insert overhead. The previous plan lacked explicit broker count justification and partition rebalance strategy during hub onboarding.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS is correctly mandated for all producer-broker and consumer-broker connections. The ACL strategy must assign per-service-account topic-level PRODUCE and CONSUME permissions rather than blanket MSK IAM policies. The prior plan did not address certificate rotation automation; ACM Private CA with 90-day cert lifetimes and automated renewal via Terraform must be specified.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: At $45,000/month budget, a 9-broker MSK cluster with db.kafka.12xlarge instances provides the compute headroom for 500K eps. The Buildkite pipeline gate for Terraform plan approval prevents accidental partition count changes that cannot be reversed without topic recreation.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The round 1 plan was solid on topology but thin on the dead-letter queue consumer architecture and the ClickHouse ingestion batching strategy. The partition rebalance during rolling broker upgrades was not addressed. This revision must close those gaps.

backend.service_design: Kafka Streams topology with two processing layers: ScanEnrichmentProcessor reads from scan-events topic, performs point-lookup enrichment against hub-metadata KTable materialized from hub-registry topic, and forwards to enriched-scan-events topic. DeliveryMilestoneProcessor reads enriched-scan-events, maintains parcel state in RocksDB, and emits delivery milestone events to milestone-events topic when terminal scan codes are detected.
backend.api_patterns: No REST API surface; integration via Kafka topic subscription. Downstream tracking API reads from Redis cache populated by a separate Redis-sink consumer. AdminClient API used internally for partition assignment inspection and consumer group offset management.
backend.failure_handling: Dead-letter queue topic scan-events-dlq receives malformed or undeserializable scan events with error metadata headers. A separate DLQ consumer replays corrected events after manual review. Kafka Streams uncaught exception handler configured to REPLACE_THREAD on transient errors and SHUTDOWN_CLIENT on deserialization failures.
backend.scaling_notes: Horizontal scale by increasing Kafka Streams instance count up to partition count (256 max parallelism for scan-events). JVM heap tuned to 8GB per instance with G1GC for predictable pause times. RocksDB block cache sized at 2GB per instance to keep hub-metadata KTable hot.

security.auth_design: ACM Private CA issues 90-day client certificates for each producer service account (one per hub scan system) and each consumer service account. Certificates rotated automatically via Terraform aws_acm_certificate resources and distributed via Secrets Manager.
security.authorization_model: MSK ACL: hub-scan-producer service accounts hold PRODUCE on scan-events topic only. kafka-streams-enrichment holds CONSUME on scan-events and hub-registry, PRODUCE on enriched-scan-events. clickhouse-sink-consumer holds CONSUME on enriched-scan-events. Least-privilege per service account.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: MSK broker logs forwarded to CloudWatch Logs. Kafka Streams application logs in JSON format with tracking_number and hub_id correlation fields. Datadog log index for audit trail queries. All Terraform plan outputs stored in Buildkite artifact store for 365-day retention.
security.security_risks: Certificate sprawl risk as hub count grows past 2000 sites; mitigated by automating certificate issuance via hub onboarding Terraform module.

data.entities: scan_events (tracking_number VARCHAR PK shard key, hub_id, scan_code ENUM inbound/outbound/exception/delivery, scan_ts TIMESTAMP, device_id, raw_payload JSONB stored in ClickHouse). hub_metadata (hub_id PK, hub_name, region, timezone, carrier_lane_ids[] stored in hub-registry Kafka topic as KTable source). delivery_milestones (tracking_number, milestone_type, hub_id, milestone_ts stored in ClickHouse milestone_events table). dead_letter_records (event_id, original_topic, error_reason, raw_bytes, received_at stored in PostgreSQL for review workflow).
data.storage_design: ClickHouse 3-shard 2-replica cluster on EC2 r6i.4xlarge. scan_events table uses ReplicatedMergeTree partitioned by toYYYYMM(scan_ts). Redis 7 cluster stores parcel_state hash keyed by tracking_number with 24-hour TTL. S3 Iceberg tables for scan_events beyond 90 days via ClickHouse S3 export job.
data.retention_and_deletion: Kafka scan-events topic retention 7 days (168 hours). ClickHouse scan_events partition TTL 90 days with DROP policy. S3 Iceberg cold tier indefinite retention. Redis TTL 24 hours per parcel_state key.

devops.deployment_topology: MSK cluster: 9 brokers across 3 AZs (3 per AZ), kafka.m5.4xlarge instance type. 256 partitions on scan-events topic, replication factor 3. Kafka Streams application deployed as ECS Fargate service with 16 tasks (256 partitions / 16 = 16 partitions per task). ClickHouse cluster on EC2 r6i.4xlarge in 2 AZs. Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster mode with 3 shards.
devops.ci_cd_design: Buildkite pipeline: (1) terraform validate and tflint on PR, (2) terraform plan with output diff posted as PR comment, (3) manual approval gate for production plan, (4) terraform apply on merge to main, (5) automated performance regression test verifying p99 consumer lag stays below 200ms under 500K eps load for 10 minutes post-apply.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog MSK integration: consumer_lag_sum per consumer-group/topic/partition, bytes_in_per_sec per broker, request_handler_avg_idle_percent. Custom metrics: kafka_streams_process_rate, kafka_streams_punctuate_latency_p99, enrichment_cache_miss_rate. PagerDuty: consumer lag sum > 50K events for 60s triggers P1 alert.
devops.rollback_strategy: Kafka partition count is immutable; rollback of partition changes requires topic recreation with consumer group offset migration. Terraform state locked during apply to prevent concurrent modifications. Kafka Streams applications rolled back via ECS service previous task-definition revision.

previous_plan.title: Parcel Carrier Kafka Streaming Infrastructure v1

target_output.title: Parcel Carrier Kafka Streaming Infrastructure v2
target_output.executive_summary: A high-throughput Kafka event streaming infrastructure on AWS MSK designed to ingest over 500,000 barcode scan events per second from thousands of global sortation hubs with sub-200ms end-to-end latency. The platform uses a 9-broker multi-AZ MSK cluster with 256 partitions on the scan-events topic, Java 21 Kafka Streams for real-time enrichment with hub metadata, a Kafka Connect ClickHouse connector for batched analytics ingestion, and Redis for low-latency tracking API reads. All producer and consumer connections use mTLS with ACM Private CA-managed certificate rotation, and all infrastructure is provisioned via Terraform with Buildkite PR-gated pipelines.
target_output.architecture_overview: Hub scan devices at each sortation facility publish barcode scan events to the MSK scan-events topic (256 partitions, keyed by tracking_number) over mTLS-authenticated connections. A Java 21 Kafka Streams application running on ECS Fargate enriches each scan event with hub metadata from a KTable materialized from the hub-registry topic, producing enriched events to the enriched-scan-events topic. A Kafka Connect cluster runs the ClickHouse Sink connector (batch.size=10000, linger.ms=500) to load enriched events into ClickHouse for analytics. A separate Redis Sink consumer materializes current parcel state into Redis 7 for sub-10ms tracking API reads. Malformed events are routed to scan-events-dlq with error headers; a DLQ consumer application exposes a review interface and supports replaying corrected events. Infrastructure is defined entirely in Terraform modules targeting MSK, ECS, ElastiCache, and EC2 ClickHouse nodes, applied via Buildkite with mandatory plan approval.
target_output.technology_stack: Stream broker: AWS MSK Apache Kafka 3.7, 9 brokers kafka.m5.4xlarge, 256 partitions. Stream processor: Java 21 Kafka Streams 3.7 with RocksDB state stores on ECS Fargate. Sink connectors: Kafka Connect 3.7 with ClickHouse Kafka Connect plugin and Redis Sink connector. Analytics DB: ClickHouse 24 on EC2 r6i.4xlarge (3-shard 2-replica). Tracking cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster mode 3 shards. Security: ACM Private CA for mTLS certificates, AWS Secrets Manager for certificate distribution. IaC: Terraform 1.8 with terragrunt. CI/CD: Buildkite. Observability: Datadog with MSK and Kafka Streams integrations.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Scan Event Ingestion: hub devices produce scan events partitioned by tracking_number ensuring ordered per-parcel processing across 256 partitions. Hub Metadata Enrichment: Kafka Streams KTable join adds hub name, region, timezone, and carrier lane assignments to each scan event. Delivery Milestone Detection: stateful Kafka Streams processor emits milestone events when terminal scan codes (DELIVERED, EXCEPTION, RETURNED) are detected. ClickHouse Analytics Load: Kafka Connect ClickHouse Sink delivers batched inserts at configurable batch.size to sustain 500K eps without merge pressure. Tracking Cache Refresh: Redis Sink consumer updates parcel_state hashes in Redis 7 on each enriched scan event. Dead-Letter Management: DLQ consumer exposes failed events for review and corrected-event replay. Certificate Rotation: ACM Private CA issues and renews hub mTLS certificates on 90-day cycle via automated Lambda.
target_output.system_components: MSK Cluster: 9 brokers across 3 AZs (us-east-1a/1b/1c), 3 per AZ, kafka.m5.4xlarge. Kafka Streams Application: 16 ECS Fargate tasks consuming 16 partitions each from scan-events, joining against hub-metadata KTable. Kafka Connect Cluster: 3 ECS Fargate tasks running ClickHouse Sink and Redis Sink connectors. ClickHouse Cluster: 3 shards, 2 replicas each, EC2 r6i.4xlarge with local NVMe. ElastiCache Redis 7: 3-shard cluster mode with 2 replicas per shard. DLQ Consumer: standalone ECS task with internal review HTTP endpoint. ACM Private CA: dedicated subordinate CA for hub certificate issuance. Secrets Manager: stores hub certificate PEM bundles per hub_id path. Buildkite Agent: EC2 auto-scaling group for Terraform pipeline execution.
target_output.workflows: Hub Scan Event Flow: hub scan device authenticates to MSK broker via mTLS certificate fetched from Secrets Manager path /hub/{hub_id}/kafka-client-cert, produces scan event JSON to scan-events partition keyed by tracking_number. Kafka Streams ScanEnrichmentProcessor consumes from scan-events, looks up hub_id in hub-metadata KTable (RocksDB-backed), emits enriched event to enriched-scan-events within 50ms. ClickHouse Sink connector batches 10,000 enriched events or waits 500ms before inserting to scan_events ReplicatedMergeTree table. Redis Sink consumer updates parcel_state:{tracking_number} hash with latest hub_id, scan_code, and scan_ts fields. Certificate Rotation Flow: ACM Private CA Lambda runs daily, identifies certificates expiring within 30 days, issues new 90-day certificates, stores PEM bundles to Secrets Manager, and signals hub agent reload endpoint via SNS topic so hub devices fetch new certificates without restart.
target_output.data_model: scan_events ClickHouse table: tracking_number String, hub_id String, scan_code Enum8('inbound'=1,'outbound'=2,'exception'=3,'delivery'=4), scan_ts DateTime64(3), device_id String, enriched_hub_name String, enriched_region String, raw_payload String -- partitioned by toYYYYMM(scan_ts) ReplicatedMergeTree. hub_metadata Kafka topic (hub-registry): hub_id String key, hub_name, region, timezone, carrier_lane_ids Array -- materialized as KTable in Kafka Streams. delivery_milestones ClickHouse table: tracking_number, milestone_type Enum8('first_scan','out_for_delivery','delivered','exception'), hub_id, milestone_ts DateTime64(3) -- ReplicatedMergeTree. dead_letter_records PostgreSQL table: event_id UUID PK, original_topic, partition, offset, error_reason, raw_bytes BYTEA, received_at, reviewed_by, replayed_at. parcel_state Redis hash: key parcel_state:{tracking_number}, fields hub_id, scan_code, scan_ts, ttl 24h.
target_output.api_design: No external REST API; integration surface is Kafka topic subscription. Internal AdminClient operations: GET /internal/consumer-groups/{group}/lag (Kafka Streams metrics endpoint on port 8080 per ECS task). DLQ review endpoint: GET /dlq/events?status=pending, POST /dlq/events/{event_id}/replay, POST /dlq/events/{event_id}/discard (internal-only ECS task endpoint, not load-balanced). Buildkite API used by Terraform pipeline for plan approval webhook callbacks.
target_output.security_and_compliance: mTLS: ACM Private CA subordinate CA issues 2048-bit RSA client certificates per hub service account (hub-{hub_id}-producer) and per consumer service account. Certificate lifetime 90 days. Automated renewal Lambda triggered at 30-day expiry threshold, stores new PEM to Secrets Manager /kafka/certs/{service_account} path. MSK ACL: hub producer accounts hold PRODUCE permission on scan-events topic only; kafka-streams-enrichment holds CONSUME on scan-events and hub-registry and PRODUCE on enriched-scan-events; clickhouse-sink holds CONSUME on enriched-scan-events. MSK encryption: AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit between brokers and clients. VPC: MSK endpoints in private subnets, no internet gateway route. SOC 2 controls: Buildkite Terraform audit logs retained 365 days, Datadog audit trail for MSK configuration changes, CloudTrail for all AWS API calls on MSK resources.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform modules: msk-cluster (broker count, instance type, AZ distribution, retention config per topic), kafka-streams-app (ECS Fargate task definition, auto-scaling 8-32 tasks based on consumer lag), kafka-connect-cluster (ECS service, connector configs as Terraform JSON resources), clickhouse-cluster (EC2 launch template, ASG, NVMe mount), redis-cluster (ElastiCache cluster mode). Buildkite pipeline stages: (1) tflint and terraform validate on PR open, (2) terraform plan with output diff as PR comment, (3) manual approval required for production workspace, (4) terraform apply on merge, (5) post-apply smoke test: produce 1000 synthetic scan events and verify enriched-scan-events consumer lag reaches zero within 60 seconds. Environments: staging-cluster (3 brokers, 64 partitions), production-cluster (9 brokers, 256 partitions). Rolling MSK broker upgrade procedure: Terraform updates one broker at a time with 5-minute wait between, consumer group rebalance monitored via Datadog consumer_lag_sum alert suppression window.
target_output.observability: Datadog MSK integration dashboards: consumer_lag_sum by consumer_group and topic_partition (alert threshold 50K events sustained 60s), bytes_in_per_sec per broker (alert on any broker exceeding 800MB/s), request_handler_avg_idle_percent (alert below 20%). Kafka Streams JMX metrics via Datadog JMX integration: kafka.streams.process.rate, kafka.streams.punctuate.latency.p99, kafka.streams.store.put.latency.p99. ClickHouse metrics: system.asynchronous_metrics InsertedRows/s, MergeTreeDataWrittenBytes. Custom alert: enrichment_cache_miss_rate above 5% indicates KTable rebalance or RocksDB eviction pressure. PagerDuty integration: P1 for consumer lag breach, P2 for broker throughput ceiling, P3 for enrichment cache miss rate.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: MSK 9-broker kafka.m5.4xlarge: ~$28,000/month. ECS Fargate Kafka Streams 16 tasks 4 vCPU/16GB: ~$3,200/month. ClickHouse EC2 r6i.4xlarge 6 nodes: ~$7,000/month. ElastiCache Redis 3-shard: ~$1,800/month. Total: ~$40,000/month under $45,000 cap. Scaling path to 1M eps: increase to 18 MSK brokers and 512 partitions (requires topic recreation with migration plan), scale Kafka Streams to 32 ECS tasks, add ClickHouse shard. Kafka Streams auto-scaling on consumer lag metric via ECS Application Auto Scaling policy.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): Terraform MSK 3-broker staging cluster, 64-partition scan-events topic, Kafka Streams enrichment application with hub-metadata KTable, ClickHouse Kafka Connect sink with batch configuration, Redis Sink consumer, Buildkite pipeline with plan approval gate, Datadog consumer lag dashboards. Phase 2 Production Scale (4 weeks): Terraform 9-broker production cluster, 256-partition topic migration from staging, ACM Private CA module with automated certificate rotation, MSK ACL definitions per service account, ECS auto-scaling for Kafka Streams based on consumer lag, post-apply performance regression test at 500K eps. Phase 3 Hardening (3 weeks): DLQ consumer review application, S3 Iceberg archival job for ClickHouse data beyond 90 days, SOC 2 audit log export to S3, rolling broker upgrade runbook codified as Buildkite pipeline stage.
target_output.development_guidelines: All MSK configuration changes via Terraform only; no manual AWS console modifications. Kafka Streams topology changes require integration tests against a Docker-based Kafka cluster before PR merge. ClickHouse schema migrations versioned in Flyway-equivalent scripts applied by Buildkite before connector restart. Consumer group offset resets require dual-approval in Buildkite pipeline. Java code uses checkstyle with Kafka Streams-specific rules. Performance benchmarks run in staging at 200K eps before any production Terraform apply.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Partition count immutability in Kafka means miscounting required partitions forces topic recreation with consumer group migration. Mitigation: staging load tests at 1.5x target throughput before production topic creation. Risk: ClickHouse MergeTree merge pressure under sustained 500K eps insert rate. Mitigation: batch.size=10000 and linger.ms=500 reduces insert operations to ~50/second; monitor system.asynchronous_metrics for merge backlog. Tradeoff: Kafka Streams over Flink simplifies operational model but limits windowed aggregation expressiveness for future SLA prediction use cases. Tradeoff: MSK over self-managed Kafka removes broker operations burden but constrains broker instance type selection to MSK-supported types.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Kafka Streams over Flink for enrichment layer given team Java expertise and simpler operational model. Confirmed ClickHouse Kafka Connect connector with batch.size=10000 over direct JDBC inserts. ACM Private CA chosen over self-signed certificates for automated rotation capability. Confirmed 256 partitions as initial target based on 500K eps load test in staging.

metadata.notes: High-scale Kafka infrastructure revision closing certificate rotation and ClickHouse batching gaps.

profile.domain: WMS Containerization DevOps Automation Tool

project_goal.value: Automate containerization of legacy WMS deployments on bare-metal Windows servers by generating Dockerfiles, Kubernetes manifests, and Helm charts from inventory scans, enabling logistics IT teams to migrate to GitOps workflows
project_goal.rationale: Core tool purpose replacing manual containerization effort per WMS installation

target_users.value: Logistics IT migration engineers running containerization projects for warehouse management system deployments
target_users.rationale: Internal IT team use only; no external access

project_class.rationale: CLI-driven automation generating IaC artifacts; no hosted platform or UI

capabilities.rationale: Inventory scan integration, manifest generation batch, and GitOps deployment automation

complexity_level.rationale: WinRM integration, multi-service dependency graph resolution, Kubernetes admission webhook validation, and drift detection

risk_level.rationale: Tool generates IaC applied to production AKS clusters; misconfigured manifests could cause service disruptions

data_sensitivity.rationale: Tool stores WMS service inventory and environment configs; internal operational data

external_exposure.rationale: Installed and executed exclusively on IT engineer workstations with VPN access to target servers

access_model.value: Azure AD service principal authentication for AKS cluster access; WinRM connections authenticated via Kerberos to target Windows servers
access_model.rationale: Enterprise authentication standards for both WinRM and Kubernetes API access

feature_scope.value: WinRM-based Windows server inventory scan, Dockerfile multi-stage generation, Kubernetes manifest and Helm chart generation, dry-run mode with admission webhook validation, SQLite state store for drift detection, structured diff reports
feature_scope.rationale: Complete feature set for WMS-to-AKS containerization workflow

mvp_scope.value: WinRM inventory scan, Dockerfile generation for single-service WMS installations, Kubernetes Deployment and Service manifest output, basic SQLite state tracking
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for first pilot WMS site containerization

security_baseline.value: Azure AD service principal with least-privilege AKS RBAC; WinRM sessions over HTTPS with Kerberos; no credentials stored in SQLite state; generated secrets in Helm values referencing Azure Key Vault
security_baseline.rationale: Tool handles production cluster credentials and Windows server access

frontend_stack.value: none; CLI tool with Typer-based command interface and Rich terminal output
frontend_stack.rationale: No browser UI; all interaction via command line

backend_stack.rationale: Typer provides typed CLI commands; Python ecosystem has mature WinRM and Kubernetes client libraries

data_platform.value: SQLite for local migration state store; no remote database
data_platform.rationale: Tool runs locally; SQLite sufficient for single-tool state tracking without infrastructure dependencies

hosting_target.value: Installed as Python package on IT engineer workstations; targets Azure AKS for generated manifest deployment
hosting_target.rationale: Local tool distributing IaC artifacts to AKS clusters

privacy_retention_policy.value: SQLite state file retained indefinitely on local workstation; no cloud data storage by the tool itself
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Tool does not transmit or store data to external systems

future_scope.value: Support for Linux bare-metal WMS installations via SSH scan; Terraform module generation alongside Helm charts; GitOps PR auto-creation to Azure DevOps repos
future_scope.rationale: Roadmap items after pilot WMS site validates core workflow

constraints.value: Must run air-gapped on engineer laptops without internet access to package registries during scan; Helm chart output must pass OPA Gatekeeper policies on target AKS cluster
constraints.rationale: Warehouse network environments may have restricted internet access during migration runs

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor for AKS cluster health post-deployment; structured JSON log output from tool written to local wms-migrate.log file
observability_baseline.rationale: Tool generates local logs for audit; AKS monitoring handled by existing Azure Monitor setup

execution_preference.value: Iterative development with pilot feedback after each WMS site migration; Azure DevOps pipeline for tool CI
execution_preference.rationale: Tool matures based on real WMS inventory diversity discovered in pilot sites

project.goal: Automate WMS containerization from Windows server inventory scan to Helm chart output
project.timeline: Q3 pilot site, Q4 multi-site rollout

backend.cli_output: Rich terminal tables and progress bars

security.auth: Azure AD service principal for AKS; WinRM HTTPS Kerberos
security.secrets: no credentials in SQLite; Helm values reference Azure Key Vault CSI
security.network: air-gapped capable; no runtime internet dependency

devops.packaging: published to internal Azure Artifacts PyPI feed

constraints.air_gapped: must run without internet during scan phase

open_questions.multi_service_ordering: Dependency graph ordering for multi-service WMS installs with shared DLL dependencies under active review

reasoner_reviews.product: After three rounds the core scan-to-Helm workflow is well established. The remaining gap is the dependency graph resolver for multi-service WMS installations where service B depends on a shared COM component registered by service A. Without topological sort on the service graph, generated Helm chart init containers will be ordered incorrectly and cause startup failures.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The dependency graph issue is the last architectural blocker. I recommend representing the discovered service inventory as a directed acyclic graph using networkx, topologically sorting services, and annotating Helm chart init containers with depends-on ordering. The dry-run admission webhook validation is correctly integrated via kubectl apply --dry-run=server. The SQLite drift detection comparing generated manifest checksums to live cluster state is working well per the round 3 audit.
reasoner_reviews.security: The Azure AD service principal scope is correctly limited to the target AKS namespace. The WinRM HTTPS Kerberos authentication removes the earlier plaintext credential risk. Confirm that the generated Helm values files do not inline any discovered Windows service account passwords; those must be written to Azure Key Vault and referenced via CSI driver annotations only.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Air-gapped operation is correctly implemented by bundling all Python dependencies into the package wheel and pre-loading the Kubernetes Python client CA bundle. The OPA Gatekeeper dry-run validation works against the existing cluster policy set.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Three rounds of revision have addressed authentication, drift detection, and dry-run validation. Round 4 must close the dependency graph ordering issue and ensure the generated Helm chart templates include correct init container sequencing. The open_questions entry for multi_service_ordering should be resolved and removed.

backend.service_design: WinRM inventory scanner module connects to target Windows server via pywinrm HTTPS session, executes PowerShell inventory script collecting installed services, registry entries, IIS site configurations, and shared DLL paths. Dependency resolver module builds a directed acyclic graph from service-to-shared-component edges using networkx and emits topologically sorted service list. Dockerfile generator module produces multi-stage Windows Server Core Dockerfiles per discovered service with appropriate COPY and ENTRYPOINT directives. Helm chart generator module emits per-service templates with init containers ordered by dependency graph sort.
backend.api_patterns: No network API; all interaction via Typer CLI commands: wms-migrate scan --host <ip> --output inventory.json, wms-migrate generate --inventory inventory.json --output ./charts, wms-migrate dry-run --charts ./charts --cluster staging-aks, wms-migrate apply --charts ./charts --cluster production-aks, wms-migrate diff --charts ./charts --cluster production-aks.
backend.failure_handling: WinRM connection failures emit structured JSON error to wms-migrate.log and abort scan with non-zero exit code. Dependency graph cycle detection raises CyclicDependencyError listing the cycle path so engineers can manually break circular WMS service dependencies before regenerating. Helm dry-run policy violations are collected into a structured PolicyViolationReport written to ./dry-run-report.json.
backend.background_jobs: Batch scan mode accepts a CSV of target server IPs and runs WinRM inventory scans concurrently using asyncio with configurable concurrency limit, writing per-server inventory JSON files to a batch output directory.

security.auth_design: WinRM connections use Kerberos authentication over HTTPS (port 5986). Azure AD service principal credentials loaded from environment variables AZURE_CLIENT_ID, AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET, AZURE_TENANT_ID; never persisted to SQLite state. Generated Helm values files use Azure Key Vault CSI driver secretProviderClass annotations for any discovered service account credentials.
security.secrets_management: Tool never writes discovered Windows service account passwords to disk. If a discovered service uses a service account, the tool emits a Helm values stub with a placeholder referencing an Azure Key Vault secret path and prints a pre-migration checklist item for the engineer to pre-load the secret.
security.security_risks: Risk of WinRM credential exposure if Kerberos ticket cache is readable by other processes. Mitigation: tool uses kinit-generated tickets in a tool-scoped ccache file and deletes it on exit.

devops.deployment_topology: Python package published to internal Azure Artifacts PyPI feed. Engineers install via pip install wms-migrate --index-url https://pkgs.dev.azure.com/org/_packaging/internal/pypi/simple/. All dependencies bundled as wheel extras to enable air-gapped installation from pre-downloaded wheels directory.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps pipeline: (1) ruff lint and mypy type check, (2) pytest unit tests with mocked WinRM and Kubernetes client, (3) integration test against Docker Desktop Kubernetes with a mock WMS Windows service inventory fixture, (4) bumpversion and publish to Azure Artifacts on main branch tag.
devops.devops_risks: Risk of OPA Gatekeeper policy version drift between staging and production AKS clusters causing dry-run to pass but apply to fail. Mitigation: dry-run must be run against the production cluster namespace directly, not staging.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 4 closes the final blocking issue: ISS-003 requires a directed acyclic graph dependency resolver for multi-service WMS installations. I will add networkx-based DAG construction from WinRM-discovered shared DLL and COM component dependencies, topological sort for service ordering, cycle detection with actionable error messages, and Helm chart template generation that emits initContainers in topological order.
