target_output.title: Last-Mile Delivery Failure Risk ML Prototype v2
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 research prototype predicting last-mile delivery failure risk using a LightGBM gradient boosting classifier trained on features derived from historical delivery attempt outcomes, address quality signals, time-window adherence patterns, and driver behavior metrics. The feature engineering pipeline uses DuckDB to compute derived features from raw delivery_attempts data and export training Parquet files to GCS. LightGBM training uses Optuna hyperparameter search with MLflow autolog for experiment tracking. After each training run, a GPT-4o narration step generates a natural language research summary of feature importances logged as an MLflow artifact. A FastAPI prediction endpoint on GCP Cloud Run allows researchers to score individual delivery attempts for model evaluation.
target_output.architecture_overview: The prototype runs as three sequential pipeline stages orchestrated by a Python runner script. The feature_pipeline stage reads delivery_attempts CSV from GCS into DuckDB, computes derived features in the feature_sets view (time_window_deviation_minutes, geocoding_confidence_bucket, driver_attempt_rate_30d, unit_completeness_score, historical_route_failure_rate), and exports a training Parquet file back to GCS. The training_pipeline stage loads the Parquet into LightGBM, runs an Optuna study for hyperparameter optimization (n_estimators 100-500, learning_rate 0.01-0.2, num_leaves 20-100), logs all trial metrics via MLflow autolog, and saves the best model as a .pkl artifact to GCS. The llm_narration stage calls GPT-4o via the openai Python SDK with the sorted feature importance dict and a structured logistics domain prompt, receiving a 3-paragraph research narrative logged as research_narrative.txt in MLflow and appended to the GCS research_log.md. The prediction_api is a separately deployed FastAPI Cloud Run service that loads the latest model artifact from GCS at startup and serves failure_probability scores.
target_output.technology_stack: ML: Python 3.12, LightGBM 4.3, scikit-learn 1.5, Optuna 3.6 for hyperparameter search. Feature store: DuckDB 0.10. Experiment tracking: MLflow 2.13 with GCS artifact store. LLM: OpenAI GPT-4o via openai Python SDK 1.30. API: FastAPI 0.111, Uvicorn, python-jose for JWT validation. GCP: Cloud Run for prediction API and MLflow server, GCS for data and artifacts, Secret Manager for API keys, Cloud Logging for structured pipeline logs, Artifact Registry for Docker images. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with ruff, mypy, pytest.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Feature Engineering: DuckDB computes 5 delivery attempt features from raw data: time_window_deviation_minutes (actual_attempt_ts minus scheduled_window_end), geocoding_confidence_bucket (high/medium/low bins on address_geocoding_confidence float), driver_attempt_rate_30d (rolling 30-day attempt count per driver_id), unit_completeness_score (1 if address_unit_present else 0), historical_route_failure_rate (30-day failure count / total attempts for route_id). Model Training: LightGBM binary classifier (outcome=failed as positive class) with Optuna study, MLflow autolog capturing per-trial AUC-ROC, log-loss, and feature importances. LLM Narration: GPT-4o generates 3-paragraph research summary of feature importances after each completed training run. Prediction API: FastAPI endpoint scoring individual delivery attempts using loaded model artifact. Experiment Comparison: MLflow UI for comparing AUC-ROC and feature importances across feature subset experiments.
target_output.system_components: Feature Pipeline: Python script executing DuckDB SQL feature engineering and GCS Parquet export. Training Pipeline: LightGBM + Optuna training loop with MLflow autolog and model artifact save. llm_narration module: openai SDK caller with Secret Manager key loading, MLflow artifact logger, and GCS research_log appender. FastAPI Prediction API: Cloud Run service loading model from GCS on startup, JWT-protected prediction endpoint. MLflow Tracking Server: Cloud Run service with GCS backend store at gs://research-delivery-risk/mlflow. GCS bucket research-delivery-risk/: /data for raw CSVs, /models for .pkl artifacts, /experiments for Parquet exports and research_log.md files. GCP Secret Manager: /research/openai-api-key and /research/jwt-secret paths.
target_output.workflows: Experiment Run Workflow: researcher runs python run_experiment.py --config config.yaml --feature-subset all|core5|no-driver. Feature pipeline executes DuckDB SQL, exports training.parquet to GCS. Training pipeline loads Parquet, runs Optuna study (50 trials by default), MLflow autolog captures per-trial metrics, best model saved as model_{run_id}.pkl to GCS. llm_narration.generate(run_id, importances) called: Secret Manager loads OPENAI_API_KEY, openai.ChatCompletion.create called with prompt 'You are a logistics research analyst reviewing a last-mile delivery failure risk model. The following feature importances were computed from a LightGBM classifier trained on {n_samples} delivery attempts (failure rate {failure_rate}%). Write a 3-paragraph summary: (1) which features are most predictive and why, (2) any surprising patterns or low-importance features, (3) recommended next feature engineering experiments. Feature importances (sorted): {importances_json}'. Response logged as MLflow artifact research_narrative.txt and appended to gs://research-delivery-risk/experiments/{run_id}/research_log.md. On GPT-4o failure: Cloud Logging error with narration_status=failed tag, pipeline continues. Prediction Evaluation Workflow: researcher POSTs a delivery_attempt JSON to FastAPI /predict, receives failure_probability float and top_3_features list, uses response to validate model behavior on specific scenarios.
target_output.data_model: delivery_attempts DuckDB table: attempt_id VARCHAR PK, tracking_number VARCHAR, route_id VARCHAR, driver_id VARCHAR, scheduled_window_start TIMESTAMP, scheduled_window_end TIMESTAMP, actual_attempt_ts TIMESTAMP, outcome VARCHAR CHECK('success','failed','rescheduled'), failure_reason TEXT, address_geocoding_confidence FLOAT, address_unit_present BOOLEAN, historical_route_failure_rate FLOAT -- loaded from GCS CSV. feature_sets DuckDB view: attempt_id, time_window_deviation_minutes FLOAT (actual_attempt_ts - scheduled_window_end in minutes), geocoding_confidence_bucket VARCHAR, driver_attempt_rate_30d FLOAT, unit_completeness_score INT, historical_route_failure_rate FLOAT, failure_label INT (1 if outcome='failed' else 0). model_experiments MLflow tracking: run_id, experiment_name, feature_subset_tag, n_estimators, learning_rate, num_leaves, auc_roc, log_loss, precision_at_10pct, top_feature (tag). Artifacts per run: plotly_feature_importance.html, model_{run_id}.pkl, research_narrative.txt (LLM output), training.parquet reference.
target_output.api_design: FastAPI prediction API on Cloud Run internal ingress. POST /predict: request {attempt_id: string, address_geocoding_confidence: float, address_unit_present: bool, time_window_deviation_minutes: float, driver_attempt_rate_30d: float, historical_route_failure_rate: float}, response {failure_probability: float, failure_label_predicted: bool, top_features: [{feature: string, importance: float}] top 3, model_version: string}. GET /model-info: response {model_version: string, trained_at: datetime, feature_count: int, training_auc_roc: float}. GET /health: response {status: 'ok', model_loaded: bool}. JWT required on /predict and /model-info via Authorization Bearer header. Custom JWT validated with python-jose using HMAC-SHA256 secret from GCP Secret Manager.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cloud Run service account: roles/storage.objectAdmin on research-delivery-risk GCS bucket; roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor on /research/* Secret Manager paths. No public Cloud Run ingress; internal-only ingress setting. OpenAI API key loaded at container startup from Secret Manager via Secret Manager environment variable injection (OPENAI_API_KEY env var). JWT secret loaded similarly (JWT_SECRET env var). No API keys in code, config.yaml, or Docker image. VPC Service Controls perimeter around research GCP project preventing data exfiltration. Research data contains no PII; delivery_attempts uses internal route_id and driver_id codes without personal names.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Run services: prediction-api (0-3 instances, 1 vCPU/2GB, internal ingress, min-instances=0) and mlflow-server (1 instance, 0.5 vCPU/1GB, internal ingress). GCS bucket created via Terraform or gcloud. GitHub Actions CI: (1) ruff and mypy on PR, (2) pytest for feature engineering functions and FastAPI endpoint with mocked LightGBM model and mocked openai responses, (3) Docker build with Artifact Registry push, (4) Cloud Run deploy via gcloud run deploy on main merge. MLflow tracking URI configured in config.yaml as https://mlflow-server-{hash}-uc.a.run.app. Reproducibility: full pipeline reproduced by installing requirements.txt (pinned versions) and running python run_experiment.py --config config.yaml with the same GCS data path.
target_output.observability: MLflow experiment tracking: per-run metrics (auc_roc, log_loss, precision_at_10pct, recall_at_10pct), parameters (n_estimators, learning_rate, feature_subset), tags (top_feature, narration_status), artifacts (feature importance chart, model pkl, research_narrative.txt). Cloud Logging structured logs: pipeline stage start/end with run_id, feature_count, training_duration_s, optuna_best_trial, narration_status (success/failed/skipped). Cloud Run request logs for prediction API. Researchers monitor MLflow UI for experiment comparison; no PagerDuty or SLA alerting for research prototype.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Cloud Run prediction API: 0 instances at rest (scale-to-zero), ~$5/month for intermittent research usage. MLflow server Cloud Run: ~$10/month. GCS storage for data and model artifacts: ~$5/month for expected research data volume. OpenAI GPT-4o narration: ~$0.015 per experiment run (1K input tokens feature importances + 500 output tokens). Total: ~$25/month. No database infrastructure cost; DuckDB runs in-process. Scales to larger historical datasets by increasing DuckDB memory limit in config.yaml.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (completed round 1): feature_pipeline with 5 core derived features, baseline LightGBM training with MLflow autolog, FastAPI prediction endpoint, GCS artifact storage, GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 (this round): llm_narration module with GPT-4o integration, Secret Manager API key management, narration artifact logging to MLflow and GCS research_log.md, non-blocking error handling for API failures. Phase 3 (research roadmap): Optuna hyperparameter search expanded to include feature selection (XGBoost SHAP-based feature pruning), additional feature experiments (weather API integration for delivery window conditions), feature drift detection comparing training data distribution against recent dispatch data, model card generation for productionization handoff.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python type hints on all public functions; mypy strict mode. Feature engineering functions have unit tests with synthetic delivery_attempts fixtures covering boundary cases (attempt exactly at window end, null geocoding confidence). openai SDK calls mocked in pytest via unittest.mock for CI reproducibility without API key. MLflow runs in pytest via mlflow.set_tracking_uri('sqlite:///test.db') for isolated test tracking. config.yaml schema validated with pydantic settings on pipeline startup. DuckDB queries in dedicated .sql files under src/queries/ not inline strings for readability. LightGBM model pkl saved with model version tag in filename for artifact traceability.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GPT-4o output quality for feature importance narration depends on prompt quality; initial prompts may produce generic summaries not tailored to delivery domain. Mitigation: include delivery domain context (failure rate, n_samples, feature definitions) in the system prompt and iterate based on researcher feedback. Risk: DuckDB in-memory processing may run out of memory for historical datasets exceeding available Cloud Run RAM. Mitigation: configure DuckDB memory_limit in config.yaml; large datasets processed in chunks via DuckDB streaming. Tradeoff: LightGBM gradient boosting over neural network approaches enables interpretable feature importances at the cost of modeling complex non-linear interactions; acceptable for research validation phase. Tradeoff: shared HMAC JWT secret over per-researcher tokens simplifies prototype auth at the cost of individual auditability; documented as upgrade requirement for productionization.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: LLM integration gap resolved: GPT-4o narration module added to post-training pipeline. OpenAI API key management via GCP Secret Manager confirmed. Feature drift detection deferred to Phase 3 roadmap; not blocking current research validation. MLflow GCS backend confirmed over SQLite for multi-researcher access.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision adding GPT-4o feature importance narration step to close missing llm_integration contract requirement from round 1.

profile.domain: AI-powered freight document processing for freight forwarders

project_goal.value: Automatically extract, classify, and validate shipping documents including bills of lading, commercial invoices, and packing lists using a multimodal LLM pipeline to eliminate manual TMS data entry for freight forwarders
project_goal.rationale: Core automation objective replacing high-cost manual keying workflow

target_users.value: Freight forwarder operations staff, document review agents, and TMS integration consumers
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in discovery workshops

project_class.rationale: LLM pipeline with human-in-the-loop correction loop defines this as an AI system

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, ai_llm, integrations, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: Covers document upload UI, extraction pipeline, TMS push, and batch reconciliation

complexity_level.rationale: Multimodal LLM orchestration, human review loop, and TMS API integration add significant complexity

risk_level.rationale: Confidential trade data but no direct financial transaction processing

data_sensitivity.rationale: Shipping documents contain shipper/consignee identities, cargo values, and customs data

external_exposure.rationale: All access requires Auth0 authentication; no public endpoints except webhooks with HMAC validation

access_model.value: RBAC with roles: operations_agent, reviewer, admin, api_consumer
access_model.rationale: Separate reviewer role needed for human-in-the-loop queue; api_consumer for TMS integration accounts

feature_scope.value: Document upload and classification, LLM field extraction, confidence scoring, human review queue, TMS push integration, fine-tuning feedback loop, reconciliation batch jobs
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for automating freight document processing end-to-end

mvp_scope.value: Bill of lading and commercial invoice classification, field extraction with confidence scores, human review UI for low-confidence fields, single TMS integration
mvp_scope.rationale: BOL and CI cover 80% of document volume; single TMS integration validates the push pattern

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 in transit, S3 SSE-KMS for document storage, Auth0 JWT validation on all endpoints, HMAC-SHA256 on TMS webhooks
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential document data requires encryption at rest and in transit with strong authentication

frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; rich component ecosystem for review queues and document annotation UI

backend_stack.rationale: Python LLM ecosystem (LangChain, litellm) and FastAPI async performance suit the extraction pipeline

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector extension
data_platform.rationale: Relational model for extraction jobs and correction logs; pgvector for similarity-based document routing

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate with S3 for document storage
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers with S3 SSE-KMS document vault and IAM-controlled access

privacy_retention_policy.value: Shipping documents retained 5 years per customs record-keeping requirements; extraction logs retained 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: CBP and EU customs regulations mandate multi-year retention of trade documents

future_scope.value: Packing list and certificate of origin extraction, multi-TMS connector marketplace, active learning pipeline for continuous model improvement
future_scope.rationale: Expand document type coverage and TMS reach after MVP validation

constraints.value: LLM provider must be OpenAI GPT-4o or Anthropic Claude; no self-hosted models; extraction SLA 30 seconds per document
constraints.rationale: Team lacks MLOps for self-hosted inference; 30s SLA matches forwarder operational workflow

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with LLM token cost tracking, structured extraction event logs, PagerDuty alerts on extraction error rate or queue backlog
observability_baseline.rationale: LLM cost visibility critical; queue backlog alerting prevents SLA misses

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints with continuous deployment to staging on main merge
execution_preference.rationale: Iterative delivery model for LLM prompt tuning cycles

llm_integration.value: OpenAI GPT-4o (primary) with Claude 3.5 Sonnet fallback via litellm; multimodal image+text input for document pages; structured output via JSON mode
llm_integration.rationale: GPT-4o multimodal handles mixed document formats; litellm abstraction enables model swapping without code changes

compliance_context.value: CBP customs data accuracy requirements for bills of lading and commercial invoices; GDPR Article 6 lawful basis for processing EU shipper contact data in documents; Article 28 DPA required with LLM API providers
compliance_context.rationale: Trade documents fall under CBP record-keeping; EU shipper names and addresses are personal data under GDPR requiring DPA with OpenAI/Anthropic

project.goal: Automate freight document extraction eliminating manual TMS keying
project.target_users: Freight forwarder operations staff and reviewers
project.timeline: MVP 12 weeks, full feature set 20 weeks

frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for review queue local state

backend.llm: litellm routing to GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet

security.auth: Auth0 with RBAC

reasoner_reviews.product: The human review loop is the critical adoption driver; forwarders will only trust the system if reviewers can efficiently correct and approve low-confidence extractions. The review UI needs field-level highlighting and side-by-side document rendering to minimize correction time per job.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: litellm abstraction is the right choice for model portability. The extraction pipeline should be idempotent with SHA-256 document hashes so retries do not double-process uploads. pgvector similarity routing can direct documents to carrier-specific prompt templates, improving extraction accuracy for known carrier BOL formats.
reasoner_reviews.security: S3 presigned URLs for document access are essential - documents must never be proxied through the application server. HMAC validation on TMS webhooks prevents spoofed push confirmations. GDPR Article 28 requires a documented DPA with any LLM provider processing EU shipper personal data embedded in document pages.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 30-second extraction SLA requires async processing with a job queue rather than synchronous HTTP. ECS Fargate task sizing must accommodate GPT-4o response latency peaks without timeout failures during document-heavy periods.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan lacked explicit GDPR DPA obligation with LLM providers and had no conditional PII redaction gate for EU-origin documents. The TMS reconciliation job also needs a dead-letter mechanism for persistently failing pushes.

backend.service_design: FastAPI app with four domain modules: document_ingestion (upload, S3 presign, SHA-256 dedup, job creation), extraction_pipeline (litellm orchestration, GDPR redaction gate, confidence scoring), review_queue (human review CRUD, batch approval), tms_integration (push, exponential retry, reconciliation)
backend.api_patterns: REST with OpenAPI 3.1; async job submission returns 202 with job_id for client polling; SSE stream endpoint for real-time extraction progress updates in review UI
backend.llm_integration_design: litellm router with GPT-4o primary and Claude 3.5 Sonnet fallback; document pages converted to base64 PNG before sending; structured JSON output via response_format; per-field confidence derived from logprob tokens; GDPR gate checks sender_country_code against EU set before constructing LLM payload
backend.background_jobs: Redis-backed Celery workers for extraction jobs; TMS push retries with exponential backoff up to 5 attempts then dead-letter status; nightly Celery beat reconciliation task querying TMS API confirmation receipts
backend.failure_handling: Extraction timeout sets job status FAILED with any partial results stored; TMS dead-letter records surfaced in admin review panel with full error detail and manual retry option

frontend.app_structure: React SPA with route-based code splitting; document viewer uses PDF.js for rendering pages alongside extracted field panel in split-pane layout
frontend.pages_and_flows: Upload page with drag-and-drop and duplicate detection warning, Processing status page with SSE progress bar, Review Queue listing low-confidence jobs sorted by age, Document Review page with field-level annotation and confidence color coding, Admin panel for TMS integration configuration
frontend.state_management: React Query for extraction job polling and review queue data; Zustand slice for active review session state including field focus, correction drafts, and approval checklist
frontend.frontend_risks: PDF.js rendering performance for large multi-page BOLs on reviewer machines; SSE reconnection on network interruption during active review sessions

security.auth_design: Auth0 tenant with custom RBAC namespace claims injected into JWT; FastAPI dependency decorator extracts and validates role claim per route; api_consumer role issued to TMS service accounts via client credentials flow
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager for OpenAI and Anthropic API keys with 30-day rotation; TMS HMAC secrets stored per-integration in Secrets Manager; S3 presigned URL expiry set to 15 minutes
security.privacy_controls: GDPR Article 28 DPA required with OpenAI and Anthropic; dpa_registry table tracks signed DPA status per provider; extraction pipeline conditionally strips consignee name, address, phone, and email from LLM payload when sender_country_code is in EU set and no confirmed DPA exists; GDPR erasure workflow hard-deletes S3 objects and extracted field values within 30 days

data.entities: freight_documents (id UUID PK, s3_key, sha256_hash UNIQUE, uploaded_by, document_type ENUM, sender_country_code, status ENUM, uploaded_at), extraction_jobs (id UUID PK, document_id FK, model_used, prompt_version, gdpr_redacted BOOLEAN, status ENUM, started_at, completed_at), extracted_fields (id UUID PK, job_id FK, field_name, raw_value, normalized_value, confidence FLOAT, reviewed_by, corrected_value), correction_logs (id UUID PK, extracted_field_id FK, original_value, corrected_value, reviewer_id, corrected_at), tms_push_records (id UUID PK, extraction_job_id FK, tms_integration_id FK, status ENUM, response_code, retry_count, pushed_at), dpa_registry (id UUID PK, provider_name, dpa_signed_at DATE, covers_eu_personal_data BOOLEAN)
data.schema_notes: pgvector embedding column on freight_documents enables cosine similarity search for routing documents to carrier-specific prompt templates; extraction_jobs stores prompt_version for A/B comparison
data.retention_and_deletion: Documents and extraction records retained 5 years; GDPR deletion workflow hard-deletes S3 objects and extracted_fields values containing personal data within 30 days of subject request

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster us-east-1: api-service (2-8 tasks, CPU 70% autoscale), extraction-worker-service (2-20 tasks, autoscale on Redis queue depth CloudWatch metric); RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ; ElastiCache Redis; S3 SSE-KMS with versioning and Glacier lifecycle after 5 years
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff lint, mypy strict, pytest 80% coverage gate using litellm response fixtures, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy to staging on main merge, production on version tag; Alembic migrations as ECS one-off task pre-deploy
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS service rollback to previous task definition on ALB health check failure; Alembic downgrade script tested in staging before each production release

ISS-001.title: No retry/dead-letter mechanism for failed TMS pushes
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described TMS push as fire-and-forget with no retry or dead-letter handling; failed pushes would silently drop validated extraction records

history.note: TMS integration section mentioned push but no failure path defined

ISS-002.title: Extraction job idempotency not addressed for duplicate uploads
ISS-002.detail: Re-uploading the same document could spawn duplicate extraction jobs without a deduplication mechanism

history.note: No document hash or idempotency key in ingestion design

ISS-003.title: GDPR Article 28 DPA obligation with LLM providers not documented
ISS-003.detail: Sending EU shipper personal data embedded in document pages to OpenAI/Anthropic APIs requires a signed DPA under GDPR Article 28; Round 1 plan mentioned GDPR but provided no mechanism for EU data handling in LLM calls

history.note: Compliance section referenced GDPR but no DPA requirement or conditional redaction mechanism

focus_issues.title: GDPR Article 28 DPA obligation with LLM providers not documented
focus_issues.detail: Sending EU shipper personal data embedded in document pages to OpenAI/Anthropic APIs requires a signed DPA; round 1 plan had no conditional PII redaction or DPA tracking mechanism

latest_recommendations: Add GDPR Article 28 DPA requirement for both OpenAI and Anthropic as data processors
Implement conditional EU personal data redaction gate in extraction pipeline before LLM API submission
Track DPA status per provider in a dpa_registry table accessible to the extraction pipeline
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Freight Document Intelligence Platform v1

convergence_state.reason: ISS-003 GDPR LLM data handling remains unresolved and blocking production deployment

previous_audits.summary: Solid LLM extraction pipeline design with well-structured litellm abstraction, but critical GDPR gap for LLM API personal data processing blocks production deployment; TMS retry and idempotency issues resolved in this audit cycle
strengths: litellm abstraction provides clean model portability between GPT-4o and Claude
Redis/Celery async job queue correctly addresses 30s extraction SLA constraint
pgvector carrier-specific prompt routing is a sound design for improving extraction accuracy
concerns: GDPR Article 28 DPA obligation with LLM providers absent from design
No conditional PII redaction mechanism for EU-origin documents before LLM submission
blocking_issues: ISS-003: GDPR compliance for LLM API processing of EU shipper personal data in shipping documents must be addressed before production deployment
recommendations: Add DPA registry table and Article 28 requirement to compliance_context
Implement sender_country_code check and conditional field stripping in extraction pipeline
Document GDPR data flow in security_and_compliance section

issue_updates.title: TMS push dead-letter mechanism
issue_updates.detail: tms_push_records table with Celery exponential retry and dead-letter status addresses the gap

issue_updates.title: GDPR LLM data processing basis
issue_updates.detail: Security section mentions GDPR but no conditional redaction or DPA mechanism present

best_plan.executive_summary: AI-powered freight document processing using GPT-4o multimodal extraction via litellm, human review queue for low-confidence fields, and TMS push integration on AWS ECS Fargate with PostgreSQL 16 and Redis.
best_plan.architecture_overview: React 18 SPA to FastAPI on ECS Fargate; litellm routing to GPT-4o; PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector; S3 SSE-KMS document vault; Redis/Celery async extraction workers.
best_plan.security_and_compliance: Auth0 RBAC with JWT validation; S3 presigned URLs; HMAC-SHA256 TMS webhooks; GDPR mentioned but EU LLM data handling not addressed.

target_output.thinking_summary: Resolving ISS-003 requires a GDPR-aware data handling layer in the LLM pipeline that conditionally strips EU shipper personal data fields before GPT-4o/Claude submission when a valid Article 28 DPA is not confirmed for that provider. The dpa_registry table tracks signed DPAs; the extraction pipeline checks sender_country_code at job dispatch time. All other architecture decisions from round 1 are retained; the round 1 TMS retry and idempotency fixes (ISS-001, ISS-002) are stable and carry forward.

fix_report.action_taken: Added GDPR Article 28 DPA requirement for OpenAI and Anthropic as data processors to compliance_context and compliance section; introduced dpa_registry table in data model; added conditional PII field stripping (consignee name, address, phone, email) in extraction pipeline when sender_country_code is EU and no confirmed DPA exists; documented GDPR erasure workflow in security_and_compliance
fix_report.expected_outcome: EU shipper personal data is never transmitted to LLM providers without a valid Article 28 DPA; compliance auditors can verify the dpa_registry state and gdpr_redacted flag on each extraction_job record

target_output.title: Freight Document Intelligence Platform v2
target_output.executive_summary: A GDPR-compliant AI-powered freight document processing system that classifies bills of lading, commercial invoices, and packing lists using GPT-4o multimodal extraction via litellm, routes low-confidence extractions to human reviewers, and pushes validated records to TMS integrations via HMAC-authenticated webhooks. Built on AWS ECS Fargate with React 18 and Python FastAPI, the system now includes a GDPR Article 28 DPA registry and a conditional EU personal data redaction gate in the extraction pipeline, resolving the blocking compliance issue from round 1.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture with React 18 SPA served via CloudFront communicating over HTTPS to a FastAPI service on ECS Fargate behind an ALB. Document uploads land in S3 SSE-KMS via presigned PUT URL; the API creates an extraction_job and enqueues it to a Redis-backed Celery queue. The extraction worker checks the dpa_registry to determine whether to strip EU shipper PII fields before dispatching the multimodal payload to litellm, which routes to GPT-4o primary or Claude 3.5 Sonnet fallback. Extracted fields with confidence below 0.85 enter the human review queue; approved jobs trigger HMAC-SHA256 TMS webhook push with Celery exponential retry. PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector stores extraction jobs, field results, correction logs, and the dpa_registry.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand, PDF.js for document page rendering. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, Celery 5, litellm (GPT-4o primary, Claude 3.5 Sonnet fallback). Database: PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector extension, Alembic migrations. Queue: Redis 7 via ElastiCache. Storage: S3 with SSE-KMS and versioning. Auth: Auth0 with custom RBAC namespace claims. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB with WAF, CloudFront, ECR, Secrets Manager, RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ. Observability: Datadog APM with LLM token cost metrics.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Document Ingestion: drag-and-drop upload with SHA-256 deduplication check preventing re-processing of already-extracted documents; S3 presigned PUT with 15-minute expiry so document bytes never transit the API server. Extraction Pipeline: litellm multimodal dispatch with GDPR PII redaction gate based on sender_country_code and dpa_registry lookup; per-field confidence scoring via GPT-4o logprobs; structured JSON output via response_format; gdpr_redacted flag recorded per job. Human Review Queue: split-pane UI with PDF.js document rendering and field panel showing extracted value, confidence indicator, and correction input; batch approval for jobs where all fields exceed 0.85 confidence threshold. TMS Integration: HMAC-SHA256 signed POST to TMS endpoints; dead-letter table for jobs exceeding 5 retry attempts; nightly reconciliation Celery beat task. Fine-tuning Data Store: corrected extraction records tagged by carrier and document type for future prompt iteration.
target_output.system_components: CloudFront CDN serving React SPA static assets. ALB with WAF rules (SQL injection, SSRF blocking AWS metadata endpoint probes, oversized payload rejection) routing to ECS Fargate API service (2-8 tasks, CPU 70% autoscale target). ECS Fargate extraction-worker service (2-20 tasks, autoscale on Redis queue depth CloudWatch metric exceeding 50). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ primary with read replica for analytics queries. ElastiCache Redis 7 single-node for Celery broker and result backend. S3 bucket with SSE-KMS, object versioning, and lifecycle rule transitioning to Glacier after 5 years. Secrets Manager storing OpenAI/Anthropic API keys (30-day rotation) and per-TMS HMAC secrets. dpa_registry table in PostgreSQL tracking Article 28 DPA status per LLM provider.
target_output.workflows: Document Upload and Extraction: Operations agent uploads BOL PDF; React frontend computes SHA-256 hash, calls POST /api/v1/documents/dedup-check, then uploads via S3 presigned PUT; API creates freight_documents record and extraction_job record, enqueues Celery task. Extraction worker fetches job, converts PDF pages to base64 PNG, queries dpa_registry for target LLM provider, conditionally strips consignee name/address/phone/email from payload when sender_country_code is in EU country set and covers_eu_personal_data DPA not confirmed, sets gdpr_redacted flag, invokes litellm with structured JSON response_format, stores extracted_fields with confidence scores, transitions job to REVIEW status if any confidence < 0.85 else APPROVED. Human Review: Reviewer opens REVIEW queue, selects job, sees PDF via PDF.js alongside color-coded field panel; corrects values in-place; corrections written to correction_logs; on approve, job transitions to APPROVED and TMS push Celery task enqueued. TMS Push and Reconciliation: Celery task serializes approved extraction to TMS payload, signs with HMAC-SHA256 from Secrets Manager, POSTs to TMS endpoint, records response code in tms_push_records; on 4xx/5xx re-enqueues with exponential backoff (delays: 30s, 2m, 10m, 30m, 2h) up to 5 attempts then status becomes dead_letter; nightly Celery beat task queries TMS API confirmation endpoint and flags any APPROVED jobs without confirmed TMS receipt for ops review.
target_output.data_model: freight_documents (id UUID PK, s3_key TEXT, sha256_hash TEXT UNIQUE, uploaded_by UUID FK, document_type ENUM bol/commercial_invoice/packing_list/unknown, sender_country_code CHAR(2), status ENUM pending/processing/review/approved/failed, uploaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ). extraction_jobs (id UUID PK, document_id UUID FK, model_used TEXT, prompt_version TEXT, gdpr_redacted BOOLEAN NOT NULL, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM queued/running/completed/failed). extracted_fields (id UUID PK, job_id UUID FK, field_name TEXT, raw_value TEXT, normalized_value TEXT, confidence FLOAT, reviewed_by UUID, corrected_value TEXT). correction_logs (id UUID PK, extracted_field_id UUID FK, original_value TEXT, corrected_value TEXT, reviewer_id UUID FK, corrected_at TIMESTAMPTZ). tms_push_records (id UUID PK, extraction_job_id UUID FK, tms_integration_id UUID FK, status ENUM pending/pushed/failed/dead_letter, response_code INT, retry_count INT, pushed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). dpa_registry (id UUID PK, provider_name TEXT UNIQUE, dpa_signed_at DATE, covers_eu_personal_data BOOLEAN NOT NULL, reviewed_by TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). pgvector embedding column on freight_documents for cosine similarity routing to carrier-specific prompt templates.
target_output.api_design: POST /api/v1/documents/dedup-check - body {sha256_hash}, returns {exists: bool, document_id?}. POST /api/v1/documents - create document record and presigned S3 PUT URL, returns {document_id, job_id, presigned_url, status: queued}. GET /api/v1/jobs/{job_id} - poll job status and extracted fields. GET /api/v1/review-queue?status=review - list jobs needing human review (role: reviewer+). PATCH /api/v1/jobs/{job_id}/fields/{field_id} - submit correction {corrected_value}. POST /api/v1/jobs/{job_id}/approve - approve and enqueue TMS push. GET /api/v1/admin/tms-integrations/{id}/push-records - list push attempts with dead-letter status. GET /api/v1/admin/dpa-registry - list DPA status per LLM provider (role: admin). All endpoints require Auth0 JWT; role enforcement via FastAPI dependency.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 RBAC with custom namespace claims (operations_agent, reviewer, admin, api_consumer); FastAPI route-level role dependency; api_consumer role for TMS service accounts via client credentials flow. S3 presigned PUT URLs with 15-minute expiry; document bytes never proxied through API server. HMAC-SHA256 on all outbound TMS webhook POST requests using per-integration secret from Secrets Manager; inbound TMS webhook confirmations validated against same secret. GDPR Article 28 compliance: dpa_registry table tracks signed DPA status with OpenAI and Anthropic as data processors; extraction pipeline evaluates sender_country_code against EU-27 country set at job dispatch time; when covers_eu_personal_data DPA not confirmed for target provider, consignee name, street address, phone, and email fields are stripped from the LLM prompt payload and gdpr_redacted=TRUE recorded on extraction_job; GDPR erasure workflow triggered on data subject request hard-deletes S3 object and extracted_fields values containing personal data within 30 days. CBP audit trail: every extraction_job records model_used, prompt_version, gdpr_redacted, and confidence scores providing immutable audit record for customs accuracy verification. WAF on ALB: OWASP CRS SQL injection rules, SSRF rule blocking requests to 169.254.169.254 AWS metadata endpoint embedded in document content, max request body 50MB.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate cluster in us-east-1a and us-east-1c. api-service: 2 tasks minimum, scales to 8 on CPU 70% target tracking. extraction-worker-service: 2 tasks minimum, scales to 20 on custom CloudWatch metric extraction_queue_depth exceeding 50. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ with 7-day automated backups and point-in-time recovery. ElastiCache Redis 7 single-node (r7g.large); upgrade to cluster mode when concurrent document volume exceeds 500. S3 bucket: SSE-KMS with CMK, object versioning enabled, lifecycle rule transitions objects to S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval after 5 years. GitHub Actions CI: ruff format check, ruff lint, mypy --strict, pytest with litellm response fixtures and 80% coverage gate, Docker multi-stage build, ECR push with image digest pinning, ECS rolling deploy with deployment circuit breaker; staging deploys on main merge, production on semver tag. Alembic migration runs as ECS run-task one-off before service update. Secrets Manager rotation Lambda for OpenAI/Anthropic keys every 30 days.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM with FastAPI and Celery auto-instrumentation; distributed trace IDs propagated from API through worker tasks. Custom metrics emitted: extraction_job_duration_seconds (histogram by model_used and document_type), llm_token_cost_usd (counter by provider and document_type), review_queue_depth (gauge, jobs in REVIEW status), tms_push_failure_rate (rate of dead_letter status), gdpr_redaction_rate (proportion of EU jobs with gdpr_redacted=TRUE). PagerDuty escalation: review_queue_depth > 200 for > 15 minutes (operations backlog), extraction error rate > 3% over 5 minutes, tms_push_failure_rate > 10% over 1 hour, daily LLM token cost > $500 (budget spike). Structured JSON logs include document_id, job_id, and gdpr_redacted on every extraction event for GDPR audit trail.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $2,200/month at 10,000 documents/month: ECS API service ~$180, extraction workers ~$400 (variable), RDS Multi-AZ ~$350, ElastiCache Redis ~$80, S3 + KMS ~$50, CloudFront ~$30, LLM API costs ~$1,100 (GPT-4o at ~$0.11 per document average). Cost controls: litellm fallback to Claude 3.5 Sonnet for document types where accuracy parity confirmed via A/B experiment (Claude ~40% cheaper per token); S3 Intelligent-Tiering for documents > 90 days old; Celery worker concurrency cap prevents over-provisioning at off-peak hours.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): Auth0 RBAC integration, BOL and commercial invoice document type support, GPT-4o extraction pipeline with litellm, GDPR dpa_registry and conditional PII redaction gate (ISS-003 resolution), human review queue UI with PDF.js split-pane, single TMS integration with HMAC push and Celery retry, SHA-256 deduplication, CI/CD pipeline, core observability. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Packing list and certificate of origin document types, second TMS connector, fine-tuning feedback data store, pgvector carrier-specific prompt routing, extraction analytics dashboard. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Multi-TMS connector admin UI, active learning prompt iteration pipeline driven by correction patterns, GDPR data subject erasure self-service portal.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python ruff formatter and linter plus mypy --strict enforced in CI; no lint bypass in PRs. pytest unit tests for extraction pipeline use pre-recorded litellm response fixtures to avoid live API calls in CI; coverage gate 80%. React TypeScript strict mode with ESLint react-hooks rules. LLM prompt changes require sign-off from reviewer with freight domain expertise. Feature flags via environment variables for document type rollout to avoid schema migrations blocking new types. ADR records for prompt engineering decisions and model selection rationale maintained in /docs/adr.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GPT-4o pricing increases erode unit economics at high document volumes. Mitigation: litellm fallback to Claude 3.5 Sonnet for document categories where A/B experiments confirm accuracy parity. Risk: LLM extraction accuracy degrades on novel carrier BOL formats not seen in prompt development. Mitigation: pgvector similarity routing selects carrier-specific prompt templates; correction data accumulates as fine-tuning signals. Risk: GDPR DPA lapse with LLM provider without automated alerting. Mitigation: dpa_registry includes dpa_signed_at; a weekly check alerts ops when DPA renewal is within 30 days. Tradeoff: async 30-second extraction SLA via Celery queue adds polling latency in UI but allows worker autoscaling to absorb document volume spikes without synchronous timeout failures.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: GPT-4o selected as primary over Claude for multimodal BOL extraction accuracy in internal benchmarks; litellm enables fallback without code changes. GDPR Article 28 DPA requirement and conditional PII redaction gate added to resolve ISS-003. SHA-256 deduplication prevents duplicate extraction jobs (ISS-002 resolved). PostgreSQL with pgvector chosen over DynamoDB to support relational correction log queries required by the fine-tuning pipeline.

metadata.notes: AI freight document extraction; GDPR Article 28 LLM data processing issue resolved in round 2; strong multimodal pipeline and compliance design with dpa_registry gate.

profile.domain: Adversarial security research on IoT cold chain temperature monitoring sensors for pharmaceutical logistics

project_goal.value: Investigate adversarial attack vectors against IoT cold chain temperature monitoring sensors by simulating spoofing and replay attack scenarios to identify vulnerabilities before deploying pharmaceutical-grade monitoring infrastructure
project_goal.rationale: Security validation of sensor network before production cold chain deployment

target_users.value: Logistics security researchers and pharmaceutical cold chain infrastructure engineers
target_users.rationale: Specialized internal research team operating in lab environment

project_class.rationale: Lab-only attack simulation tool; no production deployment or user-facing UI

capabilities.rationale: Packet injection/capture backend, InfluxDB time-series data, sensor network integration

complexity_level.rationale: Limited to lab attack simulation; complexity comes from Scapy packet crafting and InfluxDB metric storage

risk_level.rationale: Prototype contains active exploit tooling; misuse could compromise pharmaceutical cold chain monitoring in production

data_sensitivity.rationale: Vulnerability findings and attack signatures are confidential security research data

external_exposure.rationale: Air-gapped lab environment; no network egress; all attack simulation traffic confined to isolated sensor test network

access_model.value: Local execution only; no authentication layer; access controlled by physical lab access controls
access_model.rationale: Air-gapped environment makes application-level auth unnecessary; physical security controls apply

feature_scope.value: Spoofing attack injection against sensor MQTT topics, replay attack capture and replay with configurable delays, nonce validation detection measurement, detection latency logging, structured vulnerability report generation
feature_scope.rationale: Full attack surface coverage for sensor network pre-deployment security audit

mvp_scope.value: Spoofing injection and replay capture modules with InfluxDB metric logging and basic vulnerability report output
mvp_scope.rationale: Core attack scenarios sufficient for initial security assessment

security_baseline.value: Air-gapped network isolation; no external dependencies; all findings encrypted at rest on lab storage; GitLab CI runs in isolated runner with no internet access
security_baseline.rationale: Exploit tooling must be isolated; findings are confidential security research

frontend_stack.value: none - CLI output only with structured JSON and text reports
frontend_stack.rationale: Research prototype; no browser UI required

backend_stack.rationale: Scapy provides packet crafting for MQTT/TCP injection; asyncio manages concurrent sensor simulation tasks

data_platform.value: InfluxDB for attack metric time-series; local SQLite for test run metadata
data_platform.rationale: InfluxDB handles high-frequency sensor reading injection metrics; SQLite stores test run state without external dependencies

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal lab server in air-gapped environment
hosting_target.rationale: Physical isolation required for attack tooling; no cloud deployment

privacy_retention_policy.value: Vulnerability findings retained indefinitely on encrypted lab storage; attack captures purged after report sign-off to prevent misuse
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Research findings have long-term audit value; raw attack captures are a liability after research concludes

future_scope.value: Extend to BLE-based cold chain sensors; automated vulnerability report submission to pharmaceutical QA system
future_scope.rationale: BLE sensors are the next cold chain deployment generation

constraints.value: Must run fully offline with no external network calls; Python dependencies vendored locally; no cloud SDKs
constraints.rationale: Air-gapped lab environment prohibits any internet access

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus on lab network for attack metric visualization; structured JSON log files for audit trail
observability_baseline.rationale: Lab-local observability stack; no external monitoring service

execution_preference.value: Manual test run execution with scripted test suites; GitLab CI for code quality gates only
execution_preference.rationale: Attack scenarios require human oversight during execution; automated CI limited to linting and unit tests

compliance_context.value: FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requirements for electronic records and audit trails in pharmaceutical research environments; USP 1079 guidelines for temperature monitoring in pharmaceutical supply chains; vulnerability findings classified as confidential security research under internal data handling policy
compliance_context.rationale: Pharmaceutical cold chain research must meet FDA audit trail standards; findings inform regulatory submission evidence for monitoring system validation

project.goal: Simulate spoofing and replay attacks against cold chain sensor MQTT network and measure detection latency
project.target_users: Security researchers and pharmaceutical infrastructure engineers

backend.attack_modules: MQTT spoofing injector, TCP replay capturer, nonce validator probe

security.auth: Physical access control only

reasoner_reviews.product: The prototype has matured through two rounds. The core spoofing and replay modules are functionally sound, but the vulnerability report output format has remained underspecified. Pharmaceutical QA teams will need structured, reproducible finding records that can be cited in regulatory validation documentation under 21 CFR Part 11.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: InfluxDB is a good fit for high-frequency injection metric storage; the SQLite test_run metadata store avoids external dependencies appropriately. The remaining gap is the vulnerability_findings table schema - findings need a severity classification, affected_sensor_model field, and a HMAC integrity signature so reports cannot be tampered after generation.
reasoner_reviews.security: The air-gapped isolation is correct for exploit tooling. However, two rounds of audit have flagged that the vulnerability report file itself has no integrity protection. Given FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requirements for electronic records, each report must carry a cryptographic integrity signature verifiable independent of the tool itself.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Fully offline constraint is non-negotiable. All Python dependencies must be vendored in the repo or a local package mirror. GitLab CI runner must be configured in offline mode with no external package pulls.

backend.service_design: Three Python modules: attack_runner (asyncio orchestration of spoofing/replay tasks), packet_engine (Scapy-based MQTT packet crafting and TCP stream capture/replay), report_generator (structured vulnerability finding serialization with HMAC-SHA256 signing)
backend.api_patterns: No HTTP API; command-line invocation via argparse with subcommands: spoof, replay, report; JSON output to stdout and structured log files
backend.failure_handling: Attack injection failures logged with packet-level error detail to structured JSON log; test_run status set to FAILED in SQLite with error summary; partial findings preserved for analysis

security.auth_design: No application authentication; physical lab access control governs tool access; tool refuses to run if network interface is not on the designated isolated lab VLAN
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All test_run events logged to append-only structured JSON files with timestamp and operator ID; vulnerability_findings signed with HMAC-SHA256 using a lab-local signing key stored in encrypted key file meeting FDA 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signature requirements
security.security_risks: Exploit tooling could be misused if lab physical controls fail; mitigation is VLAN network check on startup and encrypted storage of signing keys

data.schema_notes: InfluxDB measurements use sensor_node_id and attack_scenario_id tags for filtering injection metric time series; SQLite stores relational test run state and signed findings
data.retention_and_deletion: vulnerability_findings retained indefinitely on encrypted lab storage; replay_captures purged 30 days after report sign-off; raw InfluxDB attack metrics retained 90 days

devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping custom Python metrics endpoint (attack injection rate, replay replay_count, detection latency histogram); Grafana dashboard visualizing attack scenario timelines

target_output.title: Cold Chain Sensor Adversarial Research Prototype v3
target_output.executive_summary: A Python research prototype running in an air-gapped pharmaceutical lab that simulates MQTT spoofing and TCP replay attacks against IoT cold chain temperature sensors using Scapy. Test runs are logged to InfluxDB (attack metric time series) and SQLite (test run metadata). Vulnerability findings are now stored with severity classification and affected sensor model, and each report carries a HMAC-SHA256 integrity signature meeting FDA 21 CFR Part 11 electronic record requirements. Resolves two persistent audit findings on report integrity and findings schema completeness.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-host Python application running on a bare-metal lab server isolated on a dedicated 192.168.100.0/24 VLAN with no external network egress. Three core modules: packet_engine (Scapy-based MQTT packet crafting and TCP stream capture for spoofing and replay scenarios), attack_runner (asyncio task orchestration executing configured attack scenarios against target sensor nodes), and report_generator (serializes vulnerability_findings to signed JSON report with HMAC-SHA256 block). InfluxDB stores high-frequency attack injection metrics; SQLite stores test_runs, sensor_nodes, attack_scenarios, and vulnerability_findings. Prometheus scrapes a local metrics endpoint; Grafana visualizes attack timelines on the lab network.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12 with Scapy 2.5 for packet crafting and TCP capture; asyncio for concurrent attack task orchestration; InfluxDB 2.7 (local) for attack metric time-series; SQLite 3 for relational test run state; argparse CLI with subcommands; HMAC-SHA256 via Python hmac stdlib for report signing; Prometheus client library for metrics endpoint; Grafana 10 and Prometheus on lab network. GitLab CI offline runner for ruff lint, mypy, and pytest. All Python dependencies vendored as wheel files.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Spoofing Attack Module: craft MQTT PUBLISH packets with fabricated temperature values targeting specific sensor topic paths; configurable injection rate (readings/sec) and target value range; detection latency measured from injection timestamp to monitoring system anomaly alert timestamp logged in InfluxDB. Replay Attack Module: capture authentic sensor MQTT traffic to pcap file at configurable output_dir; replay captured packets at configurable delay offsets to test nonce validation and timestamp freshness checks; replay result (rejected/accepted) logged per packet. Vulnerability Report Generator: aggregate test_run findings with severity ENUM and affected_sensor_model; compute HMAC-SHA256 signature over serialized finding fields using lab signing key; output signed JSON report to configured path. Lab Isolation Guard: on startup verify active network interface is bound to 192.168.100.0/24 VLAN; abort with error if non-lab interface detected.
target_output.system_components: Bare-metal lab server (isolated 192.168.100.0/24 VLAN, no egress routes). Python tool process with three modules: packet_engine, attack_runner, report_generator. InfluxDB 2.7 local instance for attack metric time-series (sensor_readings measurement with sensor_node_id and scenario_id tags). SQLite database file for test_runs, vulnerability_findings, and attack_scenarios. Prometheus client HTTP endpoint (localhost:9090) scraped by local Prometheus instance. Grafana dashboard visualizing attack scenario injection rates and detection latency histograms. Encrypted key file storing HMAC signing key, unlocked with operator passphrase at tool startup.
target_output.workflows: Spoofing Attack Execution: operator invokes 'python tool.py spoof --sensor-id SENSOR-07 --rate 10 --value-range 45.0-60.0 --duration 300'; attack_runner creates test_run record in SQLite, asyncio tasks call packet_engine to craft MQTT PUBLISH packets at configured rate, each injection timestamped and written to InfluxDB; monitoring system anomaly alert timestamp captured when available; detection latency computed and stored in attack_scenarios record. Replay Attack Execution: operator invokes 'python tool.py replay --capture --sensor-id SENSOR-07 --output-dir /lab/captures/run_042' to capture pcap; then 'python tool.py replay --replay --pcap /lab/captures/run_042/sensor07.pcap --delay 120' to replay with 2-minute offset; replay result (nonce_rejected/timestamp_stale/accepted) logged per packet to SQLite attack_scenarios. Report Generation: operator invokes 'python tool.py report --test-run-id 042 --severity-filter high,critical'; report_generator queries SQLite for findings, populates severity and affected_sensor_model fields, serializes to JSON, computes HMAC-SHA256 over canonical field string using lab key file, appends signature block, writes to /lab/reports/run_042_signed.json.
target_output.data_model: test_runs (id INTEGER PK, attack_type TEXT ENUM spoof/replay, target_sensor_model TEXT, operator_id TEXT, started_at DATETIME, completed_at DATETIME, status TEXT ENUM running/completed/failed, notes TEXT). sensor_nodes (id INTEGER PK, model TEXT, mac_address TEXT, firmware_version TEXT, lab_position TEXT). attack_scenarios (id INTEGER PK, test_run_id FK, scenario_type TEXT, parameters_json TEXT, executed_at DATETIME, outcome TEXT). replay_captures (id INTEGER PK, test_run_id FK, pcap_file_path TEXT, capture_duration_sec INT, captured_at DATETIME). vulnerability_findings (id INTEGER PK, test_run_id FK, severity TEXT ENUM critical/high/medium/low NOT NULL, title TEXT, affected_sensor_model TEXT NOT NULL, finding_detail TEXT, hmac_signature TEXT NOT NULL, generated_at DATETIME). InfluxDB measurement: sensor_readings (sensor_node_id tag, attack_scenario_id tag, value_celsius field, injected BOOLEAN field, timestamp).
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API. CLI interface via argparse: 'tool.py spoof --sensor-id ID --rate N --value-range MIN-MAX --duration SECS'. 'tool.py replay --capture|--replay --sensor-id ID --output-dir PATH|--pcap PATH --delay SECS'. 'tool.py report --test-run-id ID [--severity-filter LEVELS] [--output-path PATH]'. 'tool.py list-runs' outputs JSON array of test_run records. 'tool.py verify-report --report-path PATH' verifies HMAC signature of a signed report file. All commands log structured JSON to /lab/logs/tool_{date}.jsonl.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Air-gapped network isolation: tool checks at startup that the active network interface IP is within 192.168.100.0/24; aborts with exit code 2 if non-lab interface detected, preventing accidental execution on connected machines. Physical access controls govern lab entry; no application authentication layer. FDA 21 CFR Part 11 compliance for electronic records: vulnerability_findings records carry HMAC-SHA256 integrity signatures computed over canonical concatenation of (id, test_run_id, severity, title, affected_sensor_model, finding_detail, generated_at) using PBKDF2-derived key from operator passphrase and lab-specific salt stored in encrypted key file; signature stored in hmac_signature column and included in report JSON signature block. Signed reports can be independently verified with 'tool.py verify-report' without re-running attack scenarios. USP 1079 alignment: all temperature injection values and detection timestamps logged to InfluxDB with sub-second precision satisfying cold chain monitoring audit trail requirements. Encrypted key file stored with 600 permissions on lab server; passphrase required at tool startup.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Single bare-metal lab server running Ubuntu 22.04 LTS on isolated 192.168.100.0/24 VLAN with all external routes removed from routing table. Python tool installed from vendored wheel packages in /lab/vendor/ with no pip network calls. InfluxDB 2.7 installed as systemd service; data directory on encrypted LUKS volume. SQLite database at /lab/data/research.db on same encrypted volume. GitLab CI offline runner executes on lab server: ruff format check, mypy --strict, pytest with mock Scapy and InfluxDB clients (no live packet injection in CI). No Docker; bare Python venv installation. Manual test run execution with human oversight during attack scenarios. Backup: nightly encrypted tar of SQLite database and signed reports to lab NAS.
target_output.observability: Prometheus client library in tool exposes metrics endpoint on localhost:9100: injection_packets_total (counter by scenario_type and sensor_model), replay_result_total (counter by outcome enum), detection_latency_seconds (histogram by attack_type), report_generation_total (counter by severity_filter). Grafana dashboard on lab network visualizes injection rate timelines and detection latency distributions per attack scenario. Structured JSON logs to /lab/logs/tool_{date}.jsonl with test_run_id and scenario_id on every event for post-hoc analysis.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud cost; bare-metal lab server amortized over research engagement. InfluxDB retention policy set to 90 days for attack metrics to manage local disk; SQLite database size bounded by finding count. Python tool scales to concurrent attack scenarios via asyncio tasks up to NIC packet injection rate limit; single-host scope is appropriate for targeted sensor model evaluation.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (completed): Scapy spoofing injector and replay capture/replay modules, InfluxDB metric logging, SQLite test run tracking, configurable output_dir for captures (ISS-001 resolved). Phase 2 (this round): HMAC-SHA256 report signing with encrypted lab key file (ISS-002), extended vulnerability_findings schema with severity and affected_sensor_model (ISS-003), 'verify-report' CLI subcommand, Prometheus metrics endpoint, Grafana dashboard. Phase 3 (future): BLE sensor attack module using Scapy BLE extensions, automated finding submission to pharmaceutical QA system via secure lab-internal API.
target_output.development_guidelines: ruff lint and mypy --strict enforced in GitLab CI offline runner. pytest unit tests mock Scapy packet engine and InfluxDB client to avoid live packet injection in CI. HMAC signing logic covered by dedicated unit tests with known test vectors. No external package installation during CI; all dependencies vendored. operator_id logged on all test_run and finding records for accountability. Attack scenario parameters validated before execution to prevent accidental out-of-range injection that could damage sensor hardware.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: HMAC key file loss or corruption makes historical signed reports unverifiable. Mitigation: key file backed up to encrypted lab NAS with offline copy held by lab security officer. Risk: Scapy MQTT injection technique may not generalize to all sensor firmware versions; some may use proprietary transport layer. Mitigation: sensor_nodes table records firmware_version enabling finding scoping to specific versions. Tradeoff: SQLite over PostgreSQL keeps infrastructure minimal for air-gapped deployment but limits concurrent write performance if multiple researchers run scenarios simultaneously; acceptable for low-concurrency lab use.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: HMAC-SHA256 chosen over GPG signing for simplicity of lab-local key management without requiring GPG keyring infrastructure. SQLite retained over InfluxDB for findings metadata because relational queries on severity and sensor_model are simpler in SQL. FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requirement for audit trail integrity satisfied by HMAC signing combined with append-only structured log files.

metadata.notes: Cold chain IoT adversarial research prototype; round 3 resolves report integrity (ISS-002) and findings schema completeness (ISS-003); weak quality reflects prototype scope with some thin sections.

profile.domain: E-commerce returns management platform decomposed from monolithic WMS into microservices for fulfillment centers

project_goal.value: Decompose the returns management module from a monolithic WMS into discrete microservices handling return authorization, item inspection grading, restocking decisions, and refund triggering, with a web UI for warehouse agents and a REST API for connected e-commerce storefronts
project_goal.rationale: Monolith deployment coupling is blocking independent scaling and storefront partner onboarding

target_users.value: Warehouse agents (item grading UI), e-commerce storefront operators (REST API consumers), and operations analysts (admin dashboard)
target_users.rationale: Three distinct user groups with different access patterns and data needs

project_class.rationale: Web UI for warehouse agents plus REST API for storefront partners qualifies as fullstack

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, analytics, admin_panel, public_api
capabilities.rationale: Covers grading UI, microservice APIs, storefront integration, return analytics, and ops admin

complexity_level.rationale: Microservices decomposition with gRPC inter-service communication, event-driven grading outcomes, and PCI-scoped refund handling

risk_level.rationale: Refund triggering touches financial transactions; storefront partner access requires strict data isolation

data_sensitivity.rationale: Refund transaction records and storefront payment references require financial data classification

external_exposure.rationale: Public REST API consumed by e-commerce storefront partners with per-tenant API key scoping

access_model.value: Keycloak RBAC: warehouse_agent, grader, operations_analyst, admin, storefront_api_consumer; storefront partners use per-tenant API keys mapped to storefront_api_consumer role
access_model.rationale: Role separation between warehouse staff and API consumers; tenant isolation for storefront data

feature_scope.value: Return authorization creation and eligibility check, item inspection grading UI, restocking decision routing, refund trigger integration, storefront partner REST API, return rate analytics dashboard, admin configuration panel
feature_scope.rationale: Full returns lifecycle from shopper request through warehouse processing to refund

mvp_scope.value: Return authorization service, item grading service with warehouse agent UI, refund trigger API integration for primary storefront, basic operations admin panel
mvp_scope.rationale: Core returns processing loop with one storefront integration validates the decomposed architecture

security_baseline.value: mTLS between all microservices, TLS 1.3 for external API, Keycloak JWT validation, per-tenant API key scoping for storefront access, audit log on all refund trigger events
security_baseline.rationale: Inter-service mTLS required for partner-facing architecture; refund events require audit trail

frontend_stack.rationale: Consistent with other fulfillment center UI tooling; team expertise

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with gRPC for inter-service communication
backend_stack.rationale: Go performance and gRPC type-safety suit high-throughput returns processing during peak periods

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 per-service databases with Redis 7 for session and event cache
data_platform.rationale: Database-per-service pattern enforces service boundary isolation; Redis caches hot return authorizations

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot on GCP
hosting_target.rationale: Kubernetes managed scaling for microservices; Autopilot reduces cluster management overhead

privacy_retention_policy.value: Return records retained 7 years for financial audit; EU customer return data subject to GDPR 90-day anonymization after closure
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Financial record retention requirements; GDPR for EU shopper names and addresses in return authorizations

future_scope.value: ML-based return reason classification, automated restocking routing by condition grade, multi-carrier return label generation
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP optimization to reduce manual grading decisions

constraints.value: Must preserve existing WMS database schemas for return_authorizations and returned_items during transition period; no monolith downtime during decomposition
constraints.rationale: Strangler fig migration pattern required; WMS cannot be taken offline during cutover

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus on GKE, distributed tracing with OpenTelemetry, structured JSON logs with trace IDs
observability_baseline.rationale: Per-service metrics and distributed traces essential for microservices debugging

execution_preference.value: Strangler fig migration: new services run in parallel with WMS, traffic shifted incrementally by return type
execution_preference.rationale: Incremental migration reduces cutover risk for active fulfillment operations

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS SAQ A-EP for refund trigger API transmitting payment references to storefront payment processors; GDPR data minimization and right-to-erasure for EU shopper data in return authorizations
compliance_context.rationale: Refund API passes card-last-four and payment reference values triggering PCI scope; EU shopper addresses in return records require GDPR handling

project.goal: Decompose returns management from WMS monolith into microservices with agent UI and partner API
project.target_users: Warehouse agents, storefront partners, operations analysts

frontend.state: React Query for service state, Zustand for active grading session

backend.framework: gRPC for inter-service, Gin for external REST API gateway

security.auth: Keycloak RBAC

data.cache: Redis 7 for session and hot RA cache

confirmed_decisions.service_mesh: Istio for mTLS and traffic management

reasoner_reviews.product: The event-driven pattern for grading outcomes is correct - publishing a GradeApplied event consumed independently by restock-svc and refund-trigger-svc allows each service to act without tight coupling. The warehouse grading UI needs a barcode scan input mode for fast condition grade assignment at high-volume return stations.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Database-per-service is the right call for enforcing boundaries, but the strangler fig migration means return_authorizations and returned_items will be read from both the legacy WMS PostgreSQL and new service databases during transition. A sync job or shared read replica will be needed for the migration period. gRPC between services gives strong type contracts but adds proto file management overhead.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS between services via Istio and cert-manager is the correct pattern. The refund-trigger-svc transmits payment references to storefront processors, putting it in PCI scope even under SAQ A-EP. Refund events must be logged to an immutable audit table with full request/response detail. Per-tenant API key scoping ensures storefront A cannot query storefront B return data.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Strangler fig with zero WMS downtime requires the new services to shadow-write to both legacy and new schemas during the transition window. Cloud Build pipelines need separate jobs per service to enable independent deployment.

backend.service_design: Five Go microservices: return-auth-svc (RA creation, eligibility check against order service, RA status management), grading-svc (condition grade recording, GradeApplied event publish), restock-svc (restocking routing by grade, warehouse location assignment), refund-trigger-svc (refund API call to storefront payment processor, PCI audit log), analytics-svc (return rate aggregation, scorecard metrics)
backend.api_patterns: gRPC for inter-service calls with proto3 contracts; Gin REST API gateway for external storefront partner API (v1); API gateway translates REST to gRPC internally; OpenAPI 3.1 spec published for partner integration
backend.business_modules: Return eligibility rules engine in return-auth-svc checking order age, product return policy, and SKU return flag; grade-to-restock routing matrix in restock-svc mapping condition grades to warehouse zones (resell/refurbish/donate/scrap)
backend.failure_handling: gRPC deadline propagation on all inter-service calls; refund-trigger-svc implements saga pattern with compensating cancel-refund call on downstream failure; GradeApplied events published to Pub/Sub with at-least-once delivery

frontend.app_structure: React SPA with feature-based routing: /returns (RA list), /returns/:id/grade (grading workflow), /admin (configuration), /analytics (return rate dashboard)
frontend.pages_and_flows: Return Authorization List with filter by status and storefront; Grading Workflow with barcode scan input, condition grade selector (A/B/C/D), and photo capture upload; Restock Confirmation showing assigned warehouse zone; Operations Analytics with 90-day return rate charts by SKU and return reason; Admin panel for storefront API key management
frontend.state_management: React Query for RA list, grading job, and analytics data; Zustand slice for active grading session state including scanned barcode, selected grade, and photo upload queue

security.auth_design: Keycloak realm with client applications per service; warehouse_agent and grader roles for UI users; storefront_api_consumer role for partner API keys issued via Keycloak service accounts
security.authorization_model: Row-level tenant isolation in return-auth-svc and analytics-svc: all queries filter by storefront_tenant_id extracted from Keycloak JWT claim; storefront partners can only read their own RAs via API
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Immutable audit_events table in refund-trigger-svc PostgreSQL recording refund_trigger_request_payload, refund_trigger_response_code, amount_cents, currency, storefront_tenant_id, triggered_by, triggered_at; append-only enforced via INSERT-only Postgres role for service account

data.entities: return_auth_svc DB: return_authorizations (id, storefront_tenant_id, order_id, storefront_order_ref, shopper_email_hashed, return_reason ENUM, status ENUM, created_at), returned_items (id, ra_id, sku, quantity, expected_condition). grading_svc DB: inspection_grades (id, returned_item_id, grade ENUM A/B/C/D, grader_id, graded_at, photo_s3_key, notes). restock_svc DB: restock_decisions (id, returned_item_id, grade, assigned_zone ENUM, assigned_at, warehouse_agent_id). refund_trigger_svc DB: refund_triggers (id, ra_id, amount_cents, currency, storefront_payment_ref, status ENUM, triggered_at), audit_events (immutable). analytics_svc DB: return_metrics_hourly (storefront_tenant_id, sku, return_reason, grade_distribution_json, count, hour_bucket)
data.schema_notes: Strangler fig transition: return-auth-svc dual-writes to both legacy WMS return_authorizations table and new service table during migration window; sync validation job compares counts nightly
data.migration_notes: Legacy WMS schema compatibility layer: new return-auth-svc reads existing return_authorizations rows via a read-replica connection string during transition; new rows written to both tables until WMS module is decommissioned

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster with five service namespaces; Istio service mesh for mTLS and traffic shifting; cert-manager for automatic mTLS certificate rotation; Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 per-service instances; Memorystore Redis for shared session cache
devops.ci_cd_design: Cloud Build with separate trigger per service repository; each pipeline: golangci-lint, go test with -race flag, Docker build, Artifact Registry push, GKE rolling deploy; independent deployments enable single-service releases without monolith coordination
devops.observability_stack: OpenTelemetry Collector sidecar per pod exporting traces to Cloud Trace; Prometheus ServiceMonitor per service with Grafana dashboards; custom metrics: ra_creation_rate, grade_throughput, refund_trigger_latency_ms, restock_assignment_rate
