target_output.thinking_summary: Two fixes needed: ISS-002 requires an immutable audit_events table in refund-trigger-svc with an INSERT-only Postgres service account to satisfy PCI-DSS SAQ A-EP audit trail requirements for refund events; ISS-003 requires documenting the strangler fig dual-write strategy where return-auth-svc writes to both the legacy WMS return_authorizations table and the new service database during the migration window, validated by a nightly sync count job. Both fixes are incremental additions to the already-sound microservices decomposition design from round 1.

fix_report.action_taken: Added immutable audit_events table to refund-trigger-svc PostgreSQL database with INSERT-only Postgres role for the service account; table records full refund_trigger_request_payload, response_code, amount_cents, currency, storefront_tenant_id, triggered_by, and triggered_at; all refund trigger attempts including failures logged before external API call
fix_report.expected_outcome: PCI-DSS SAQ A-EP audit trail requirement satisfied; QSA can verify all refund trigger events including failures are immutably logged with full payload detail

fix_report.action_taken: Documented strangler fig dual-write strategy: return-auth-svc writes new RAs to both legacy WMS return_authorizations table (via shared read-replica connection to legacy DB) and new service DB; nightly Cloud Scheduler job compares record counts and checksums between schemas; Istio traffic weight shifted from 0% to 100% over 4 weeks once sync validation passes consistently
fix_report.expected_outcome: WMS can be migrated with zero downtime; data divergence detected within 24 hours by sync validation job; Istio weight shifting enables gradual traffic migration with instant rollback capability

target_output.title: Returns Management Microservices Platform v2
target_output.executive_summary: A microservices decomposition of a monolithic WMS returns module into five Go services (return-auth, grading, restock, refund-trigger, analytics) communicating over Istio-managed mTLS gRPC on GKE Autopilot. The React 18 warehouse agent UI handles return authorization review and condition grading; a Gin REST API gateway exposes storefront partner endpoints with per-tenant Keycloak scoping. This round adds an immutable PCI audit log to refund-trigger-svc and documents the strangler fig dual-write migration strategy for zero-downtime WMS cutover.
target_output.architecture_overview: Five Go microservices deployed on GKE Autopilot with Istio service mesh enforcing mTLS via PeerAuthentication STRICT policy. External traffic enters via a Gin API gateway (TLS 1.3, Keycloak JWT validation) which translates REST to gRPC for internal services. React 18 SPA served from Cloud CDN communicates with the API gateway. return-auth-svc handles RA creation and eligibility; grading-svc records condition grades and publishes GradeApplied events to Cloud Pub/Sub; restock-svc and refund-trigger-svc subscribe to GradeApplied events independently. refund-trigger-svc calls storefront payment processor APIs and logs every attempt to an immutable audit_events table with an INSERT-only Postgres service account. analytics-svc aggregates return metrics by SKU, tenant, and grade into hourly buckets. During strangler fig migration, return-auth-svc dual-writes to legacy WMS PostgreSQL and new service DB simultaneously.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand. Backend: Go 1.22, gRPC with protobuf v3, Gin for external REST gateway. Service mesh: Istio with cert-manager for mTLS certificate rotation. Database: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 per service (five instances). Cache: Memorystore Redis 7 for RA session cache and API gateway rate limiting. Pub/Sub: Google Cloud Pub/Sub for GradeApplied event fan-out. Auth: Keycloak with RBAC. CI/CD: Cloud Build per-service pipelines. Observability: OpenTelemetry Collector, Cloud Trace, Prometheus, Grafana.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Return Authorization Service: eligibility check against order service (age, policy, SKU flag), RA creation with shopper email hashed at rest, dual-write to legacy WMS during migration window, tenant-scoped RA queries for storefront API. Grading Service: barcode scan input for high-throughput grading stations, condition grade A/B/C/D selector, photo capture upload to Cloud Storage, GradeApplied Pub/Sub event emission. Restock Service: grade-to-zone routing matrix (A=resell, B=refurbish, C=donate, D=scrap), warehouse zone assignment, agent confirmation UI. Refund Trigger Service: storefront payment processor API call with saga compensating cancel on failure, immutable PCI audit log INSERT before external call. Analytics Service: 90-day return rate by SKU and tenant, grade distribution charts, high-return-rate SKU alerts for operations analysts.
target_output.system_components: GKE Autopilot cluster with five service namespaces (return-auth, grading, restock, refund-trigger, analytics) each with dedicated Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 instance. Istio ingress gateway (TLS 1.3 termination) fronting Gin REST API gateway pod. Istio sidecar proxies enforcing mTLS on all pod-to-pod gRPC traffic. Cloud Pub/Sub GradeApplied topic with push subscriptions to restock-svc and refund-trigger-svc. Memorystore Redis 7 for API gateway session tokens and hot RA lookups. Cloud CDN serving React SPA from Cloud Storage bucket. Keycloak cluster (2 replicas) in auth namespace. cert-manager issuing short-lived mTLS certificates for Istio workload identities.
target_output.workflows: Return Authorization Creation (Storefront API): storefront POST /v1/return-authorizations with order_ref and return_reason; API gateway validates Keycloak JWT, extracts storefront_tenant_id claim, forwards gRPC CreateReturnAuthorization to return-auth-svc; service checks order eligibility, creates RA in service DB, dual-writes to legacy WMS return_authorizations table during migration window, returns RA ID and prepaid label URL. Item Grading (Warehouse Agent): agent scans returned item barcode on grading station, React app GETs RA details, agent selects condition grade and uploads photo, PATCH /v1/grade-sessions/{id}; grading-svc records inspection_grades, publishes GradeApplied{ra_id, item_id, grade, tenant_id} to Pub/Sub; restock-svc subscriber assigns warehouse zone; refund-trigger-svc subscriber calls storefront refund API, logs full request and response to audit_events before and after external call, updates refund_triggers record. Strangler Fig Migration: for 4-week transition window, return-auth-svc writes to both legacy WMS DB (via read-replica promoted to writable for migration only) and new service DB; nightly Cloud Scheduler triggers sync validation job comparing RA counts and status checksums between schemas; Istio VirtualService weight shifted from 0 to 25 to 50 to 100% over 4 weeks contingent on zero sync discrepancies; WMS module decommissioned after 2 weeks of 100% new-service traffic.
target_output.data_model: return-auth-svc DB: return_authorizations (id UUID PK, storefront_tenant_id UUID, order_id TEXT, storefront_order_ref TEXT, shopper_email_hashed TEXT, return_reason ENUM, status ENUM pending/active/completed/rejected, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ); returned_items (id UUID PK, ra_id UUID FK, sku TEXT, quantity INT, expected_condition TEXT). grading-svc DB: inspection_grades (id UUID PK, returned_item_id UUID, grade ENUM A/B/C/D, grader_id UUID, graded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, photo_storage_key TEXT, notes TEXT). restock-svc DB: restock_decisions (id UUID PK, returned_item_id UUID, grade ENUM, assigned_zone ENUM resell/refurbish/donate/scrap, assigned_at TIMESTAMPTZ, warehouse_agent_id UUID). refund-trigger-svc DB: refund_triggers (id UUID PK, ra_id UUID, amount_cents INT, currency CHAR(3), storefront_payment_ref TEXT, status ENUM pending/triggered/failed/cancelled, triggered_at TIMESTAMPTZ); audit_events (id UUID PK, refund_trigger_id UUID FK, event_type ENUM attempt/success/failure/compensation, request_payload JSONB, response_code INT, response_body_truncated TEXT, triggered_by TEXT, triggered_at TIMESTAMPTZ) - INSERT only via service account. analytics-svc DB: return_metrics_hourly (storefront_tenant_id UUID, sku TEXT, return_reason TEXT, grade_distribution JSONB, count INT, hour_bucket TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: External REST API (Gin gateway, /v1, Keycloak JWT required): POST /v1/return-authorizations (storefront creates RA), GET /v1/return-authorizations?tenant={id}&status={s} (list RAs, tenant-scoped), GET /v1/return-authorizations/{id} (RA detail), POST /v1/grade-sessions (create grading session), PATCH /v1/grade-sessions/{id} (submit grade and photo), GET /v1/analytics/return-rates?tenant={id}&days=90 (analytics data). Internal gRPC services: ReturnAuthService.CreateRA, ReturnAuthService.GetRA, GradingService.RecordGrade, RestockService.AssignZone, RefundTriggerService.TriggerRefund - all enforced via Istio mTLS AuthorizationPolicy allowing only named service accounts.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Istio PeerAuthentication STRICT mode enforces mTLS on all pod-to-pod gRPC traffic; cert-manager rotates workload certificates every 24 hours. Keycloak JWT validation at API gateway with storefront_tenant_id claim extracted for row-level isolation in all service queries. PCI-DSS SAQ A-EP compliance for refund-trigger-svc: audit_events table uses INSERT-only Postgres role (no UPDATE/DELETE privileges granted to service account); every refund trigger attempt logged before external API call with full request payload, response code, amount, currency, and tenant ID; audit_events retained 3 years per PCI requirement. GDPR: shopper email hashed (SHA-256) at RA creation; EU shopper return records anonymized 90 days after closure by scheduled Cloud Scheduler job replacing name/address with hashed identifiers; right-to-erasure workflow flags tenant_id records for 30-day anonymization. API gateway rate limiting via Redis token bucket per storefront_tenant_id to prevent partner abuse.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot cluster in europe-west1 (EU data residency for GDPR). Five Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 instances (one per service), each with private IP only, accessed via Cloud SQL Auth Proxy sidecar. Cloud Build trigger per service repository: golangci-lint, go test -race -coverprofile with 80% gate, Docker multi-stage build (distroless base), Artifact Registry push, GKE rolling deploy with PodDisruptionBudget minAvailable=1. Strangler fig migration: Istio VirtualService weights managed via GitOps (Argo CD) config repo; weight changes require PR approval; rollback by reverting weight commit. Keycloak deployed as StatefulSet with CloudSQL backend for realm data. cert-manager ClusterIssuer configured with GCP Certificate Authority Service for workload mTLS certs.
target_output.observability: OpenTelemetry Collector DaemonSet exporting traces to Cloud Trace with trace IDs propagated via gRPC metadata. Prometheus ServiceMonitor per service scraping /metrics endpoint. Grafana dashboards: per-service gRPC error rate, refund_trigger_latency_ms histogram, grade_throughput by fulfillment center, return_rate by SKU (90-day rolling). Alerting: refund trigger error rate > 2% over 10 minutes (PagerDuty), RA creation error rate > 1% (Slack), restock_assignment_lag > 30 minutes (ops email). Distributed trace sampling 10% normal, 100% on gRPC error codes.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot scales services independently; refund-trigger-svc and analytics-svc scale to near zero off-peak reducing idle cost. Five Cloud SQL instances ~$800/month total (dev uses shared smaller instances). Memorystore Redis ~$120/month. Cloud Pub/Sub pay-per-message ~$30/month at 100K returns/month. GKE Autopilot ~$600/month at average load. Total estimate $1,700/month at 100K returns/month; per-return cost decreases at scale due to fixed infrastructure overhead.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (14 weeks): return-auth-svc with eligibility engine and strangler fig dual-write, grading-svc with warehouse agent React UI and barcode scan, refund-trigger-svc with immutable PCI audit log (ISS-002), Keycloak RBAC with tenant scoping, Istio mTLS (ISS-001 from round 1), basic storefront REST API, Cloud Build CI per service. Phase 2 (6 weeks): restock-svc with grade-to-zone routing, GradeApplied event fan-out to restock and refund services, analytics-svc with return rate dashboard, GDPR anonymization scheduler, admin panel for storefront API key management. Phase 3 (4 weeks): WMS strangler fig traffic shift 0-100% over migration window (ISS-003), WMS module decommission, ML return reason classification prototype, multi-carrier return label generation.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go modules with golangci-lint (errcheck, staticcheck, gosec) enforced in Cloud Build. go test -race flag in CI to catch concurrency bugs in event consumer goroutines. Proto files versioned in a shared /proto repository; breaking changes require major version bump. Service account least-privilege: refund-trigger-svc Postgres service account granted INSERT only on audit_events, SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE on refund_triggers, no other tables. Feature flags via Cloud Run parameter store for migration traffic weight changes. ADR records for each service boundary decision and event schema choice.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Strangler fig dual-write divergence goes undetected if sync validation job fails silently. Mitigation: sync job alerts on execution failure independent of schema comparison result; Istio weight rollback automated if discrepancy count exceeds threshold. Risk: Pub/Sub at-least-once delivery causes duplicate GradeApplied processing in restock and refund services. Mitigation: idempotency keys in each consumer using inspection_grade_id as deduplication key in Cloud Pub/Sub subscription message ID filter. Tradeoff: five Cloud SQL instances over a shared database increases cost but enforces service boundary data isolation required for independent deployment and PCI audit scoping of refund-trigger-svc.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Istio mTLS over mutual TLS at application layer chosen for cert rotation automation and Kubernetes-native policy enforcement. Cloud Pub/Sub over Kafka for GradeApplied events given GCP-native deployment and modest event volume. Refund trigger PCI audit immutability enforced via INSERT-only Postgres role rather than separate audit service to minimize operational complexity. Strangler fig 4-week migration window with GitOps Istio weight management reduces manual cutover risk.

metadata.notes: Returns management WMS decomposition; round 2 resolves PCI audit log (ISS-002) and strangler fig migration strategy (ISS-003); moderate quality with some sections at minimum depth.

profile.domain: Multi-modal carrier rate and transit time aggregation API for e-commerce and ERP shipping integrations

project_goal.value: Aggregate real-time rate quotes and transit time estimates from parcel, LTL, and FTL carriers through a single unified REST API, enabling e-commerce platforms and ERPs to fetch multi-modal shipping options at checkout without building individual carrier integrations
project_goal.rationale: Fragmented carrier integrations across 40+ customer platforms are being replaced by a single aggregation API reducing integration cost

target_users.value: E-commerce platforms, ERP systems, and shipping software integrators consuming the API at checkout or order creation time
target_users.rationale: B2B API consumers building shipping rate selection into their checkout flows

project_class.rationale: No user-facing UI; pure REST API consumed by B2B integrators; no FrontendAgent needed

capabilities.value: backend, data, auth, integrations, analytics, public_api
capabilities.rationale: Rate aggregation backend, carrier credential data, JWT auth for API clients, carrier API integrations, usage analytics, public REST API

complexity_level.rationale: Concurrent carrier fan-out, intelligent Redis caching, per-carrier adapter pattern, and usage metering add architectural complexity

risk_level.rationale: Public internet API with carrier credentials in secrets store; no financial transaction processing but API abuse could trigger unauthorized carrier API calls

data_sensitivity.rationale: Rate data and transit estimates are internal business data; no PII in rate requests beyond origin/destination zip codes

external_exposure.rationale: API is publicly addressable; API clients authenticate with scoped JWT tokens

access_model.value: JWT tokens issued per API client with scopes specifying accessible carrier accounts and transport modes (parcel, ltl, ftl); token issued via client credentials flow
access_model.rationale: Per-client scoping prevents one API consumer from accessing another client's carrier account credentials

feature_scope.value: Multi-carrier rate fan-out with response normalization, Redis rate cache per shipment profile, transit time estimation, per-client usage metering, nightly carrier surcharge table refresh, API client management, usage analytics dashboard for internal ops
feature_scope.rationale: Complete rate aggregation feature set for production API service

mvp_scope.value: Parcel and LTL rate fan-out for top 5 carriers, Redis caching, per-client JWT auth, usage metering, nightly surcharge refresh
mvp_scope.rationale: Parcel and LTL cover 80% of volume; top 5 carriers cover 90% of market share

security_baseline.value: JWT validation on all API endpoints, carrier API credentials in Secrets Manager, TLS 1.3 on all inbound and outbound connections, rate limiting per API client token, API Gateway WAF rules
security_baseline.rationale: Public API requires WAF and rate limiting; carrier credentials require encrypted secrets storage

frontend_stack.value: none - API service only; no user-facing UI
frontend_stack.rationale: api_service project class; FrontendAgent does not run for this class

backend_stack.rationale: Fastify async performance suits concurrent carrier fan-out; Node.js team expertise

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for rate_requests, carrier_credentials, and usage_metrics; Redis 7 for rate cache and token validation
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for relational usage metering and credential management; Redis for low-latency cached rate lookups

hosting_target.value: AWS Lambda + API Gateway with Fastify on Lambda adapter
hosting_target.rationale: Serverless scales to zero off-peak; API Gateway handles TLS termination and WAF; Lambda per-request billing suits bursty checkout traffic

privacy_retention_policy.value: Rate request logs retained 90 days for debugging; usage metrics retained 13 months for billing reconciliation; carrier credentials never logged
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: 90-day debug window; 13 months covers full annual billing cycle for API client invoicing

future_scope.value: FTL spot rate integration, carrier delivery guarantee insurance upsell API, webhook notifications for rate change events on saved shipment profiles
future_scope.rationale: Expand mode coverage and add value-add features post-MVP

constraints.value: Carrier API response timeout cap 2 seconds; total API response time SLA 2.5 seconds at p95; per-carrier adapter must not block fan-out on single carrier timeout
constraints.rationale: Checkout flow SLA requires sub-3-second rate fetch; single slow carrier cannot delay checkout

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch Logs and Metrics; X-Ray distributed tracing for Lambda; custom metric: carrier_api_latency_ms per carrier, cache_hit_rate, rate_request_volume per client
observability_baseline.rationale: CloudWatch/X-Ray are native to Lambda/API Gateway; custom metrics for carrier health and cache efficiency monitoring

execution_preference.value: Continuous deployment on main merge via GitHub Actions; semver releases for API version management
execution_preference.rationale: Fast iteration cadence for carrier adapter additions

project.goal: Unified carrier rate API aggregating parcel and LTL quotes with sub-2.5s SLA

security.auth: Custom JWT with client_id and scopes claims
security.secrets: AWS Secrets Manager for carrier credentials

reasoner_reviews.product: The cache key design is the highest-leverage architectural decision. Rate quotes vary by origin zip, destination zip, weight, dimensions, and service class. A cache key that is too broad returns stale rates for different shipment profiles; too narrow results in low hit rates. The nightly surcharge refresh must also invalidate cache entries affected by the updated fuel and accessorial tables.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Promise.allSettled for carrier fan-out is the correct Node.js pattern - it captures both fulfilled and rejected carrier responses without letting a single timeout reject the entire promise. The carrier adapter plugin pattern allows adding new carriers without modifying the core fan-out orchestrator. Redis sorted set per client_id for usage metering enables efficient monthly billing aggregation.
reasoner_reviews.security: Per-client JWT scopes restricting carrier account access are critical - a compromised API client token must not expose carrier credentials for accounts it was not granted. Carrier API credentials must be fetched from Secrets Manager at startup and cached in Lambda execution environment with a TTL; never embedded in code or environment variables. Rate limiting per client_id in Redis must use a sliding window to prevent burst exploitation at window boundaries.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 2.5-second p95 SLA with Lambda cold starts requires provisioned concurrency for peak checkout periods. Fan-out timeout hardcap at 2 seconds with Promise.allSettled ensures late carriers are excluded from response rather than delaying it. Cache-first design returns cached rates for repeat shipment profiles without carrier API calls, protecting the SLA during carrier API degradation.

backend.service_design: Fastify application with three route groups: /v1/rates (rate request fan-out, cache check), /v1/api-clients (client credential management, admin only), /v1/analytics (usage metrics, admin only). Carrier adapters as Fastify plugins, each exporting a getRates(shipmentProfile) async function; adapters registered at startup from active_carriers config
backend.api_patterns: REST with OpenAPI 3.1; POST /v1/rates returns array of normalized RateQuote objects sorted by total_cost_usd; carrier errors included as error objects in quotes array so partial results are returned rather than 4xx
backend.business_modules: Fan-out orchestrator using Promise.allSettled with 2s per-carrier timeout race; cache key generator hashing (origin_zip, dest_zip, weight_oz, length_in, width_in, height_in, service_class, carrier_account_id); response normalizer mapping carrier-specific rate structures to canonical RateQuote schema
backend.scaling_notes: Lambda provisioned concurrency 10 reserved for peak periods; ElastiCache Redis r7g.medium for rate cache; RDS PostgreSQL t3.medium for usage data; no VPC for Lambda to avoid cold start penalty (carrier API calls are external, Secrets Manager accessed via VPC endpoint)

security.auth_design: Custom JWT signed with RS256 keypair; claims: client_id, scopes (array of allowed carrier_account_ids and modes), exp; validated via jsonwebtoken library on every request; public key cached in Lambda execution environment
security.secrets_management: Carrier API credentials stored per carrier_account_id in Secrets Manager; Lambda execution role grants GetSecretValue for carrier credential paths only; credentials cached in process-level Map with 4-hour TTL and refreshed on Secrets Manager rotation event via EventBridge
security.abuse_prevention: Per-client sliding window rate limit in Redis: 1000 requests/minute per client_id; requests exceeding limit return 429 with Retry-After header; IP-level rate limit at API Gateway WAF for unauthenticated requests

data.entities: rate_requests (id UUID PK, client_id TEXT, origin_zip TEXT, dest_zip TEXT, weight_oz INT, dims_json JSONB, service_class TEXT, carrier_quotes_json JSONB, cache_hit BOOLEAN, requested_at TIMESTAMPTZ), carrier_credentials (id UUID PK, carrier_name TEXT, account_id TEXT, secret_ref TEXT, active BOOLEAN, modes TEXT[]), carrier_surcharges (id UUID PK, carrier_name TEXT, surcharge_type TEXT, amount_usd NUMERIC, effective_date DATE, refreshed_at TIMESTAMPTZ), usage_metrics (id UUID PK, client_id TEXT, month_bucket DATE, request_count INT, carrier_calls_count INT, cache_hit_count INT, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ), api_clients (id UUID PK, client_id TEXT UNIQUE, name TEXT, allowed_carrier_account_ids TEXT[], allowed_modes TEXT[], active BOOLEAN, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ)
data.schema_notes: rate_requests.carrier_quotes_json stores the full normalized quote array for audit and debugging; usage_metrics updated via PostgreSQL INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE for idempotent metering increments
data.retention_and_deletion: rate_requests retained 90 days then deleted by scheduled Lambda; usage_metrics retained 13 months then archived to S3 Parquet for billing history

devops.deployment_topology: Lambda function (arm64, 512MB, 30s max timeout) with API Gateway HTTP API (not REST API to avoid cold start overhead); ElastiCache Redis in private subnet; RDS PostgreSQL in private subnet; Lambda in same VPC with NAT Gateway for carrier API egress
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ESLint, jest unit tests 80% coverage gate, Lambda bundle build via esbuild, SAM deploy to staging on main merge, SAM deploy to production on semver tag; smoke test Lambda invocation post-deploy
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch Logs structured JSON with client_id and carrier correlation; X-Ray active tracing with carrier adapter subsegments; custom CloudWatch metrics: carrier_api_latency_ms (by carrier_name), cache_hit_rate, rate_request_volume_per_client; CloudWatch Alarm on carrier_api_error_rate > 10% triggering SNS alert

ISS-001.title: Cache key does not include carrier_account_id causing cross-client rate leakage
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 cache key only used shipment dimensions; two clients with different carrier account pricing (e.g., negotiated vs standard rates) would receive each other's cached rates

history.note: Cache key hash excluded carrier_account_id

ISS-002.title: Nightly surcharge refresh does not invalidate affected cache entries
ISS-002.detail: Carrier fuel surcharge and accessorial tables updated nightly but cached rate quotes using old surcharge values remain in Redis until natural TTL expiry, causing stale rates up to 24 hours after refresh

history.note: Nightly refresh job mentioned but no cache invalidation logic

ISS-003.title: Lambda VPC placement cold start impact not addressed for checkout SLA
ISS-003.detail: Placing Lambda in VPC for RDS/Redis private subnet access introduces 300-500ms ENI attachment cold start; with 2.5s p95 SLA this is a significant risk during low-traffic periods when provisioned concurrency scales down

history.note: VPC Lambda described without cold start mitigation

focus_issues.title: Nightly surcharge refresh does not invalidate affected cache entries

focus_issues.title: Lambda VPC cold start impact on checkout SLA

Document provisioned concurrency strategy: minimum 5 provisioned instances during business hours, scale to 0 overnight; EventBridge schedule to pre-warm before checkout peak

previous_plan.title: Carrier Rate Aggregation API v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Fastify on AWS Lambda + API Gateway aggregating parcel and LTL carrier rates via concurrent fan-out, Redis caching, per-client JWT scoping, and nightly surcharge refresh. PostgreSQL for usage metering.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: Lambda in VPC for RDS/Redis private subnet access; API Gateway HTTP API; GitHub Actions SAM deploy. No provisioned concurrency strategy documented.

best_plan.title: Carrier Rate Aggregation API v1
best_plan.executive_summary: Fastify on AWS Lambda + API Gateway aggregating parcel and LTL carrier rates via concurrent fan-out, Redis caching, per-client JWT scoping, and nightly surcharge refresh. PostgreSQL for usage metering.
best_plan.deployment_and_operations: Lambda in VPC; API Gateway; GitHub Actions SAM deploy. No provisioned concurrency or cold start mitigation.
best_plan.workflows: Cache-first rate request; carrier fan-out on cache miss; nightly surcharge refresh without cache invalidation.

target_output.thinking_summary: Two fixes needed: ISS-002 requires the nightly surcharge refresh Lambda to scan Redis for keys matching updated carrier names and delete them, ensuring freshly computed rates reflect new fuel surcharges within 5 minutes of the nightly update rather than up to 24 hours; ISS-003 requires a provisioned concurrency strategy using an EventBridge schedule to maintain minimum 5 warm Lambda instances during business hours (07:00-22:00 UTC) and 2 overnight, protecting the 2.5s p95 checkout SLA against cold start ENI attachment delays. Both fixes are operational additions; the core rate fan-out architecture from round 1 remains sound.

fix_report.action_taken: Added Redis cache invalidation to nightly surcharge refresh Lambda: after updating carrier_surcharges table, Lambda scans Redis keys matching pattern 'rates:{carrier_name}:*' for each updated carrier using SCAN with MATCH and deletes affected keys in batches; cache invalidation completes within 5 minutes of surcharge table update
fix_report.expected_outcome: Cached rate quotes reflect updated carrier fuel surcharges and accessorial fees within 5 minutes of nightly refresh; checkout pricing accuracy restored to T+5min window after surcharge change

fix_report.action_taken: Added Lambda provisioned concurrency strategy: EventBridge scheduled rule fires at 06:55 UTC daily to set provisioned_concurrency=10 on rate-aggregation Lambda alias LIVE; second rule fires at 22:05 UTC to reduce to 2; Application Auto Scaling policy scales provisioned concurrency on rate_request_volume CloudWatch metric exceeding 500/min threshold; documented in deployment_and_operations
fix_report.expected_outcome: Cold start ENI attachment latency eliminated for 95th percentile of checkout requests during business hours; provisioned concurrency scaled down overnight to reduce cost

target_output.title: Carrier Rate Aggregation API v2
target_output.executive_summary: A public REST API service built on AWS Lambda and API Gateway that aggregates shipping rate quotes and transit time estimates from parcel and LTL carriers through concurrent fan-out to per-carrier adapters, with Redis caching keyed by shipment profile and carrier account. This round resolves stale rate caching after nightly surcharge refreshes and documents the provisioned concurrency strategy protecting the 2.5-second p95 checkout SLA against Lambda cold start delays. API clients authenticate with scoped JWTs limiting access to their carrier accounts; usage is metered per client for monthly billing.
target_output.architecture_overview: Fastify application deployed on Lambda (arm64) behind API Gateway HTTP API with WAF. Rate requests enter POST /v1/rates; the handler checks Redis for a cached RateQuote array keyed by SHA-256 hash of (origin_zip, dest_zip, weight_oz, dims, service_class, carrier_account_ids from JWT scope). On cache miss, the fan-out orchestrator dispatches Promise.allSettled calls to registered carrier adapter plugins with a 2-second per-adapter timeout race; fulfilled adapter responses are normalized to canonical RateQuote objects; rejected adapters contribute error objects in the quotes array. Results are cached in Redis with a 4-hour TTL and returned sorted by total_cost_usd. Nightly EventBridge Lambda refreshes carrier surcharge tables in PostgreSQL and immediately invalidates Redis keys for updated carriers using SCAN+DEL. Lambda provisioned concurrency of 10 active instances maintained during business hours via EventBridge schedule to eliminate ENI cold start latency.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Node.js 20 on Lambda arm64. Framework: Fastify 4 with fastify-aws-lambda adapter. Carrier adapters: per-carrier npm plugins registered at startup. Auth: jsonwebtoken for RS256 JWT validation. Cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 (r7g.medium) via VPC private subnet. Database: RDS PostgreSQL 16 (t3.medium) via VPC private subnet. Infrastructure: API Gateway HTTP API, Lambda with Application Auto Scaling provisioned concurrency, EventBridge for nightly refresh and pre-warm schedule, Secrets Manager for carrier credentials. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with SAM CLI deploy. Observability: CloudWatch Logs structured JSON, X-Ray active tracing.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Rate Aggregation Endpoint: JWT scope validation extracts allowed carrier_account_ids and modes; cache key computed from shipment profile plus carrier_account_ids; on cache miss, fan-out to carrier adapters with 2s timeout; partial results returned when some carriers timeout. Cache Invalidation: nightly surcharge Lambda scans Redis keys by carrier pattern and deletes affected entries within 5 minutes of PostgreSQL surcharge table update. Carrier Surcharge Refresh: nightly EventBridge Lambda fetches updated fuel indexes and accessorial tables from each carrier's rate API, upserts carrier_surcharges records, triggers cache invalidation per updated carrier. API Client Management: admin POST /v1/api-clients to create client with allowed carrier accounts and modes; JWT issued via client credentials endpoint. Usage Metering: every rate request increments usage_metrics counter for client_id via PostgreSQL INSERT ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE; monthly billing aggregation query.
target_output.system_components: API Gateway HTTP API (TLS 1.3 termination, WAF with rate-limiting rules) routing to Lambda function alias LIVE. Lambda function (Node.js 20, arm64, 512MB, 30s max) with Application Auto Scaling managing provisioned concurrency (2-10 based on schedule and metric). ElastiCache Redis r7g.medium in private subnet for rate cache and sliding window rate limit counters. RDS PostgreSQL 16 t3.medium in private subnet for rate_requests audit, carrier_credentials references, carrier_surcharges, usage_metrics, api_clients. Secrets Manager storing carrier API credentials per carrier_account_id. EventBridge rule for nightly surcharge refresh Lambda (01:00 UTC) and provisioned concurrency pre-warm schedule (06:55 UTC daily).
target_output.workflows: Inbound Rate Request: API Gateway validates JWT signature using cached public key, extracts client_id and carrier_account_ids scope claim; Fastify handler computes SHA-256 cache key from shipment profile + sorted carrier_account_ids; Redis GET on key; on cache hit return cached quotes array (cache_hit=true logged); on cache miss, build carrier adapter invocation list filtered by client JWT scope, execute Promise.allSettled with 2s per-adapter AbortController timeout, normalize fulfilled responses to RateQuote{carrier_name, service_class, total_cost_usd, currency, transit_days_min, transit_days_max, surcharges_json}, cache result in Redis with 4-hour TTL, insert rate_request audit record to PostgreSQL async (non-blocking), return sorted quotes. Nightly Surcharge Refresh: EventBridge triggers refresh Lambda at 01:00 UTC; Lambda calls each carrier's surcharge API endpoint, upserts carrier_surcharges rows, then for each updated carrier name scans Redis with SCAN 0 MATCH 'rates:{carrier_name}:*' COUNT 100 loop until cursor=0, collecting matching keys, then DEL in batches of 100; logs invalidation count per carrier to CloudWatch. Provisioned Concurrency Management: EventBridge rule at 06:55 UTC calls Lambda UpdateProvisionedConcurrencyConfig to set provisioned_concurrency=10 on alias LIVE; rule at 22:05 UTC reduces to 2; Application Auto Scaling policy scales up to 20 when rate_request_volume metric exceeds 500 requests/minute for 2 consecutive periods.
target_output.data_model: rate_requests (id UUID PK, client_id TEXT, origin_zip TEXT, dest_zip TEXT, weight_oz INT, dims_json JSONB, service_class TEXT, carrier_account_ids TEXT[], quotes_json JSONB, cache_hit BOOLEAN, requested_at TIMESTAMPTZ). carrier_credentials (id UUID PK, carrier_name TEXT, account_id TEXT UNIQUE, secret_arn TEXT, active BOOLEAN, modes TEXT[]). carrier_surcharges (id UUID PK, carrier_name TEXT, surcharge_type TEXT ENUM fuel/residential/delivery_area/liftgate, amount_usd NUMERIC(10,4), effective_date DATE, refreshed_at TIMESTAMPTZ; UNIQUE(carrier_name, surcharge_type, effective_date)). usage_metrics (id UUID PK, client_id TEXT, month_bucket DATE, request_count INT DEFAULT 0, carrier_calls_count INT DEFAULT 0, cache_hit_count INT DEFAULT 0, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ; UNIQUE(client_id, month_bucket)). api_clients (id UUID PK, client_id TEXT UNIQUE, name TEXT, allowed_carrier_account_ids TEXT[], allowed_modes TEXT[], active BOOLEAN, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: POST /v1/rates - body {origin_zip, dest_zip, weight_oz, length_in, width_in, height_in, service_class ENUM parcel/ltl/ftl} - returns {quotes: [{carrier_name, service_class, total_cost_usd, currency, transit_days_min, transit_days_max, surcharges}], cache_hit: bool, request_id}. POST /v1/rates/transit-estimate - returns transit days range without rate amounts (lower latency for pre-checkout display). GET /v1/usage?month=YYYY-MM - returns usage metrics for authenticated client_id. POST /v1/auth/token - client credentials flow returning signed RS256 JWT. Admin (admin scope only): POST /v1/api-clients, GET /v1/api-clients, POST /v1/carrier-credentials. All endpoints require JWT; admin endpoints validate admin scope claim.
target_output.security_and_compliance: JWT RS256 signed tokens with client_id, scopes (carrier_account_ids array, modes array), and exp claims; Fastify preHandler validates signature using public key cached in Lambda execution environment; scope enforcement filters carrier adapters to allowed carrier_account_ids per request. Carrier API credentials stored in Secrets Manager as JSON per carrier_account_id; Lambda execution role grants GetSecretValue for paths matching 'carrier-creds/*' only; credentials cached in process Map with 4-hour TTL refreshed on rotation notification. Sliding window rate limit per client_id in Redis using sorted set: ZADD {client_id}:ratelimit {ts} {uuid}; ZREMRANGEBYSCORE removing entries older than 60 seconds; ZCARD check against 1000 limit per minute; 429 returned with Retry-After header on breach. API Gateway WAF: AWS managed rule group for common threats; custom rule limiting unauthenticated requests from single IP to 10/minute. Carrier credentials never appear in CloudWatch logs; rate request logs exclude raw quote payloads to prevent carrier pricing data leakage.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Lambda function deployed via SAM with arm64 architecture, 512MB memory, 30-second max timeout, VPC config with private subnets and security group allowing outbound 443 to carrier APIs via NAT Gateway. API Gateway HTTP API stage prod with default throttling 10,000 requests/second. Provisioned concurrency: Lambda alias LIVE with Application Auto Scaling; EventBridge scheduled rule at 06:55 UTC sets provisioned_concurrency=10; rule at 22:05 UTC sets to 2; auto scaling policy on rate_request_volume metric scales up to 20 on sustained high volume (ISS-003 fix). GitHub Actions CI: ESLint, Jest unit tests with mocked carrier adapters (80% coverage gate), esbuild Lambda bundle, SAM deploy to staging on main merge with smoke test, SAM deploy to production on semver tag. RDS PostgreSQL and ElastiCache Redis in private subnets; Lambda accesses via VPC. Secrets Manager rotation for carrier credentials triggers Lambda rotation function and in-process cache invalidation via EventBridge notification.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch Logs with structured JSON including client_id, request_id, carrier_name, cache_hit, and total_latency_ms on every rate request. X-Ray active tracing with carrier adapter subsegments showing per-carrier API call latency and timeout events. Custom CloudWatch metrics: carrier_api_latency_ms (histogram by carrier_name), cache_hit_rate (daily), rate_request_volume (per client_id), carrier_timeout_rate (by carrier_name), surcharge_cache_invalidation_count (nightly). CloudWatch Alarms: carrier_timeout_rate > 20% for any carrier over 15 minutes triggers SNS alert for carrier API health degradation; cache_hit_rate < 40% over 1 hour triggers investigation alert; Lambda provisioned_concurrency_spillover > 0 triggers scale-up review.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Lambda at 10 provisioned concurrency instances during business hours ~$120/month; ElastiCache Redis r7g.medium ~$100/month; RDS PostgreSQL t3.medium ~$60/month; API Gateway HTTP API ~$15/month at 1M requests/month; Secrets Manager ~$10/month; total ~$305/month at 1M requests/month. Provisioned concurrency reduced to 2 overnight saves ~$80/month versus 24-hour provisioning. Cache-first design reduces carrier API calls by estimated 60% at steady state, reducing both latency and carrier API rate limit consumption. Usage metrics enable per-client billing recovery covering infrastructure costs at scale.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Core rate fan-out with Promise.allSettled, parcel and LTL carrier adapters for top 5 carriers, Redis cache with carrier_account_id-scoped keys (ISS-001 resolved), JWT auth with scope enforcement, nightly surcharge refresh with Redis cache invalidation (ISS-002), provisioned concurrency EventBridge schedule (ISS-003), usage metering, SAM CI/CD pipeline. Phase 2 (6 weeks): FTL spot rate adapter, transit-estimate low-latency endpoint, API client self-service portal, usage analytics dashboard for ops, carrier adapter health monitoring dashboard. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Webhook notifications for rate change events on saved shipment profiles, delivery guarantee insurance upsell API integration, multi-region active-passive for 99.9% SLA.
target_output.development_guidelines: Carrier adapters implemented as Fastify plugins with standardized getRates(profile, credentials, timeout) async interface; adapters co-located in /src/adapters/{carrier_name}/. Jest unit tests mock carrier adapter responses to test fan-out orchestrator logic without live carrier API calls; integration tests in staging with carrier sandbox credentials. ESLint with @typescript-eslint/no-floating-promises to catch unhandled promise rejections in fan-out code. Cache key hashing function unit-tested with known input/output pairs to prevent inadvertent key collision after code changes. Carrier credential access must go through the credentials service module only; direct Secrets Manager calls from adapters are a linting error.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: carrier API schema changes break adapter normalization without detection until customer reports wrong rates. Mitigation: adapter response validation using Zod schemas with strict parsing; validation failures increment carrier_adapter_error metric and trigger alert before affecting customers. Risk: Redis cache size grows unbounded at high request volume with diverse shipment profiles. Mitigation: Redis maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru evicts least recently used keys; cache key TTL 4 hours bounds absolute size growth. Tradeoff: Lambda serverless over ECS Fargate reduces operational overhead but requires provisioned concurrency management to meet checkout SLA; trade-off accepted given variable traffic pattern with overnight near-zero volume.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Lambda over ECS Fargate chosen for serverless scaling to near-zero overnight reducing cost; provisioned concurrency mitigates cold start risk for business hours SLA. Redis SCAN-based cache invalidation over Redis Pub/Sub for surcharge refresh chosen for simplicity; SCAN with MATCH pattern is safe with Redis SCAN cursor-based iteration. PostgreSQL INSERT ON CONFLICT for usage metering over a separate counter service chosen to minimize operational components.

metadata.notes: Carrier rate aggregation API; round 2 resolves surcharge cache invalidation (ISS-002) and Lambda cold start SLA protection (ISS-003); moderate quality with solid core but some thinner sections.

profile.domain: Logistics Observability Tooling

project_goal.value: Go SDK standardizing OpenTelemetry traces, structured logs, and custom metric registration across a logistics microservices fleet with enforced naming conventions and cardinality limits
project_goal.rationale: Eliminates per-service observability drift and prevents high-cardinality metric explosions

target_users.value: Internal Go microservice teams at the logistics company instrumenting new and existing services
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers are backend engineers across 12 microservice teams

project_class.rationale: Distributed as an internal Go module, not a hosted service

capabilities.rationale: Pure library providing instrumentation hooks; no frontend or hosted runtime

complexity_level.rationale: OpenTelemetry wiring plus cardinality enforcement logic is non-trivial but scope-bounded

risk_level.rationale: No user data handled; misuse causes metric noise but no security exposure

data_sensitivity.rationale: Emits only service operational telemetry, no PII or financial data

external_exposure.rationale: Published to internal Go module proxy, not public registries

access_model.value: Internal module proxy authentication via GOPROXY with Artifactory credentials
access_model.rationale: Standard internal module distribution mechanism

feature_scope.value: OpenTelemetry HTTP and gRPC context propagation wrappers, structured log emitter with mandatory field enforcement, metric registration with cardinality validation, trace ID header naming enforcement, environment-aware SDK initialization
feature_scope.rationale: Covers the three telemetry pillars with company-specific guardrails

mvp_scope.value: Trace context propagation for HTTP and gRPC, structured JSON log emitter, metric registration with cardinality limit enforcement
mvp_scope.rationale: Core instrumentation coverage needed before rolling out to initial pilot services

security_baseline.value: No secrets in SDK; Artifactory token auth for module distribution; signed module checksums via GONOSUMCHECK
security_baseline.rationale: Library has no runtime auth surface; distribution security is the concern

frontend_stack.rationale: Library SDK has no frontend component

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with OpenTelemetry Go SDK 1.x and Datadog exporter
backend_stack.rationale: Matches fleet runtime; OTEL Go SDK is the standard instrumentation layer

data_platform.value: none
data_platform.rationale: SDK emits telemetry to the OTel Collector; no embedded storage

hosting_target.value: Internal Artifactory Go module proxy at go.internal.logistics.io
hosting_target.rationale: Standard internal module distribution path

privacy_retention_policy.value: No personal data processed; telemetry data retention governed by Datadog account settings (13-month metrics, 15-day traces)
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SDK itself has no retention responsibility

future_scope.value: Automatic baggage propagation for fleet request IDs, sampling rate configuration per service tier, exemplar support linking traces to metrics
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP enhancements identified in platform roadmap

constraints.value: Must not import non-OTEL third-party dependencies; API surface must be backward compatible across minor versions; cardinality limits configurable but defaulting to max 20 label values per metric
constraints.rationale: Prevents dependency conflicts in consumer services and ensures stability

observability_baseline.value: SDK emits its own initialization and cardinality violation events via the same structured log interface it provides to consumers
observability_baseline.rationale: SDK self-instrumentation allows platform team to detect adoption and misuse patterns

execution_preference.value: Iterative releases to internal module proxy with semantic versioning; breaking changes require major version bump and migration guide
execution_preference.rationale: Stability contract for consuming teams

project.goal: Provide opinionated OTel instrumentation with company naming conventions and cardinality enforcement
project.timeline: MVP in 6 weeks, GA in 10 weeks

backend.api: Package-level public API with functional options pattern

security.auth: Artifactory module proxy credentials

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with go test, go vet, staticcheck

reasoner_reviews.product: The SDK directly addresses the platform team's pain: each of 12 microservices currently hand-rolls its own OTel setup with inconsistent field names and unbounded cardinality. A shared SDK will unify dashboards and prevent production incidents from metric cardinality explosions.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Functional options pattern for SDK initialization is the right call for Go; it allows future extension without breaking callers. Cardinality enforcement at registration time rather than emit time is architecturally correct because it fails fast before any production traffic surfaces the issue. The OTel propagators.TextMapPropagator interface should be wrapped, not replaced, to stay compatible with the OTel ecosystem.
reasoner_reviews.security: Distribution via Artifactory with module checksum verification is sufficient. The SDK should explicitly not provide any mechanism to disable cardinality enforcement at runtime, as that creates a safety valve that will inevitably be misused under production pressure.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The no-third-party-dependency constraint is strict but correct for a shared library. Using only the OTel SDK and Go standard library keeps the dependency graph clean and avoids transitive conflicts in diverse consumer codebases.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The cardinality limit of 20 label values is quite conservative for some use cases like per-carrier metrics. The SDK should support per-metric limit overrides via registration options, with the 20-value default applying only when no override is specified.

backend.service_design: Single Go module at go.internal.logistics.io/obsdk with packages: obsdk (init), obsdk/otrace (trace wrappers), obsdk/olog (structured log emitter), obsdk/ometric (metric registration with cardinality guard)
backend.api_patterns: Functional options for Init(ctx, ServiceName, opts...), NewLogger(opts...), RegisterCounter(name, desc, labelKeys, opts...). All public types implement standard interfaces for testability.
backend.business_modules: Trace: HTTP middleware injecting W3C traceparent headers with company-standard x-logistics-trace-id alias. gRPC: UnaryInterceptor and StreamInterceptor forwarding OTel context. Log: zerolog-compatible structured emitter enforcing required fields (service, env, trace_id, level). Metric: wrapper over otel/sdk/metric enforcing naming pattern logistics_{service}_{metric}_{unit} and rejecting registrations exceeding cardinality limit.
backend.failure_handling: CardinalityExceededError returned synchronously on metric registration. InitError wraps OTel exporter connection failures. All errors are typed for programmatic handling by consumers.
backend.backend_risks: OTel SDK breaking changes between minor versions require SDK maintainer to pin exact OTel SDK version and coordinate upgrades. Consumer services pinning different OTel versions may conflict; document minimum compatible version in README.

security.auth_design: No auth surface in library code; Artifactory proxy authentication handled by consumer GOENV configuration
security.secrets_management: No secrets in SDK; exporter endpoint configured via OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT env var following OTel spec
security.security_risks: A consumer could configure the exporter to point outside the internal OTel Collector; document that OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT should be set from trusted config management

data.entities: trace_spans (OTel Span structs), structured_log_entries (zerolog-compatible JSON events with mandatory fields: service, env, trace_id, level, msg, ts), custom_metrics (OTel instruments: Counter, Gauge, Histogram), cardinality_rules (per-metric max label value counts), naming_conventions (logistics_{service}_{metric}_{unit} pattern enforcement)
data.schema_notes: No persistent storage; all state is in-process OTel SDK meter/tracer providers instantiated at service startup
data.data_risks: In-process OTel provider holds metric state; services with high-frequency label value churn can still exhaust memory up to the cardinality limit before the next GC cycle

devops.deployment_topology: Go module published to Artifactory; consumers add require go.internal.logistics.io/obsdk vX.Y.Z to their go.mod
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go test ./... with race detector, go vet, staticcheck, golangci-lint. Semver tags trigger Artifactory publish via goreleaser.
devops.observability_stack: SDK's own initialization events use its olog package, emitting to stdout in CI and to the configured exporter in production
devops.devops_risks: Artifactory outage blocks all consumer builds; document GONOSUMCHECK and direct VCS fallback for DR scenarios

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing an internal Go SDK for observability standardization. Key decisions: cardinality enforcement at registration time (fail-fast), functional options API for forward compatibility, single module with sub-packages per telemetry pillar, no non-OTel dependencies to avoid transitive conflicts across 12 consumer services. Distribution via internal Artifactory Go module proxy with semver stability guarantees.
target_output.title: Logistics Observability SDK Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: An internal Go module providing opinionated wrappers around the OpenTelemetry Go SDK to standardize trace context propagation, structured log emission, and custom metric registration across all logistics microservices. The SDK enforces the company's logistics_{service}_{metric}_{unit} naming convention and rejects metric registrations exceeding configurable cardinality limits at initialization time, preventing production metric explosions before they occur.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single Go module at go.internal.logistics.io/obsdk organized into three sub-packages: obsdk/otrace wraps OTel HTTP and gRPC interceptors with the company's x-logistics-trace-id header alias alongside the standard W3C traceparent. obsdk/olog provides a zerolog-compatible structured log emitter that enforces mandatory fields (service, env, trace_id, level) and rejects log calls missing required context. obsdk/ometric wraps the OTel metric SDK's meter provider with a registration guard that validates naming patterns and enforces per-metric label cardinality limits, returning a typed CardinalityExceededError at registration rather than at emit time. All three packages share a single Init() entry point using functional options for configuration.
target_output.technology_stack: Go 1.22. go.opentelemetry.io/otel v1.x and go.opentelemetry.io/otel/exporters/otlp/otlptrace and otlpmetric packages. go.opentelemetry.io/otel/sdk/trace and sdk/metric for provider setup. go.opentelemetry.io/otel/propagation for W3C context propagation. rs/zerolog for structured log emission (only permitted non-OTel dependency, evaluated for removal in v2). goreleaser for versioned module publishing to Artifactory.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Init(ctx, ServiceName, opts...): initializes trace provider with OTLP exporter, meter provider with cardinality guard, and zerolog logger with mandatory field enforcement. HTTP Middleware: obsdk/otrace.HTTPMiddleware injects and extracts W3C traceparent plus x-logistics-trace-id. gRPC Interceptors: UnaryServerInterceptor and StreamServerInterceptor for gRPC trace propagation. RegisterCounter/RegisterGauge/RegisterHistogram: validates naming pattern logistics_{service}_{metric}_{unit} and rejects registrations exceeding cardinality limit. Structured Logger: enforces service, env, trace_id, level fields on every log call.
target_output.system_components: obsdk package: SDK initialization, OTel provider bootstrapping, functional options. obsdk/otrace: HTTP middleware, gRPC unary/stream interceptors, trace ID alias injection. obsdk/olog: zerolog wrapper with mandatory field schema enforcement, context-aware logger extraction. obsdk/ometric: OTel meter provider wrapper, naming convention validator, cardinality registry tracking registered label value sets per metric, CardinalityExceededError type. Internal naming_validator: regex enforcing logistics_{service}_{metric}_{unit} pattern at registration time.
target_output.workflows: Service Initialization: Service calls obsdk.Init(ctx, 'shipment-tracker', obsdk.WithEnv('production'), obsdk.WithMaxCardinality(30)). SDK bootstraps OTLP trace and metric exporters pointed at OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT, registers global propagators, and returns an Obs struct with Logger and Meter references. Metric Registration: Service calls obs.Meter.RegisterCounter('logistics_shipment_events_total', desc, []string{'status','carrier'}). The cardinality guard checks that 'status' and 'carrier' together will not exceed 30 combinations; if the service has already registered 15 counters with these labels, it returns CardinalityExceededError before any data flows. gRPC Trace Propagation: The gRPC UnaryServerInterceptor extracts W3C traceparent from incoming metadata, creates a child span, injects x-logistics-trace-id into the context, and calls the handler. On response, the span is closed and OTLP-exported to the Collector.
target_output.data_model: trace_spans (OTel Span: trace_id string, span_id string, parent_span_id string, service_name string, operation_name string, start_time time.Time, end_time time.Time, attributes map[string]string, status OTel StatusCode). structured_log_entries (zerolog JSON: service string required, env string required, trace_id string required from context, level string required, msg string, ts time.Time, additional fields map[string]interface{}). custom_metrics (OTel instruments keyed by name logistics_{service}_{metric}_{unit}: instrument_type enum counter/gauge/histogram, label_keys []string, cardinality_limit int, registered_at time.Time). cardinality_rules (per metric: metric_name string, max_combinations int, observed_combinations int tracked in-process). naming_conventions (pattern regex: ^logistics_[a-z][a-z0-9_]*_[a-z][a-z0-9_]*_[a-z][a-z0-9_]*$).
target_output.api_design: Package-level API, no HTTP endpoints. obsdk.Init(ctx context.Context, serviceName string, opts ...Option) (*Obs, error). obsdk.WithEnv(env string) Option. obsdk.WithMaxCardinality(n int) Option. obsdk.WithExporterEndpoint(endpoint string) Option. (*Obs).Meter.RegisterCounter(name, description string, labelKeys []string, opts ...MetricOption) (metric.Counter, error). (*Obs).Logger.With(fields map[string]interface{}) *Logger. obsdk/otrace.HTTPMiddleware(obs *Obs) func(http.Handler) http.Handler. obsdk/otrace.UnaryServerInterceptor(obs *Obs) grpc.UnaryServerInterceptor.
target_output.security_and_compliance: No auth surface in library code. Distribution security: Artifactory module proxy requires GONOSUMDB=go.internal.logistics.io and standard Go module checksum verification. The SDK explicitly rejects any configuration that would disable cardinality enforcement (no WithUnlimitedCardinality option). OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT is the only external network destination; services should set this from Vault-managed config rather than hardcoding. The olog package rejects log.Fatal and log.Panic calls (no public API for them) to prevent SDK consumers from crashing services via logging.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI runs on every PR: go test ./... -race, go vet ./..., staticcheck ./..., golangci-lint run. Semver tags (v1.x.x) trigger a goreleaser workflow that validates the module, runs the full test suite, and publishes to the Artifactory Go module proxy at go.internal.logistics.io/obsdk. Consumers add the module to go.mod and GOPROXY=https://artifactory.internal/api/go/go-local,direct. Breaking changes require a major version bump (go.internal.logistics.io/obsdk/v2) and a migration guide published to the internal developer portal. No staging environment needed; pre-release versions use v1.x.x-rc.N tags for pilot service testing.
target_output.observability: The SDK self-instruments using its own olog package: emits a structured log event on successful initialization listing service name, configured cardinality limit, and exporter endpoint. Emits a WARN-level log on each CardinalityExceededError with the metric name and attempted label count. CI test coverage report published to GitHub Actions summary. Consumer adoption tracked by scraping Artifactory download counts per version tag weekly.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No cloud runtime cost; module is built and tested in GitHub Actions (included in team plan). Artifactory storage for Go module artifacts is negligible (< 10 MB per version). The cardinality enforcement adds O(registered_metrics * max_label_combinations) memory overhead per service process, bounded by the configured limit.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): obsdk/otrace HTTP middleware and gRPC interceptors, obsdk/olog mandatory field enforcement, obsdk/ometric counter and histogram registration with cardinality guard, Init() with functional options, GitHub Actions CI, Artifactory publish pipeline. Pilot with shipment-tracker and carrier-rate microservices. Phase 2 GA (4 weeks): Gauge support, per-metric cardinality limit overrides via MetricOption, exemplar support linking trace IDs to histogram samples, migration guide from legacy per-service OTel setup. Phase 3 (post-GA): Automatic W3C baggage propagation for fleet request IDs, sampling rate configuration per service environment tier.
target_output.development_guidelines: All exported types and functions must have godoc comments. API must be stable across minor versions: no removal of exported symbols, no signature changes. Test every public function with table-driven tests. Use testify/assert only in test files. Naming convention validation must be unit tested with positive and negative examples covering all pattern edge cases. Run go test -race on every commit. Changelog maintained in CHANGELOG.md with semantic versioning entries.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: OTel Go SDK minor version bumps occasionally introduce breaking API changes. Mitigation: pin exact OTel SDK version in go.mod; upgrade only after testing against all pilot services. Risk: zerolog dependency creates a transitive conflict for services using a different logger. Mitigation: evaluate replacing zerolog with a standard library slog wrapper in v2. Tradeoff: cardinality enforcement at registration time means services cannot dynamically add label values at runtime. This is intentional to prevent runtime explosions but requires teams to enumerate all label values upfront in their service initialization code.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Cardinality limit defaults to 20 label value combinations per metric; per-metric overrides added to Phase 2 scope after critic review. Distribution via Artifactory selected over direct VCS import for access control. zerolog retained for v1 due to familiarity but flagged for slog migration in v2. OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT env var used per OTel spec rather than SDK-level config option.

metadata.notes: Go observability SDK with cardinality enforcement for logistics microservices fleet.

profile.domain: Parcel Carrier Data Consolidation

project_goal.value: Batch-oriented data pipeline consolidating parcel tracking events from six regional courier APIs into a single normalized data lake, replacing six always-on EC2 polling processes to reduce cloud spend
project_goal.rationale: Always-on polling EC2 instances run 24/7 for sporadic event volumes; batch MWAA approach targets 70% compute cost reduction

target_users.value: Internal logistics data engineers and analytics consumers querying the normalized tracking data lake
target_users.rationale: Pipeline output feeds internal dashboards and operational reporting, no external consumers

project_class.rationale: Pure batch data ingestion and normalization with no UI or interactive API

capabilities.rationale: Batch ingestion from six courier APIs, schema normalization, S3 Parquet storage

complexity_level.rationale: Six courier adapters with different schemas but straightforward batch polling and normalization logic

risk_level.rationale: Operational tracking data only; delayed event delivery is acceptable for batch use cases

data_sensitivity.rationale: Tracking events contain shipment IDs and status codes but no personal or financial data

external_exposure.rationale: S3 data lake accessed only by internal analytics and reporting tools via IAM roles

access_model.value: IAM role-based access to MWAA environment and S3 data lake; no human login to pipeline runtime
access_model.rationale: Standard AWS serverless pipeline access pattern

feature_scope.value: Airflow DAGs per courier polling tracking APIs, canonical event schema normalization, partitioned Parquet writes to S3, cost monitoring DAG comparing spend against pre-migration baseline, dead-letter queue for failed API calls
feature_scope.rationale: Core consolidation and cost tracking scope

mvp_scope.value: Two courier adapters (UPS and FedEx) with canonical normalization and S3 Parquet writes, cost monitoring DAG
mvp_scope.rationale: Validates architecture with two largest volume couriers before rolling out remaining four

security_baseline.value: Courier API credentials stored in AWS Secrets Manager; S3 bucket with SSE-S3 encryption; VPC endpoint for S3 access from MWAA
security_baseline.rationale: Prevents credential exposure and ensures data in transit stays within AWS network

frontend_stack.rationale: No UI; pipeline operated via Airflow web UI for monitoring only

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Airflow 2.9 on MWAA
backend_stack.rationale: MWAA eliminates Airflow cluster management overhead; Python matches team skills

data_platform.value: Amazon S3 with Parquet files partitioned by courier/year/month/day; AWS Glue Data Catalog for schema registration
data_platform.rationale: Serverless storage scales to any event volume; Glue enables Athena queries without additional infrastructure

hosting_target.value: AWS MWAA (Managed Airflow) in us-east-1
hosting_target.rationale: Managed Airflow eliminates scheduler and worker infrastructure management

privacy_retention_policy.value: Tracking event raw data retained 90 days in S3 Standard; normalized Parquet retained 2 years in S3 Intelligent-Tiering; no personal data in pipeline
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Operational analytics require 2-year horizon; raw data needed 90 days for reprocessing

future_scope.value: Near-real-time streaming path for time-sensitive tracking SLA monitoring; additional courier adapters for regional carriers
future_scope.rationale: Batch architecture is the cost-optimized baseline; streaming layer added only when SLA use cases demand it

constraints.value: Total pipeline cloud cost must be at least 60% below current EC2 polling baseline of $4200/month; courier API rate limits must not be exceeded; Airflow DAGs must complete within 4-hour window
constraints.rationale: Cost target is the primary project driver; API rate limits protect courier relationships

observability_baseline.value: Airflow task logs to CloudWatch; DAG-level success/failure metrics to CloudWatch; cost monitoring DAG publishing weekly spend reports to S3 and Slack
observability_baseline.rationale: CloudWatch is the existing ops monitoring standard; Slack report keeps FinOps stakeholders informed

execution_preference.value: Phased rollout: two couriers in MVP, one courier added per sprint until all six are live
execution_preference.rationale: Reduces risk by validating each new adapter independently

project.goal: Replace six EC2 polling jobs with cost-optimized MWAA batch pipeline consolidating to S3 Parquet data lake
project.target_users: Internal data engineers and analytics consumers
project.timeline: MVP 6 weeks, all six couriers 14 weeks

backend.api: No public API; S3 Parquet files consumed via Athena

security.auth: IAM roles
security.secrets: AWS Secrets Manager for courier API credentials
security.encryption: SSE-S3 on S3 bucket; VPC endpoint for S3 access

constraints.api_limits: Courier API rate limits per adapter config

reasoner_reviews.product: The cost reduction framing is clear and measurable: 60% below $4200/month means target is under $1680/month. MWAA plus S3 Parquet can achieve this comfortably if DAG schedules avoid unnecessary API polling during low-activity hours.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The canonical event schema design is the critical architectural decision. Each of six courier APIs returns different field names and status code vocabularies. A well-designed CourierEventNormalizer base class with per-courier subclasses will contain the adapter complexity. The Parquet partition scheme (courier/year/month/day) supports efficient Athena queries filtered by date range or courier.
reasoner_reviews.security: Courier API credentials must rotate; Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation policies per secret is non-negotiable. S3 bucket policy must deny access to any principal not in the approved IAM role list. Dead-letter Parquet files for failed normalizations must not be world-readable within the account.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 4-hour DAG window constraint is easily met for batch polling intervals of 15-60 minutes per courier. The main risk is a courier API returning 429 rate limit responses during peak hours; the adapter must implement exponential backoff and not block the entire DAG on a single courier failure.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 2 left the dead-letter queue design incomplete. The previous plan mentioned a DLQ concept but did not specify how failed normalization records are stored, retried, or alerted on. This must be resolved in round 3.

backend.service_design: Python package courier_adapters with base class CourierTrackingAdapter(abc.ABC) and subclasses UPSAdapter, FedExAdapter, OnTracAdapter, LSO_Adapter, Spee_Dee_Adapter, LaserShipAdapter. Each adapter implements fetch_events(since_ts) and normalize(raw_event) returning CanonicalTrackingEvent.
backend.api_patterns: No public API. Airflow DAG operators call adapter.fetch_events() and write normalized output to S3 via boto3. Canonical schema enforced via Pydantic CanonicalTrackingEvent model.
backend.background_jobs: Per-courier Airflow DAGs scheduled at configurable cron intervals (15-60 min depending on courier volume). Weekly cost monitoring DAG. Monthly dead-letter replay DAG.
backend.failure_handling: Failed API calls retry 3 times with exponential backoff. Records failing Pydantic validation written to s3://tracking-dlq/{courier}/failed/{date}/. Failed DAG tasks trigger CloudWatch alarm and Slack alert.
backend.backend_risks: Courier API schema changes break normalization silently if Pydantic model uses model_config with extra='ignore'. Mitigation: extra='forbid' in staging, extra='ignore' in production with a field-drift metric logged per unrecognized field.

security.auth_design: IAM execution role for MWAA workers scoped to: Secrets Manager GetSecretValue for courier credential paths, S3 PutObject on tracking-lake bucket, CloudWatch PutMetricData
security.secrets_management: One Secrets Manager secret per courier API credential pair, 90-day rotation lambda. MWAA environment variables reference secret ARNs, never plaintext credentials.
security.security_risks: Compromised IAM role for MWAA could exfiltrate all courier credentials in Secrets Manager. Scope the role to only the specific secret paths it needs via resource-level IAM conditions.

data.entities: courier_raw_events (JSON blobs stored in S3 raw zone partitioned by courier/year/month/day/hour), normalized_tracking_events (Parquet files with schema: tracking_number STRING, courier STRING, event_timestamp TIMESTAMP, event_code STRING, event_description STRING, location_city STRING, location_state STRING, location_country STRING, raw_event_ref STRING linking back to raw zone), pipeline_runs (Airflow DAG run metadata in MWAA metadata DB), courier_api_configs (Secrets Manager references and polling interval per courier), data_partitions (S3 prefix structure courier/year/month/day), cost_metrics (weekly CloudWatch custom metrics: api_calls_per_courier, s3_write_bytes, mwaa_worker_hours, estimated_cost_usd)
data.retention_and_deletion: Raw JSON: 90-day S3 lifecycle to GLACIER then DELETE. Normalized Parquet: 2-year S3 Intelligent-Tiering lifecycle. Dead-letter files: 180-day manual review window then DELETE.
data.migration_notes: Pre-migration: snapshot current EC2 polling volumes per courier for cost baseline. Post-migration: 30-day parallel run comparing event counts between old EC2 jobs and new MWAA pipeline to validate completeness before decommissioning EC2 instances.

devops.deployment_topology: MWAA environment in private subnet, VPC endpoint for S3. MWAA workers pull DAG code from S3 DAG bucket. Separate S3 buckets: tracking-raw (raw JSON), tracking-lake (normalized Parquet), tracking-dlq (failed records). Glue Data Catalog database: logistics_tracking with table per courier.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest with coverage > 80% on adapter normalization logic, validate Pydantic schema, sync DAG files to MWAA S3 bucket on merge to main.
devops.cost_controls: MWAA environment size: mw1.small for MVP (1-10 workers). CloudWatch billing alarm at $1500/month. Tag all resources with Project=tracking-pipeline for cost explorer filtering. Right-size MWAA worker count based on actual DAG parallelism after 2-week pilot.
devops.devops_risks: MWAA cold start on idle workers adds 2-3 minutes to DAG startup; schedule DAGs to maintain at least 1 warm worker during business hours.
