target_output.title: Parcel Tracking Event Consolidation Pipeline v3
target_output.executive_summary: Batch-oriented AWS pipeline replacing six always-on EC2 courier polling processes with MWAA-orchestrated DAGs writing normalized CanonicalTrackingEvent Parquet files to a partitioned S3 data lake. Six courier adapter subclasses isolate schema normalization. Failed records are durably written to a dead-letter S3 zone with a monthly replay DAG. Glue partition registration is appended to each DAG run for immediate Athena query visibility. Weekly cost monitoring DAG tracks spend against the $4200/month EC2 baseline.
target_output.architecture_overview: MWAA 2.9 on mw1.small orchestrates six per-courier DAGs scheduled between 15-60 minute intervals. Each DAG task calls the courier-specific adapter subclass to fetch tracking events since last watermark, validates and normalizes via Pydantic CanonicalTrackingEvent, writes Parquet to s3://tracking-lake/{courier}/year={y}/month={m}/day={d}/, then calls GlueCreatePartitionOperator to register the partition. Records failing Pydantic validation are written to s3://tracking-dlq/{courier}/failed/{date}/ as JSON with original payload and error message. All S3 access uses IAM execution roles attached to EC2 instance profiles for the MWAA workers (intentional flaw: MWAA uses task-level IAM roles, not EC2 instance profiles). A weekly cost monitoring DAG computes API call volumes and S3 storage costs against the pre-migration EC2 baseline.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12. Apache Airflow 2.9 on AWS MWAA mw1.small. Pydantic v2 for CanonicalTrackingEvent schema validation. boto3 for S3 writes and Secrets Manager credential retrieval. apache-airflow-providers-amazon for GlueCreatePartitionOperator. Parquet via pyarrow. AWS Glue Data Catalog for partition registration. Athena for downstream analytics. GitHub Actions for CI and DAG sync.
target_output.functional_feature_map: CourierTrackingAdapter base class with fetch_events(since_ts) and normalize(raw) abstract methods. UPSAdapter, FedExAdapter, OnTracAdapter, LSOAdapter, SpeeDeAdapter, LaserShipAdapter subclasses. CanonicalTrackingEvent Pydantic model: tracking_number, courier, event_timestamp, event_code, event_description, location_city, location_state, location_country, raw_event_ref. Dead-letter writer capturing ValidationError payloads. Weekly CostMonitoringDAG aggregating CloudWatch cost metrics. Monthly DLQReplayDAG reprocessing failed records after manual triage.
target_output.system_components: MWAA environment (mw1.small, 1-10 workers, private subnet). S3 tracking-raw bucket (raw JSON 90-day lifecycle). S3 tracking-lake bucket (normalized Parquet, Intelligent-Tiering, 2-year lifecycle). S3 tracking-dlq bucket (failed records, 180-day lifecycle). Glue Data Catalog database logistics_tracking with table per courier. AWS Secrets Manager (one secret per courier API credential pair). CloudWatch custom metrics namespace TrackingPipeline. SNS topic for DLQ alarm notifications.
target_output.workflows: Courier Polling DAG (per courier, e.g. UPS): fetch_events task calls UPSAdapter.fetch_events(since_ts=last_watermark) using credentials from Secrets Manager, returns list of raw UPS JSON events. normalize_and_write task validates each event against CanonicalTrackingEvent, writes valid records as Parquet to tracking-lake, writes ValidationError failures to tracking-dlq with JSON payload including original raw event and pydantic error details. register_partition task calls GlueCreatePartitionOperator for the current date partition on logistics_tracking.ups_events table. update_watermark task writes new high-water timestamp to MWAA Variables. DLQ Replay DAG (monthly): lists s3://tracking-dlq/{courier}/failed/ prefixes older than 30 days that have been manually triaged (tagged Reviewed=true in S3 metadata), reruns normalization, promotes successful records to tracking-lake. Cost Monitoring DAG (weekly): queries CloudWatch for MWAA worker hours, S3 write bytes, and API call counts per courier; computes estimated cost vs $4200 baseline; writes report to s3://tracking-reports/ and posts to Slack.
target_output.data_model: courier_raw_events (S3 JSON: courier STRING, fetched_at TIMESTAMP, raw_payload JSON, batch_id STRING). normalized_tracking_events Parquet schema: tracking_number STRING NOT NULL, courier STRING NOT NULL, event_timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL, event_code STRING, event_description STRING, location_city STRING, location_state STRING, location_country STRING, raw_event_ref STRING (s3 key of originating raw file), pipeline_run_id STRING. data_partitions S3 prefix: courier/year={y}/month={m}/day={d}/. courier_api_configs (Secrets Manager): secret_arn STRING, polling_interval_minutes INT, api_base_url STRING, rate_limit_per_minute INT. cost_metrics CloudWatch: api_calls_per_courier COUNTER, s3_write_bytes COUNTER, mwaa_worker_hours GAUGE, estimated_cost_usd GAUGE, dlq_write_count COUNTER per DAG run.
target_output.api_design: No public HTTP API. Internal interfaces: CourierTrackingAdapter.fetch_events(since_ts: datetime) -> list[dict]. CourierTrackingAdapter.normalize(raw: dict) -> CanonicalTrackingEvent. write_parquet_partition(events: list[CanonicalTrackingEvent], s3_prefix: str) -> str (returns S3 key). write_dlq_record(raw: dict, error: ValidationError, courier: str, date: str) -> None. Athena SQL for downstream consumers: SELECT tracking_number, event_timestamp, event_code FROM logistics_tracking.fedex_events WHERE year='2026' AND month='03' AND courier='fedex'.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Courier API credentials stored in Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation Lambda. S3 bucket policies deny PutObject from any principal outside the MWAA task execution role ARN. S3 SSE-S3 encryption on all three buckets. VPC endpoint for S3 ensures no data traverses public internet. MWAA workers access S3 via IAM execution roles attached to EC2 instance profiles (note: this is architecturally incorrect for MWAA which uses task-level IAM roles via mwaa:CreateWebLoginToken scoping, not EC2 instance profiles; this inconsistency would be caught by a plan auditor). CloudWatch alarms on DLQ write count > 50 per run, courier API error rate > 10% per run.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI on PR: pytest --cov=courier_adapters with 80% coverage gate on normalization logic, pydantic schema validation tests for all six courier event formats. On merge to main: sync DAG Python files to s3://mwaa-dags-{env}/ bucket via aws s3 sync. MWAA automatically picks up updated DAGs within 30 seconds of S3 sync. Infrastructure provisioned via Terraform: MWAA environment, S3 buckets with lifecycle policies, Glue database and table schemas, Secrets Manager secrets, CloudWatch alarms, SNS topic. Post-deployment: 30-day parallel run with EC2 jobs measuring daily event count deltas per courier; decommission EC2 jobs when delta < 0.1% for 7 consecutive days.
target_output.observability: Airflow task logs streamed to CloudWatch Logs group /aws/mwaa/tracking-pipeline. CloudWatch dashboard: DAG success rate per courier, task duration p95 per courier, DLQ write count per run, S3 write latency. Custom metric TrackingPipeline/DLQWriteCount triggers SNS alarm to on-call when exceeding 50 per DAG run. Weekly cost report compares api_calls_per_courier, mwaa_worker_hours, and estimated_cost_usd against $4200/month EC2 baseline, publishing delta to Slack #data-platform channel.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Target: under $1680/month (60% below $4200 baseline). MWAA mw1.small: ~$300/month. S3 storage and requests: ~$150/month at current event volumes. MWAA auto-scales workers 1-10 based on DAG queue depth. S3 Intelligent-Tiering for normalized Parquet moves data to lower-cost tiers after 30 days of inactivity. CloudWatch billing alarm at $1500/month for early warning before breaching target.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): UPS and FedEx adapters, CanonicalTrackingEvent schema, S3 Parquet writes, Glue partition registration, DLQ with CloudWatch alarm, GitHub Actions CI, Terraform infra. 30-day parallel run begins. Phase 2 (4 weeks per courier): OnTrac, LSO, Spee-Dee, LaserShip adapters added one per sprint. Each adapter validated with 2-week parallel run before EC2 counterpart is decommissioned. Phase 3 (post-GA): Monthly DLQ replay DAG, cost optimization review after 6 months of production data.
target_output.development_guidelines: Each courier adapter must have unit tests covering: successful fetch and normalize, API 429 retry backoff, Pydantic ValidationError DLQ write. Use pytest fixtures for mock courier API responses. All Pydantic models use model_config with extra='ignore' in production, extra='forbid' in test to catch schema drift. DAG dependencies defined via explicit task dependencies not implicit imports. No hardcoded courier API URLs; all configuration from Airflow Variables or Secrets Manager.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Courier API schema change breaks normalization silently. Mitigation: log unrecognized fields as CloudWatch metric field_drift_count per courier; alert when non-zero. Risk: MWAA cold start on idle workers adds 2-3 minutes to infrequent DAG runs. Mitigation: schedule a lightweight heartbeat DAG every 30 minutes to keep one warm worker. Tradeoff: batch polling introduces 15-60 minute event latency versus previous near-real-time EC2 polling. Accepted per PRD as cost-reduction priority outweighs latency for batch analytics use cases.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: DLQ path defined as s3://tracking-dlq/{courier}/failed/{date}/ with JSON payload including original raw event (ISS-001 resolved). Glue partition registration added via GlueCreatePartitionOperator as final DAG task (ISS-002 resolved). EC2 decommission criteria: delta < 0.1% event count for 7 consecutive days (ISS-003 resolved). Athena selected over Redshift Spectrum for cost at current data volumes.

quality_flags: intentional_inconsistency: security section references EC2 instance profiles for MWAA access which is architecturally incorrect; MWAA uses task-level IAM roles
metadata.notes: Cost-reduction data pipeline consolidating six courier tracking streams; intentional flaw in security section conflating EC2 instance profiles with MWAA IAM task roles.

profile.domain: Carrier API Integration SDK

project_goal.value: Rust SDK providing strongly-typed async-first bindings for FedEx Ship API v2 and FedEx Rates and Transit Times API with compile-time required field validation and zero-copy deserialization
project_goal.rationale: Eliminates runtime errors from missing required shipment fields and reduces serialization overhead in high-throughput shipping applications

target_users.value: Rust backend engineers at high-throughput shipping applications integrating with FedEx
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers are Rust services processing thousands of shipment creates per minute

project_class.rationale: Published to crates.io as a library; no hosted runtime

capabilities.rationale: HTTP client bindings to external FedEx APIs; no frontend or storage

complexity_level.rationale: OAuth2 token lifecycle management, compile-time builder patterns, and async HTTP are non-trivial but well-scoped

risk_level.rationale: No user data stored in SDK; FedEx API credentials are consumer responsibility

data_sensitivity.rationale: Shipment data flows through SDK to FedEx; not persisted in SDK

external_exposure.rationale: OAuth2 client credentials required; SDK wraps authenticated FedEx API calls

access_model.value: OAuth2 client credentials (FedEx production and sandbox environments) stored by consumer, passed to SDK at initialization
access_model.rationale: Standard FedEx API v2 authentication mechanism

feature_scope.value: create_shipment with compile-time required field enforcement, rate_shop returning RateShopResponse with ranked_by_cost and ranked_by_transit_time, get_transit_time, cancel_shipment, label retrieval as bytes, OAuth2 token auto-refresh with transparent retry, Zeroizing token on drop
feature_scope.rationale: Covers core ship-and-rate workflow for high-throughput applications

mvp_scope.value: create_shipment, rate_shop with RateShopResponse, and OAuth2 token lifecycle management with Zeroizing and transparent retry
mvp_scope.rationale: Core ship workflow needed before supplementary operations

security_baseline.value: OAuth2 tokens stored as Zeroizing<String> and zeroed on FedexClient drop; TLS 1.3 for all FedEx API calls via reqwest rustls; no FedEx credentials embedded in SDK code; Authorization headers excluded from tracing events
security_baseline.rationale: Consumer credentials must not leak through SDK telemetry, logs, or heap memory post-drop

frontend_stack.rationale: Library SDK has no UI component

backend_stack.value: Rust stable edition 2021 with Tokio 1.x, reqwest 0.12 with rustls-tls, serde 1.x and serde_json 1.x, zeroize 1.x
backend_stack.rationale: Tokio is the fleet async runtime standard; zeroize provides safe credential zeroing without unsafe code

data_platform.value: none
data_platform.rationale: No storage; all data passed through to FedEx API and returned to caller

hosting_target.value: Published to crates.io as fedex-shipping-sdk
hosting_target.rationale: Standard Rust crate distribution

privacy_retention_policy.value: No data retained in SDK; shipment data is ephemeral in-process; OAuth2 tokens stored in Zeroizing<String> and zeroed on drop
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SDK is stateless except for in-memory token cache which is zeroed on drop

future_scope.value: FedEx Ground Economy rate support, pickup scheduling API, tracking events via FedEx webhook adapter, batch shipment creation
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 features after core ship-and-rate is validated in production

constraints.value: Must compile on stable Rust; async support via Tokio only; minimum Rust edition 2021; no nightly features
constraints.rationale: Stable Rust ensures broad consumer compatibility; Tokio is already the fleet standard

observability_baseline.value: SDK emits tracing-compatible structured events at TRACE level per API call with endpoint, HTTP status, and latency; Authorization header values explicitly excluded from trace fields
observability_baseline.rationale: tracing crate integrates with the Rust ecosystem without forcing a specific backend on consumers

execution_preference.value: Semver releases to crates.io; CHANGELOG per Keep a Changelog; breaking changes only in major versions with deprecation period
execution_preference.rationale: Standard Rust ecosystem release conventions

project.goal: Strongly-typed async Rust FedEx API bindings with compile-time field enforcement and zero-copy deserialization
project.target_users: Rust backend engineers building high-throughput shipping services

backend.api: Pub async fn API surface with typestate builder pattern

security.auth: OAuth2 client credentials per FedEx environment
security.transport: TLS 1.3 via reqwest rustls; tokens not logged; Zeroizing on drop

data.state: In-memory OAuth2 token as Arc<RwLock<Option<TokenCacheEntry>>> with Zeroizing<String>

devops.ci: GitHub Actions cargo test, cargo clippy, cargo fmt, cargo tarpaulin
devops.environments: Sandbox and production via FedexEnvironment enum

reasoner_reviews.product: The compile-time required field enforcement is the primary value proposition. Moving FedEx API errors like 'shipper.contact.personName is required' from an HTTP 400 at runtime to a compile error saves engineering time at every consumer integration. The typestate builder with phantom data is the correct Rust mechanism for this.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Typestate builders using generic struct parameters to track SenderSet/RecipientSet/PackageSet/ServiceSet phantom states ensure build() only compiles when all four required groups are present. OAuth2 token refresh using tokio::sync::RwLock rather than Mutex avoids blocking concurrent read access during the non-refresh case. zeroize::Zeroizing<String> is the correct approach for credential zeroing over manual unsafe ptr::write_bytes.
reasoner_reviews.security: ISS-001 is resolved in this round: TokenCacheEntry now holds access_token as Zeroizing<String> which automatically zeros heap bytes when dropped. Authorization headers are explicitly excluded from tracing trace! events. reqwest connection pooling is noted as a risk for shared-client scenarios.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Stable Rust constraint correctly honored. zeroize 1.x is stable and well-maintained. The no-unsafe constraint for public API is met since zeroize provides safe zeroing abstractions.
reasoner_reviews.critic: ISS-002 is resolved: RateShopResponse with ranked_by_cost() and ranked_by_transit_time() provides the flexibility callers need. The API surface is clean and consistent with Rust idioms.

backend.service_design: Single crate fedex-shipping-sdk with modules: client (FedexClient, OAuth2 token lifecycle with Arc<RwLock<Option<TokenCacheEntry>>>, Zeroizing<String> token), ship (ShipmentRequestBuilder typestate, create_shipment, cancel_shipment, get_label), rates (RateShopRequestBuilder, rate_shop returning RateShopResponse, get_transit_time), models (all request/response types with serde derives), error (FedexError enum)
backend.api_patterns: pub async fn create_shipment(&self, req: ShipmentRequest) -> Result<ShipmentResponse, FedexError>. Builder: ShipmentRequestBuilder::new().sender(s).recipient(r).package(p).service(FedexService::Ground).build() where build() only compiles when all four typestate generics are set. rate_shop returns RateShopResponse with ranked_by_cost() and ranked_by_transit_time() methods.
backend.business_modules: OAuth2 module: fetch_token and refresh_token storing Zeroizing<String> in TokenCacheEntry. Transparent 1-retry on 401 after token refresh. FedexEnvironment enum with Sandbox and Production variants mapping to corresponding base URLs. RateShopResponse struct with sorting methods for cost and transit time.
backend.failure_handling: FedexError::ApiError { status_code: u16, message: String, error_codes: Vec<String> }. FedexError::NetworkError(reqwest::Error). FedexError::AuthError(String). FedexError::SerdeError for unexpected response shapes. All variants implement std::error::Error.
backend.backend_risks: FedEx API v2 deprecations require SDK version bumps; subscribe to FedEx developer changelog. reqwest minor upgrades may change TLS defaults; pin with compatible version range and document in CHANGELOG.

security.auth_design: OAuth2 client_credentials grant against api.fedex.com/oauth2/token. Token stored as Zeroizing<String> inside TokenCacheEntry wrapped in Arc<RwLock<Option<TokenCacheEntry>>>. TokenCacheEntry derives zeroize::Zeroize; Drop calls self.access_token.zeroize() explicitly. TLS enforced by reqwest rustls feature.
security.secrets_management: FedexClient::new(client_id, client_secret, env) accepts credentials from caller; SDK never logs credentials or token values. tracing::trace! events explicitly exclude Authorization header values from fields.
security.security_risks: reqwest connection pooling may retain TCP connections longer than token TTL; document that FedexClient instances should not be shared across differently-authenticated callers.

data.entities: ShipmentRequest (sender ContactInfo, recipient ContactInfo, package PackageDimensions, service FedexService, special_services Vec<SpecialService>). ContactInfo (person_name String, company Option<String>, phone String, address Address). RateQuote (service_type String, currency String, net_charge f64, transit_days u8, surcharges Vec<Surcharge>). RateShopResponse (quotes Vec<RateQuote>). TrackingNumber (newtype String). LabelResponse (label_bytes Vec<u8>, label_format LabelFormat, tracking_number TrackingNumber). TokenCacheEntry (access_token Zeroizing<String>, expires_at Instant, token_type String).
data.schema_notes: All public structs derive serde::Deserialize with serde(rename_all = 'camelCase') matching FedEx API v2 JSON. Public types use owned String for lifetime safety; zero-copy Cow reserved for internal parsing only.
data.data_risks: FedEx API response schema changes break deserialization; use serde(deny_unknown_fields) in integration tests against recorded responses, permissive defaults in production to avoid breaking callers on additive FedEx changes.

devops.deployment_topology: Published to crates.io as fedex-shipping-sdk. Sandbox and production FedEx environments via FedexEnvironment enum passed to FedexClient::new.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on every PR: cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy -- -D warnings, cargo test --all (unit and integration with sandbox credentials in GitHub Actions secrets), cargo tarpaulin coverage > 75%. Semver tags trigger cargo publish to crates.io via CARGO_REGISTRY_TOKEN.
devops.devops_risks: crates.io publish failures require manual republish; maintain private registry mirror for internal consumers as fallback. FedEx sandbox downtime blocks integration tests; use wiremock recorded response fixtures for offline CI fallback.

strengths: Typestate builder pattern for ShipmentRequestBuilder is correctly designed with phantom data generics

Introduce RateShopResponse struct with ranked_by_cost() returning sorted slice and ranked_by_transit_time() returning sorted references

previous_plan.title: FedEx Shipping SDK v2 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: Strongly-typed async Rust SDK for FedEx Ship API v2 and Rates API. Typestate ShipmentRequestBuilder enforces required fields at compile time. OAuth2 token lifecycle managed with Arc<RwLock<Option<TokenCacheEntry>>> but Drop zeroing absent (ISS-001). rate_shop returns Vec<RateQuote> sorted by cost (ISS-002).
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Single crate with client, ship, rates, models, error modules. FedexClient holds token cache via RwLock. TokenCacheEntry stores access_token as plain String without Drop zeroing.
previous_plan.api_design: create_shipment(req: ShipmentRequest) -> Result<ShipmentResponse, FedexError>. rate_shop(req: RateShopRequest) -> Result<Vec<RateQuote>, FedexError> sorted by cost. cancel_shipment(tracking_number: TrackingNumber) -> Result<(), FedexError>.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: OAuth2 client_credentials flow. TLS via reqwest. Token not logged. Drop zeroing absent (ISS-001 unresolved).

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 resolves ISS-001 by adopting the zeroize crate to store the OAuth2 access token as Zeroizing<String> in TokenCacheEntry, with an explicit Drop impl calling zeroize(). ISS-002 is resolved by replacing the Vec<RateQuote> return from rate_shop with RateShopResponse carrying ranked_by_cost() and ranked_by_transit_time() methods. Both fixes are localized and do not affect the typestate builder architecture established in earlier rounds.

fix_report.action_taken: Added zeroize = '1' dependency with derive feature. Changed access_token field in TokenCacheEntry from String to Zeroizing<String>. Added impl Drop for TokenCacheEntry calling self.access_token.zeroize() explicitly. Updated security_and_compliance, data_model, and technology_stack sections.
fix_report.expected_outcome: FedEx OAuth2 access tokens are zeroed in heap memory when FedexClient is dropped, preventing credential exposure via memory inspection tools

fix_report.action_taken: Changed rate_shop() return type from Result<Vec<RateQuote>, FedexError> to Result<RateShopResponse, FedexError>. RateShopResponse holds quotes: Vec<RateQuote> and exposes ranked_by_cost() returning &[RateQuote] sorted ascending by net_charge, and ranked_by_transit_time() returning Vec<&RateQuote> sorted ascending by transit_days. Updated api_design and functional_feature_map.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Callers can select the cheapest or fastest service without pre-sorting, supporting both economy and time-critical shipment selection workflows

target_output.title: FedEx Shipping SDK Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A strongly-typed async Rust SDK published to crates.io providing compile-time validated bindings for FedEx Ship API v2 and the Rates and Transit Times API. Typestate builder patterns on ShipmentRequestBuilder prevent missing required field API errors at compile time. OAuth2 token lifecycle is managed transparently with Zeroizing<String> ensuring token bytes are zeroed on FedexClient drop. rate_shop() returns RateShopResponse with ranked_by_cost() and ranked_by_transit_time() methods for flexible carrier selection.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single Rust crate fedex-shipping-sdk organized into five modules: client (FedexClient initialization, OAuth2 token fetch and refresh with Arc<RwLock<Option<TokenCacheEntry>>> where TokenCacheEntry holds Zeroizing<String> and implements Drop calling zeroize()), ship (ShipmentRequestBuilder typestate with SenderSet/RecipientSet/PackageSet/ServiceSet phantom states, create_shipment, cancel_shipment, get_label), rates (RateShopRequestBuilder, rate_shop returning RateShopResponse with ranking methods, get_transit_time), models (all request/response structs with serde camelCase renaming), error (FedexError enum with ApiError, NetworkError, AuthError, SerdeError variants). All async functions are Tokio-runtime compatible. TLS enforced via reqwest rustls feature. tracing::trace! events instrument each API call while explicitly excluding Authorization header values.
target_output.technology_stack: Rust stable edition 2021. tokio 1.x with full features for async runtime. reqwest 0.12 with rustls-tls feature for HTTP client and TLS. serde 1.x and serde_json 1.x with derive features for request and response types. zeroize 1.x with derive feature for TokenCacheEntry credential zeroing. tracing 0.1 for structured event emission without forcing a subscriber backend. wiremock for integration test HTTP recording. cargo-tarpaulin for test coverage reporting. cargo-release for semver tagging and crates.io publish automation.
target_output.functional_feature_map: FedexClient::new(client_id, client_secret, env: FedexEnvironment) initializes reqwest HTTP client and token cache. create_shipment accepts ShipmentRequest built via ShipmentRequestBuilder requiring .sender().recipient().package().service() before .build() compiles; build() is only available on ShipmentRequestBuilder<SenderSet, RecipientSet, PackageSet, ServiceSet>. cancel_shipment(TrackingNumber) sends DELETE to FedEx cancellation endpoint. get_label(TrackingNumber, LabelFormat) returns LabelResponse with label_bytes. rate_shop(RateShopRequest) returns RateShopResponse with ranked_by_cost() returning slice sorted ascending by net_charge and ranked_by_transit_time() returning references sorted by transit_days. get_transit_time(origin, destination, service) returns TransitTimeResponse.
target_output.system_components: FedexClient struct: holds reqwest::Client (connection pooled), Arc<RwLock<Option<TokenCacheEntry>>>, FedexEnvironment with Sandbox and Production variants. TokenCacheEntry: access_token Zeroizing<String>, expires_at Instant, token_type String; derives Zeroize; Drop calls self.access_token.zeroize() before deallocation. ShipmentRequestBuilder<S, R, P, Svc> typestate struct with PhantomData tracking four required field groups; build() only available on fully-set generic variant. RateShopResponse: quotes Vec<RateQuote>, ranked_by_cost() returning &[RateQuote], ranked_by_transit_time() returning Vec<&RateQuote>. FedexError enum: ApiError { status_code u16, message String, error_codes Vec<String> }, NetworkError(reqwest::Error), AuthError(String), SerdeError.
target_output.workflows: Shipment Creation: Caller constructs ShipmentRequestBuilder::new().sender(ContactInfo{person_name: 'Jane Doe', phone: '555-0100', address: Address{...}}).recipient(ContactInfo{...}).package(PackageDimensions{weight_lb: 5.0, length_in: 12, width_in: 8, height_in: 4}).service(FedexService::Ground).build(). Calls client.create_shipment(req).await. FedexClient acquires RwLock read on TokenCacheEntry; if expired acquires write lock, posts to api.fedex.com/oauth2/token, stores new Zeroizing<String> token. Sends POST to /ship/v1/shipments. Returns ShipmentResponse with tracking_number. Rate Shopping: Caller builds RateShopRequest with origin city/state/zip, destination, package dimensions. client.rate_shop(req).await returns RateShopResponse. Caller iterates response.ranked_by_transit_time() to find the fastest service within a budget ceiling. Token Drop: When FedexClient goes out of scope, Arc ref count reaches zero, TokenCacheEntry Drop fires, self.access_token.zeroize() overwrites heap allocation with zero bytes before deallocation.
target_output.data_model: ShipmentRequest (sender ContactInfo, recipient ContactInfo, package PackageDimensions, service FedexService, special_services Vec<SpecialService>, shipper_account String). ContactInfo (person_name String, company Option<String>, phone String, address Address). PackageDimensions (weight_lb f64, length_in u32, width_in u32, height_in u32). RateQuote (service_type String, currency String, net_charge f64, transit_days u8, surcharges Vec<Surcharge>). RateShopResponse (quotes Vec<RateQuote>; ranked_by_cost() and ranked_by_transit_time() methods). TrackingNumber (newtype String, format-validated on construction). LabelResponse (label_bytes Vec<u8>, label_format LabelFormat enum, tracking_number TrackingNumber). TokenCacheEntry (access_token Zeroizing<String>, expires_at Instant, token_type String; derives Zeroize).
target_output.api_design: pub async fn create_shipment(&self, req: ShipmentRequest) -> Result<ShipmentResponse, FedexError>. ShipmentRequestBuilder::new().sender(s).recipient(r).package(p).service(svc).build() where build() only compiles on ShipmentRequestBuilder<SenderSet,RecipientSet,PackageSet,ServiceSet>. pub async fn cancel_shipment(&self, tn: TrackingNumber) -> Result<(), FedexError>. pub async fn get_label(&self, tn: TrackingNumber, fmt: LabelFormat) -> Result<LabelResponse, FedexError>. pub async fn rate_shop(&self, req: RateShopRequest) -> Result<RateShopResponse, FedexError>. impl RateShopResponse: pub fn ranked_by_cost(&self) -> &[RateQuote]; pub fn ranked_by_transit_time(&self) -> Vec<&RateQuote>. pub async fn get_transit_time(&self, origin: Address, dest: Address, svc: FedexService) -> Result<TransitTimeResponse, FedexError>.
target_output.security_and_compliance: OAuth2 client_credentials grant against api.fedex.com/oauth2/token; client_id and client_secret passed by caller and never stored in SDK source. TokenCacheEntry derives zeroize::Zeroize and stores access_token as Zeroizing<String>; Drop impl calls self.access_token.zeroize() overwriting heap memory before deallocation. reqwest enforces TLS 1.2+ via rustls; no HTTP plaintext. tracing::trace! logs endpoint URL (sanitized), HTTP status code, and request duration in milliseconds; Authorization header values are explicitly absent from all trace fields. FedexEnvironment::Sandbox directs calls to sandbox API URLs preventing accidental production credential use in tests.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI on every PR: cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy -- -D warnings, cargo test --all (unit tests and integration tests against FedEx sandbox with client_id and client_secret stored as GitHub Actions encrypted secrets), cargo tarpaulin --min-coverage 75. Integration tests use wiremock to record and replay FedEx sandbox HTTP interactions for offline CI fallback when sandbox is unavailable. Semver tags (v0.x.x pre-GA, v1.0.0 GA) trigger cargo publish to crates.io via CARGO_REGISTRY_TOKEN GitHub Actions secret. Yanking policy: yank within 24 hours if a security issue is reported for a published version. Minimum supported Rust version 1.75 documented in Cargo.toml rust-version field.
target_output.observability: tracing::trace! on each FedEx API call: fields http.url (no query params containing credentials), http.status_code, http.request_duration_ms, fedex.environment. tracing::warn! on OAuth2 token refresh triggered mid-request due to expiry. tracing::error! on FedexError::ApiError with status_code and first error_code. Consumers wire in their preferred tracing subscriber (tracing-subscriber, OpenTelemetry tracing bridge, etc.) without SDK imposing a backend. No SDK-emitted metrics; consumers measure rates and latency via spans in their subscriber.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No cloud runtime cost for the SDK itself. crates.io hosting is free. GitHub Actions CI costs are minimal. Consumers pay for FedEx API call volume per their developer account tier. Zeroizing<String> overhead on drop is negligible compared to HTTP round-trip latency. Connection pooling in reqwest minimizes per-request overhead for callers processing over 1000 shipment creates per minute.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): FedexClient with Zeroizing<String> OAuth2 token lifecycle (ISS-001 resolved), create_shipment with typestate builder, rate_shop with RateShopResponse and ranking methods (ISS-002 resolved), cancel_shipment, GitHub Actions CI with wiremock fixtures, crates.io publish pipeline. Phase 2 (4 weeks): get_label with LabelFormat enum, get_transit_time, tracing instrumentation, CHANGELOG, 75% coverage gate. Phase 3 post-GA: FedEx Ground Economy rates, pickup scheduling API, batch shipment creation for label printing workflows.
target_output.development_guidelines: All public types and functions must have rustdoc comments with usage examples. No unsafe code in public API surface; zeroize crate provides safe zeroing. Table-driven tests with #[tokio::test] for all async public functions. wiremock response fixtures for each FedEx API endpoint used in integration tests. All FedexError variants implement std::error::Error and Display. Minimum supported Rust version 1.75 in Cargo.toml rust-version field. CHANGELOG maintained per Keep a Changelog with semantic versioning entries for each release.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: FedEx API v2 schema changes break serde deserialization. Mitigation: use serde(deny_unknown_fields) in integration test models against wiremock-recorded responses; use permissive defaults in production types to avoid breaking callers on additive FedEx changes. Risk: reqwest minor version upgrades change TLS defaults. Mitigation: pin with compatible semver range and document changes in CHANGELOG. Tradeoff: Zeroizing<String> adds a small allocation overhead vs plain String; accepted as correct credential hygiene for a shipping API SDK. Tradeoff: typestate builder pattern increases compile-time complexity for SDK consumers; documented as intentional cost of compile-time safety.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: zeroize crate selected over manual unsafe ptr::write_bytes for token zeroing to keep public crate free of unsafe blocks (ISS-001 resolved). RateShopResponse with ranked_by_cost() and ranked_by_transit_time() replaces Vec<RateQuote> pre-sorted by cost (ISS-002 resolved). wiremock selected over vcr-cassette for HTTP interaction recording due to better async Rust support. Minimum Rust version set to 1.75 for stable async fn in traits support.

metadata.notes: Rust SDK for FedEx Ship API v2 with compile-time field enforcement; round 3 resolves Zeroizing token security issue and RateShopResponse API design.

profile.domain: Cross-Docking Terminal Operations

project_goal.value: Automation tool generating optimized unload-to-load dock door assignments by matching inbound trailer arrival schedules against outbound departure windows, minimizing freight travel distance across the terminal dock floor
project_goal.rationale: Manual door assignment by supervisors results in excessive freight travel distance and missed outbound connections

target_users.value: Cross-docking terminal operators and dock supervisors at logistics facilities
target_users.rationale: Primary users are terminal ops staff who plan and execute daily dock door schedules

project_class.rationale: Batch scheduling optimizer with TMS integration and webhook notifications; no interactive web UI

capabilities.rationale: Constraint optimization engine, PostgreSQL state store, TMS integration, webhook dispatcher

complexity_level.rationale: Constraint-based optimization over hundreds of doors and trailers per planning cycle is non-trivial but scope-bounded

risk_level.rationale: Tool produces recommendations; final execution authority remains with dock supervisors

data_sensitivity.rationale: Trailer arrival schedules and freight manifests are internal operational data

external_exposure.rationale: Tool runs on terminal LAN; TMS integration is internal system-to-system

access_model.value: Custom JWT authentication for TMS integration API calls; no human login to the automation tool itself
access_model.rationale: Tool operates as a scheduled service; human interaction is via TMS UI and webhook notifications

feature_scope.value: Constraint-based dock door assignment optimizer, inbound arrival and outbound departure schedule ingestion from TMS, freight manifest cross-referencing for freight-to-door matching, delay-triggered reassignment with webhook notifications to dock supervisors, assignment publication back to TMS
feature_scope.rationale: Full planning and exception management lifecycle

mvp_scope.value: Daily planning cycle optimization for a single terminal, TMS schedule ingestion, door assignment generation, and assignment publication to TMS
mvp_scope.rationale: Single-terminal validation before multi-terminal rollout

security_baseline.value: JWT tokens for TMS API authentication; PostgreSQL with local Unix socket access; webhook endpoints authenticated with HMAC-SHA256 signed payloads; no public internet exposure
security_baseline.rationale: Terminal LAN deployment; primary security concern is TMS API auth and webhook payload integrity

frontend_stack.rationale: Automation tool with no UI; supervisors view assignments in TMS and receive webhook notifications

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI for internal REST endpoints and OR-Tools 9.x for constraint optimization
backend_stack.rationale: OR-Tools provides mature CP-SAT solver for constraint satisfaction; FastAPI for TMS callback and health endpoints

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 running in Docker on bare-metal terminal server
data_platform.rationale: Relational model suits trailer-door assignment state; local Docker deployment matches terminal infrastructure constraints

hosting_target.value: Docker Compose on bare-metal terminal server, self-hosted, no cloud dependency
hosting_target.rationale: Terminals run in environments with limited or unreliable internet connectivity; local deployment is mandatory

privacy_retention_policy.value: Arrival and departure schedules retained 90 days for performance analysis; door assignment history retained 1 year for audit; no personal data processed
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Operational performance review and audit requirements

future_scope.value: Multi-terminal coordination for interline transfers, lane utilization analytics dashboard, mobile app for dock supervisor webhook interactions
future_scope.rationale: Single-terminal MVP validates core optimization before expanding scope

constraints.value: Must run without internet connectivity; Docker Compose only (no Kubernetes); planning cycle must complete within 60 seconds for up to 200 doors and 400 trailers
constraints.rationale: Terminal infrastructure and performance requirements

observability_baseline.value: Structured JSON logs to file with Grafana+Prometheus for planning cycle duration, optimization score, and webhook delivery metrics
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing terminal monitoring infrastructure uses Grafana+Prometheus

execution_preference.value: Containerized deployment with GitLab CI building Docker images; tested against representative terminal fixture data before each release
execution_preference.rationale: Reproducible builds and tested releases for terminal IT teams to deploy

project.goal: Automated dock door assignment optimizer for cross-docking terminals minimizing freight travel distance

backend.framework: FastAPI for REST endpoints, OR-Tools 9.x CP-SAT solver for optimization
backend.api: Internal REST endpoints for TMS schedule ingestion and assignment retrieval

security.auth: Custom JWT for TMS API
security.transport: LAN only; no public internet exposure

data.schema: inbound_trailers, outbound_trailers, dock_doors, door_assignments, freight_manifests, arrival_schedules, departure_windows

constraints.performance: 60-second planning cycle for 200 doors and 400 trailers

reasoner_reviews.product: The core value is reducing freight travel distance within the cross-dock, which directly reduces labor hours for freight handlers moving pallets from unload doors to load doors. A 15-20% reduction in travel distance is a realistic optimization target for a CP-SAT model with door distance matrix and freight volume constraints. The delay-triggered reassignment feature is critical for intraday value.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: OR-Tools CP-SAT is the correct solver choice over greedy heuristics because it can incorporate multiple constraint types simultaneously: door distance costs, freight volume balance per door, departure time compatibility windows, and trailer availability constraints. The planning cycle should precompute a door-to-door distance matrix from the terminal floor layout at startup and pass it as coefficients to the CP-SAT model. PostgreSQL is correct for tracking assignment state and history; no need for a graph database.
reasoner_reviews.security: HMAC-SHA256 signed webhook payloads are the right integrity mechanism for supervisor notifications in a LAN environment. JWT tokens for TMS API calls should have short expiry (15 minutes) and be refreshed before each planning cycle. No internet exposure means the attack surface is limited to the terminal LAN.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 60-second planning cycle constraint for 200 doors and 400 trailers is achievable with CP-SAT given a well-formulated model. OR-Tools CP-SAT with a 45-second solver time limit and a fallback to the best incumbent solution at timeout is the correct approach. The no-internet constraint drives all dependencies into the Docker image; use a fat image build with pip install --no-index from a vendored package directory.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The freight manifest cross-referencing for freight-to-door matching needs explicit design. Not all freight on an inbound trailer needs to go to the same outbound trailer; the model should operate at the freight line item level for accurate distance optimization, not just the trailer level.

backend.service_design: Python package crossdock_optimizer with modules: scheduler (planning cycle orchestration, PlanningCycle dataclass), optimizer (OR-Tools CP-SAT model builder, DoorAssignmentSolver class), tms_client (TMS REST API adapter for schedule ingestion and assignment publication), webhook_dispatcher (HMAC-signed notifications to supervisor endpoints), api (FastAPI app with /health, /assignments, /planning-cycles endpoints)
backend.api_patterns: POST /api/v1/planning-cycles to trigger an on-demand planning run. GET /api/v1/assignments?date={date} to retrieve current door assignments. POST /api/v1/schedules/ingest to receive TMS push of arrival/departure schedule updates. All endpoints return JSON; error responses follow RFC 7807 problem detail format.
backend.background_jobs: Scheduled planning cycle job running at configurable cron (default: daily at 03:00 local terminal time for next-day plan, intraday at 06:00 and 12:00). Delay detection poller querying TMS for updated ETAs every 10 minutes during operational hours, triggering reassignment when inbound trailer delay exceeds 30-minute threshold.
backend.failure_handling: If OR-Tools CP-SAT exceeds 45-second time limit, returns best incumbent solution rather than failing. TMS API call failures retry 3 times with exponential backoff; if TMS unavailable, planning cycle uses last cached schedule from PostgreSQL. Webhook delivery failures retry 5 times with jitter; undelivered webhooks logged to failed_webhooks table for manual review.
backend.backend_risks: OR-Tools CP-SAT model infeasibility if constraints are over-specified (e.g., too many hard time-window constraints). Mitigation: define time-window constraints as soft (penalty cost) rather than hard when possible, allowing the solver to find a feasible solution with a cost rather than returning infeasible.

security.auth_design: TMS API JWT tokens fetched before each planning cycle call with 15-minute expiry. Tokens stored in memory only; never written to disk or logs. Webhook payloads signed with HMAC-SHA256 using a shared secret stored in Docker secret mount.
security.secrets_management: TMS client credentials and webhook HMAC key stored in Docker secrets mounted at /run/secrets/; read at startup into environment variables. No credentials in Docker Compose YAML or environment variable files committed to Git.
security.security_risks: LAN-local PostgreSQL with Unix socket access is appropriate for bare-metal deployment; if terminal server is shared with other services, create a dedicated postgres user with access only to the crossdock database.

data.entities: inbound_trailers (id UUID PK, trailer_number VARCHAR, carrier_code VARCHAR, scheduled_arrival TIMESTAMPTZ, actual_arrival TIMESTAMPTZ, delay_minutes INT, freight_manifest_id UUID FK, status ENUM scheduled/arrived/unloaded/departed). outbound_trailers (id UUID PK, trailer_number VARCHAR, carrier_code VARCHAR, scheduled_departure TIMESTAMPTZ, assigned_door_id UUID FK, status ENUM scheduled/loading/departed). dock_doors (id UUID PK, door_number INT, dock_side ENUM inbound/outbound/cross, x_coordinate FLOAT, y_coordinate FLOAT, terminal_id UUID FK). door_assignments (id UUID PK, planning_cycle_id UUID FK, inbound_trailer_id UUID FK, outbound_trailer_id UUID FK, inbound_door_id UUID FK, outbound_door_id UUID FK, freight_travel_distance_meters FLOAT, assigned_at TIMESTAMPTZ). freight_manifests (id UUID PK, trailer_id UUID FK, line_items JSONB with sku, weight_kg, destination_trailer_id). arrival_schedules (id UUID PK, terminal_id UUID FK, schedule_date DATE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, source ENUM tms_push/manual). departure_windows (id UUID PK, outbound_trailer_id UUID FK, open_at TIMESTAMPTZ, close_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 in Docker on bare-metal. Connection pool via asyncpg with max 10 connections. Door-to-door Euclidean distance matrix precomputed at service startup from dock_doors coordinates and cached in memory as numpy array indexed by door_id.
data.migration_notes: Alembic for schema versioning. Migrations run in Docker entrypoint before service start. Initial data load: terminal floor layout (dock_doors coordinates) seeded from JSON config file at first startup.

devops.deployment_topology: Docker Compose on bare-metal terminal server: crossdock-api container (FastAPI + optimizer), postgres container (PostgreSQL 16 with named volume), prometheus container (scraping /metrics endpoint), grafana container (dashboards for planning cycle metrics). No internet required after initial image pull.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: pytest with 80% coverage on optimizer and scheduler modules, docker build, docker-compose up --detach with smoke test against /health endpoint, docker push to internal GitLab registry. Terminal IT deploys by pulling latest image and running docker-compose up -d.
devops.cost_controls: No cloud spend; bare-metal hardware is terminal-owned. Docker image build is the only external compute dependency.
devops.devops_risks: Bare-metal server failure has no automatic failover; document manual recovery procedure with PostgreSQL named volume backup to USB drive weekly.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a self-hosted cross-docking door assignment optimizer. Key decisions: OR-Tools CP-SAT with a 45-second time limit and best-incumbent fallback for the 200-door/400-trailer scale requirement; door-to-door Euclidean distance matrix precomputed at startup as a numpy array; Docker Compose bare-metal deployment with no internet dependency; freight manifest modeled at line-item granularity for accurate distance optimization; HMAC-SHA256 webhook signatures for supervisor notification integrity.
target_output.title: Cross-Docking Terminal Door Assignment Optimizer Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A self-hosted Python automation tool that generates optimized dock door assignments for cross-docking terminals by solving a constraint satisfaction problem over inbound arrival schedules, outbound departure windows, and freight manifest line items. OR-Tools CP-SAT minimizes total freight travel distance across the dock floor. Assignments are published back to the TMS and delay-triggered reassignments are dispatched to dock supervisors via HMAC-signed webhooks. Deployed as Docker Compose on bare-metal terminal servers with no internet dependency.
target_output.architecture_overview: FastAPI service hosting internal REST endpoints for TMS schedule ingestion and assignment retrieval, backed by a DoorAssignmentSolver class wrapping OR-Tools CP-SAT. A door-to-door Euclidean distance matrix indexed by door_id is precomputed from PostgreSQL dock_doors coordinates at startup and held in memory as a numpy array. A planning cycle orchestrator runs on a cron schedule and on-demand, fetching inbound and outbound schedules from PostgreSQL, building the CP-SAT model with distance minimization objective and departure-window compatibility constraints, and writing assignments back to the door_assignments table. A webhook dispatcher sends HMAC-SHA256 signed JSON payloads to configured supervisor endpoints when delay-triggered reassignments fire. A delay poller runs every 10 minutes during operational hours, comparing current TMS ETAs against scheduled arrivals and triggering reassignment when delays exceed 30 minutes.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12. FastAPI 0.111 with uvicorn for internal REST endpoints. OR-Tools 9.x CP-SAT solver for constraint optimization. asyncpg for async PostgreSQL connection pooling. Alembic for schema migrations. numpy for in-memory door distance matrix computations. APScheduler 3.x for cron-based planning cycle and delay poller jobs. prometheus-client for /metrics endpoint. Docker Compose 2.x for bare-metal deployment. GitLab CI for build, test, and registry push.
target_output.functional_feature_map: DoorAssignmentSolver: loads door distance matrix, builds CP-SAT model with inbound-to-outbound freight routing variables, departure-window soft constraints as penalties, trailer capacity constraints, and a distance minimization objective. Solves with 45-second time limit; returns best incumbent on timeout. TMS Integration: schedule ingestion via TMS REST API pull at planning cycle start and push webhook receiver. Assignment Publication: writes solved door_assignments to PostgreSQL and sends assignment payload to TMS via REST. Delay Management: delay poller detects inbound ETA changes, triggers incremental reassignment for affected trailers, dispatches supervisor webhooks with HMAC-SHA256 signatures. Planning Cycle Audit: stores optimizer solution metadata (solve time, objective value, incumbent solution found) per planning_cycle record.
target_output.system_components: crossdock-api Docker container: FastAPI app with /health, /api/v1/planning-cycles, /api/v1/assignments, /api/v1/schedules/ingest endpoints; DoorAssignmentSolver; TmsClient; WebhookDispatcher; APScheduler for background jobs; prometheus-client /metrics. postgres Docker container: PostgreSQL 16 with named volume crossdock-pgdata. prometheus Docker container: scraping crossdock-api:9090/metrics. grafana Docker container: dashboards for planning_cycle_duration_seconds, optimization_objective_value, webhook_delivery_success_rate, delay_reassignment_count. Docker secrets mount at /run/secrets/ for TMS credentials and webhook HMAC key.
target_output.workflows: Daily Planning Cycle: APScheduler triggers planning cycle at 03:00. Orchestrator fetches next-day inbound_trailers and outbound_trailers from PostgreSQL. DoorAssignmentSolver precomputes freight line item routing options from freight_manifests. Builds CP-SAT model: binary variables x[freight_line, inbound_door, outbound_door] indicating door pair assignment; objective minimizes sum of x[i,j,k] * distance_matrix[j][k] * line_item_weight. Departure window compatibility enforced as soft constraints with configurable penalty cost. Solves within 45 seconds; writes door_assignments to PostgreSQL. TmsClient publishes assignments to TMS. Delay-Triggered Reassignment: Delay poller detects inbound trailer ETA slipping 30+ minutes past scheduled arrival. Identifies outbound trailers at risk of missing departure windows. Runs incremental CP-SAT solve for affected freight lines only. Publishes updated assignments to TMS. WebhookDispatcher sends HMAC-SHA256 signed payload to supervisor endpoint listing reassigned doors and at-risk outbound trailers.
target_output.data_model: inbound_trailers (id UUID PK, trailer_number VARCHAR, carrier_code VARCHAR, scheduled_arrival TIMESTAMPTZ, actual_arrival TIMESTAMPTZ, delay_minutes INT, status ENUM scheduled/arrived/unloaded/departed). outbound_trailers (id UUID PK, trailer_number VARCHAR, carrier_code VARCHAR, scheduled_departure TIMESTAMPTZ, assigned_door_id UUID FK, status ENUM scheduled/loading/departed). dock_doors (id UUID PK, door_number INT, dock_side ENUM inbound/outbound/cross, x_coordinate FLOAT, y_coordinate FLOAT). door_assignments (id UUID PK, planning_cycle_id UUID FK, inbound_trailer_id UUID FK, outbound_trailer_id UUID FK, inbound_door_id UUID FK, outbound_door_id UUID FK, freight_travel_distance_meters FLOAT, assigned_at TIMESTAMPTZ). freight_manifests (id UUID PK, trailer_id UUID FK, line_items JSONB: [{sku, weight_kg, destination_trailer_id}]). arrival_schedules (id UUID PK, schedule_date DATE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). departure_windows (id UUID PK, outbound_trailer_id UUID FK, open_at TIMESTAMPTZ, close_at TIMESTAMPTZ). planning_cycles (id UUID PK, cycle_date DATE, solve_duration_seconds FLOAT, objective_value FLOAT, incumbent_found BOOL, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: POST /api/v1/planning-cycles { date: YYYY-MM-DD } triggers on-demand planning cycle; returns { planning_cycle_id, status: running }. GET /api/v1/planning-cycles/{id} returns cycle metadata and status. GET /api/v1/assignments?date=YYYY-MM-DD returns door_assignments list with inbound_door, outbound_door, trailer_numbers, freight_distance_meters. POST /api/v1/schedules/ingest accepts TMS push of arrival and departure schedule updates; idempotent on trailer_number+schedule_date key. GET /health returns { status: ok, db: connected, solver: ready }. GET /metrics returns prometheus text format metrics.
target_output.security_and_compliance: TMS API JWT tokens fetched before each planning cycle with 15-minute expiry; stored in memory only, never logged or written to disk. Webhook payloads signed with HMAC-SHA256 using shared secret read from Docker secrets mount at /run/secrets/webhook_hmac_key; supervisors verify signature before acting on payload. PostgreSQL accessed via Unix socket from crossdock-api container using a dedicated postgres user crossdock_app with GRANT on crossdock database only; no TCP exposure of PostgreSQL port outside the Docker network. No public internet exposure; all communication is LAN-internal between terminal server, TMS, and supervisor workstations.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Docker Compose on bare-metal terminal server with four services: crossdock-api (Python 3.12 slim image with OR-Tools and dependencies vendored in /app/vendor for offline pip install), postgres (postgres:16-alpine with named volume crossdock-pgdata), prometheus (prom/prometheus with scrape config for crossdock-api:9090/metrics), grafana (grafana/grafana with provisioned dashboards). GitLab CI pipeline: pytest --cov=crossdock_optimizer --cov-report=term-missing with 80% coverage gate on optimizer and scheduler modules, docker build --no-cache, docker-compose -f docker-compose.test.yml up --abort-on-container-exit smoke test, docker push to internal GitLab registry. Terminal IT deployment: docker-compose pull && docker-compose up -d. Alembic migrations run in crossdock-api entrypoint before uvicorn start. PostgreSQL named volume backed up weekly to USB via pg_dump cron job.
target_output.observability: prometheus-client Counter and Histogram metrics: planning_cycle_duration_seconds (Histogram), optimization_objective_value (Gauge), delay_reassignment_count (Counter), webhook_delivery_success_total (Counter), webhook_delivery_failure_total (Counter). Structured JSON logs to stdout captured by Docker logging driver to /var/log/crossdock/. Grafana dashboard panels: daily planning cycle duration trend, optimization objective value vs baseline heuristic, webhook delivery success rate, delay reassignment frequency per day.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No cloud spend; terminal server hardware is existing infrastructure. OR-Tools CP-SAT scales to 200 doors and 400 trailers within 45 seconds on a single CPU core. For larger terminals (500+ doors), increase solver time limit or switch to a decomposed model solving by freight line category. PostgreSQL on the same bare-metal host handles the moderate write load of one planning cycle per day plus intraday delay polling updates.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Single-terminal deployment, TMS schedule ingestion, OR-Tools CP-SAT door assignment optimizer with distance matrix, door_assignments PostgreSQL write, TMS assignment publication, Docker Compose deployment, GitLab CI. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Delay detection poller, incremental reassignment solver, HMAC-signed webhook dispatcher to supervisor endpoints, Grafana dashboards, PostgreSQL weekly backup cron. Phase 3 post-GA: Multi-terminal coordination for interline freight transfers, lane utilization analytics, mobile supervisor webhook notification app.
target_output.development_guidelines: OR-Tools CP-SAT model must be unit tested with a small fixture terminal (10 doors, 20 trailers) asserting that the solver returns a feasible solution and that all assigned freight line items have compatible departure windows. Integration tests use a dockerized PostgreSQL fixture. All CP-SAT model constraint additions must be documented with the business rule they enforce. TMS client mocked in unit tests using responses library. HMAC signature verification tested in webhook dispatcher tests with known key and payload. No hardcoded terminal configuration; all terminal-specific data (door coordinates, TMS endpoint URLs) loaded from config files mounted as Docker volumes.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: OR-Tools CP-SAT returns infeasible for over-constrained scenarios where too many hard departure window constraints conflict. Mitigation: all departure window constraints defined as soft penalties rather than hard constraints; solver always returns a solution even if some windows are violated, with violations surfaced in assignment output for supervisor review. Risk: bare-metal server failure results in unplanned outage with no automatic failover. Mitigation: document manual recovery runbook; weekly PostgreSQL backup to USB provides 7-day recovery point objective. Tradeoff: freight manifest modeled at line-item granularity increases CP-SAT model size but is required for accurate distance optimization; trailer-level modeling would be faster but miss intra-trailer freight routing opportunities.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: OR-Tools CP-SAT selected over PuLP+GLPK for its better performance on large binary optimization problems. Departure window constraints implemented as soft penalties rather than hard constraints to ensure solver always returns a feasible assignment plan. Freight manifest modeled at line-item level per critic recommendation for accurate distance optimization. Docker secrets used for TMS credentials and webhook HMAC key rather than environment variables in Docker Compose YAML.

metadata.notes: Cross-docking door assignment optimizer using OR-Tools CP-SAT; self-hosted Docker Compose on bare-metal terminal server with no internet dependency.

project.goal: Live yard visibility and truck assignment desktop tool for dispatchers

frontend.canvas: HTML5 Canvas via Konva.js for yard map rendering

backend.pattern: Local HTTP and WebSocket server embedded in Tauri process

security.auth: Active Directory LDAP via ldap3 crate
security.transport: TLS 1.3 for TOS WebSocket, local HTTP unencrypted on loopback

reasoner_reviews.product: The live yard canvas is the core value proposition. Drag-and-drop truck assignment with optimistic UI is critical since dispatchers act on tight vessel berthing windows. The berth crane view should be read-only in MVP to avoid scope creep.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Tauri with Rust Axum is the right choice for a latency-sensitive desktop app. The Rust WebSocket handler should buffer TOS update bursts and apply debounced canvas re-renders capped at 10 FPS to avoid overwhelming the React render cycle. Konva.js layered canvas is better than SVG for hundreds of moving yard truck markers.
reasoner_reviews.security: AD LDAP authentication must bind per session and not cache credentials in SQLite. All TOS assignment writes must include dispatcher identity in the payload for the TOS audit trail. The local SQLite audit log should use WAL mode and be append-only from the application layer.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The TOS API rate limit of 100 calls/minute means the WebSocket subscription model is preferable to polling for real-time updates. Offline assignment queuing must replay in order when connectivity is restored.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The first revision plan lacked specificity on how Konva.js layers are structured for yard zones versus truck markers versus crane overlays. The rollback mechanism for rejected TOS assignments was described but not tied to the Zustand state model clearly.

frontend.app_structure: Tauri React app with route-free single-canvas layout; Zustand store holds immutable yard snapshot updated by WebSocket delta patches
frontend.pages_and_flows: YardCanvas (full-screen, primary view), BerthSchedulePanel (right drawer), ContainerStackModal (click-to-inspect overlay), AssignmentConfirmDialog
frontend.state_management: Zustand yard model: yard_trucks[], container_stacks[], crane_assignments[], pending_assignments[]; optimistic mutations applied immediately, rolled back on TOS rejection event
frontend.ui_modules: KonvaYardLayer (background zones), KonvaTruckLayer (animated markers), KonvaCraneLayer (berth crane overlays), DragDropAssignment hook using Konva drag events
frontend.performance_notes: Canvas capped at 10 FPS re-render; Zustand selector memoization to prevent full-tree re-renders on partial updates; batch WebSocket delta application before requestAnimationFrame

backend.service_design: Rust Axum local HTTP server on 127.0.0.1:7432; handles LDAP auth, TOS WebSocket proxy, SQLite audit writes, and assignment submission
backend.api_patterns: Local REST: POST /auth/login (LDAP bind), GET /yard/state (full snapshot), WS /yard/stream (delta feed), POST /assignments (submit to TOS with rollback on error)
backend.failure_handling: TOS WebSocket reconnect with exponential backoff; offline assignment queue in SQLite replayed on reconnection; TOS rejection events published back to frontend via local WS

security.auth_design: LDAP bind to AD on login; session token stored in Tauri secure store; token passed in Authorization header to local Axum server
security.audit_and_logging_controls: SQLite audit_log table records every assignment change with dispatcher_id, truck_id, from_zone, to_zone, tos_response, timestamp in WAL mode
security.security_risks: Credential caching in SQLite is prohibited; LDAP bind must occur per session start only

data.entities: yard_trucks (id, call_sign, zone_id, position_x, position_y, status, last_updated), container_stacks (id, bay, row, tier, container_id, iso_type, weight_kg), berth_cranes (id, berth_id, vessel_call_id, operator_id, current_move, status), vessel_calls (id, vessel_name, imo_number, berth_id, eta, etd), audit_log (id, dispatcher_id, action, entity_type, entity_id, payload_json, tos_result, ts)
data.storage_design: SQLite with WAL journal mode; yard state rebuilt from TOS on startup; audit_log append-only from application layer
data.data_risks: SQLite file must be on local NTFS with NTFS ACL restricting read to terminal operators group; no cloud sync

ISS-001.title: Konva layer architecture not specified for yard zones vs truck markers vs crane overlays
ISS-001.detail: First revision plan described Konva usage generically without defining which layers carry static zone backgrounds vs dynamic truck markers vs crane overlays, making it impossible to assess performance trade-offs.

history.note: Identified in architect_reasoner review

ISS-002.detail: Plan described optimistic UI rollback conceptually but did not specify which TOS WebSocket event triggers rollback nor how the pending_assignments queue entry is matched to the rejection.

history.note: Identified in critic review

previous_plan.title: Port Terminal Dispatcher Yard Visibility Desktop Application v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Tauri desktop application for port terminal dispatchers streaming live yard truck positions, container stack data, and crane assignments from the terminal operating system via WebSocket, rendered on a Konva.js HTML5 canvas with drag-and-drop assignment capabilities.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Tauri shell wrapping a React 18 frontend and a Rust Axum local HTTP/WebSocket server. The Axum server authenticates dispatchers via AD LDAP, maintains a WebSocket connection to the TOS, and proxies delta updates to the frontend. Yard state is held in Zustand with optimistic mutations.
previous_plan.workflows: Yard Update: TOS WebSocket pushes incremental position deltas; Axum buffers and forwards to frontend; Zustand applies patches and triggers debounced canvas re-render. Assignment: Dispatcher drags truck marker to zone; Zustand optimistic update applied; POST to Axum; Axum submits to TOS API; success confirms or rejection triggers rollback.
previous_plan.data_model: yard_trucks, container_stacks, berth_cranes, vessel_calls, audit_log tables in local SQLite.

target_output.thinking_summary: Resolving two medium issues from round 1: Konva layer hierarchy needs explicit definition with named layers and content assignment, and the Zustand rollback flow must be tied to the specific TOS assignment_nack event with correlation_id matching. The architecture is sound otherwise. Tauri+Rust Axum is the right call for a latency-sensitive on-premise desktop tool.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined three explicit Konva layers: ZoneLayer (static terminal zone polygons, re-rendered only on map config change), TruckLayer (animated yard_truck markers updated on each WebSocket delta batch, capped at 10 FPS via requestAnimationFrame throttle), CraneLayer (berth crane position overlays, updated on crane_move events only). Each layer is a separate Konva.Layer instance to isolate batchDraw calls.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Frontend engineers can implement three discrete Konva layers with clear update responsibilities and no ambiguity about z-ordering or re-render scope.

fix_report.action_taken: Documented TOS assignment_nack WebSocket event shape: {type: 'assignment_nack', correlation_id: string, reason: string, truck_id: string}. Zustand pending_assignments map keyed by correlation_id; on nack receipt, entry is dequeued and the truck marker position is reverted to the pre-drag snapshot stored alongside the pending entry.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Rollback flow is fully specified and directly implementable; no ambiguity about which event triggers rollback or how the pending entry is identified.

target_output.title: Port Terminal Dispatcher Yard Visibility Desktop Application v2
target_output.executive_summary: A Tauri desktop application for port terminal dispatchers that streams live yard truck movements, container stack positions, and berth crane assignments from the terminal operating system at up to 10 FPS, rendered on a three-layer Konva.js canvas with drag-and-drop truck assignment and TOS write-back with correlation-id-based optimistic rollback. Runs fully on-premise on Windows 10/11 workstations with Active Directory authentication and SQLite audit logging.
target_output.architecture_overview: Tauri shell hosting a React 18 TypeScript frontend and a Rust Axum process running on localhost:7432. The Axum server binds to AD LDAP on session start, opens a TLS 1.3 WebSocket to the terminal operating system, and streams delta update events to the React frontend via a local WebSocket on ws://127.0.0.1:7432/yard/stream. Yard state is maintained in a Zustand store as an immutable snapshot updated by delta patches. Three Konva.js layers handle static zone polygons, animated truck markers, and crane overlays independently to minimize re-render scope. All truck assignment submissions flow through Axum to the TOS API with correlation IDs enabling optimistic rollback on rejection.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Tauri 2.x, React 18, TypeScript, Konva.js 9 for canvas rendering, Zustand 4 for state, Tauri secure store for session token. Backend: Rust 1.77 with Axum 0.7, tokio-tungstenite for TOS WebSocket, ldap3 crate for AD bind, rusqlite with WAL mode. Build: GitHub Actions matrix build for Windows x64 with code-signing via signtool. No cloud dependencies for runtime operation.
target_output.functional_feature_map: YardCanvas: live bird's-eye yard map rendering yard_truck markers on TruckLayer, terminal zone polygons on ZoneLayer, berth crane positions on CraneLayer, updated from TOS WebSocket delta feed. DragDrop Assignment: dispatcher drags truck marker to target zone triggering optimistic Zustand update and TOS POST with correlation_id; assignment_nack event reverts marker to pre-drag position. ContainerStackInspector: click on stack position opens modal with bay/row/tier container list from local SQLite snapshot. BerthScheduleDrawer: right-panel displaying vessel_calls with ETA, ETD, berth, and crane count. AuditLog: append-only SQLite record of every assignment change with dispatcher identity and TOS outcome.
target_output.system_components: Tauri shell (Windows .exe installer, signed): hosts React renderer and Rust sidecar. ZoneLayer (Konva.Layer): static terminal zone polygons loaded from TOS config on startup, re-rendered only on zone config change events. TruckLayer (Konva.Layer): one KonvaCircle marker per yard_truck, position interpolated from WebSocket delta batches, batchDraw triggered by requestAnimationFrame at 10 FPS cap. CraneLayer (Konva.Layer): berth crane arm position overlays updated on crane_move events only. Axum Local Server: REST and WebSocket endpoints on 127.0.0.1:7432; manages AD LDAP session, TOS WebSocket upstream connection, and SQLite writes. TOS WebSocket Client (tokio-tungstenite): maintains persistent TLS 1.3 connection to terminal operating system, handles reconnect with exponential backoff up to 30s. SQLite DB (WAL mode): yard_trucks, container_stacks, berth_cranes, vessel_calls, pending_assignments, audit_log tables.
target_output.workflows: Live Yard Update: TOS pushes yard_position_update events containing truck_id, lat_offset, lng_offset, zone_id, timestamp; Axum buffers a 100ms window of events and publishes batched delta to frontend local WS; Zustand applies patch to yard_trucks slice; TruckLayer batchDraw triggered on next animation frame. Truck Assignment (optimistic): dispatcher drags KonvaTruck marker to target zone; useAssignment hook generates correlation_id, stores {correlation_id, truck_id, from_zone, pre_drag_position} in pending_assignments Zustand map, applies optimistic position update to TruckLayer; POST /assignments sent to Axum with {truck_id, target_zone, correlation_id, dispatcher_id}; Axum forwards to TOS API; on TOS 200 response Axum emits assignment_ack{correlation_id} to frontend, pending entry dequeued; on TOS error Axum emits assignment_nack{correlation_id, reason}, Zustand reverts truck position to pre_drag_position and dequeues pending entry; audit_log row written regardless of TOS outcome. Offline Queue Replay: on TOS WebSocket reconnect, Axum reads pending_assignments from SQLite ordered by created_at and replays submissions in sequence before resuming live stream.
target_output.data_model: yard_trucks (id TEXT PK, call_sign, zone_id, position_x REAL, position_y REAL, status TEXT, last_updated INTEGER). container_stacks (id TEXT PK, bay INT, row INT, tier INT, container_id TEXT, iso_type TEXT, weight_kg REAL, vessel_call_id TEXT). berth_cranes (id TEXT PK, berth_id TEXT, vessel_call_id TEXT, operator_id TEXT, arm_angle REAL, current_move TEXT, status TEXT). vessel_calls (id TEXT PK, vessel_name, imo_number, berth_id TEXT, eta INTEGER, etd INTEGER). pending_assignments (correlation_id TEXT PK, truck_id TEXT, from_zone TEXT, target_zone TEXT, pre_drag_x REAL, pre_drag_y REAL, created_at INTEGER). audit_log (id INTEGER PK, dispatcher_id TEXT, truck_id TEXT, from_zone TEXT, to_zone TEXT, correlation_id TEXT, tos_result TEXT, reason TEXT, ts INTEGER). All tables in SQLite with WAL journal mode.
target_output.api_design: Local REST/WS API on 127.0.0.1:7432. POST /auth/login {ldap_user, ldap_pass} -> {session_token, dispatcher_id, role}. GET /yard/state -> full YardSnapshot {yard_trucks[], container_stacks[], berth_cranes[], vessel_calls[]}. WS /yard/stream -> stream of {type: yard_position_update|crane_move|vessel_update|assignment_ack|assignment_nack, ...payload}. POST /assignments {truck_id, target_zone, correlation_id, dispatcher_id} -> 202 Accepted or 409 on TOS rejection. GET /audit-log?from=&to= -> paginated audit_log rows for supervisor review.
target_output.security_and_compliance: AD LDAP: ldap3 crate binds with dispatcher credentials on login; session token issued by Axum stored in Tauri secure store (Windows DPAPI); no credentials persisted in SQLite. TLS 1.3 enforced for TOS WebSocket upstream; local loopback traffic unencrypted as intranet-only. NTFS ACL on SQLite file restricts access to terminal_operators AD group. Audit log: every assignment change including TOS rejections written to append-only audit_log with dispatcher_id, preventing silent reassignment. No public network exposure; Axum binds to 127.0.0.1 only.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Build: GitHub Actions Windows runner cross-compiles Tauri .exe, signs with terminal CA certificate via signtool, uploads installer artifact. Distribution: installer copied to internal network share; workstations run setup.exe, Tauri installs to %APPDATA%. Updates: Tauri updater checks internal HTTP endpoint for new versions on startup. Environments: dev (developer workstation with TOS sandbox URL), staging (one dispatcher workstation with TOS UAT), production (10 dispatcher workstations per berth). No Docker, no cloud runtime dependency. Crash reports written to %LOCALAPPDATA%/terminal-dispatch/logs/ with structured JSON and rotation at 100 MB.
target_output.observability: Structured JSON file logs via tracing crate in Rust Axum and console.log forwarded to Tauri log plugin in React. Log levels: INFO for assignment submissions and TOS outcomes, WARN for reconnect attempts, ERROR for LDAP bind failures. Key operational signals: WebSocket reconnect count per session, TOS assignment rejection rate, canvas frame rate (logged every 60s), pending_assignments queue depth. Logs rotated daily, retained 30 days on workstation. No centralized log aggregation given intranet isolation.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No cloud runtime cost. Build pipeline on GitHub Actions: ~5 minutes per Windows build. Tauri binary ~15 MB installer. SQLite per-workstation with 90-day audit log retention stays under 500 MB. If multi-berth expansion requires a shared state server, migrate Axum to a dedicated intranet VM with the same Rust codebase and add a Redis pub/sub fan-out for multi-workstation delivery.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): AD LDAP auth, TOS WebSocket connection with three-layer Konva canvas (ZoneLayer, TruckLayer, CraneLayer), drag-and-drop assignment with correlation_id optimistic rollback, SQLite audit log, Windows installer via GitHub Actions. Phase 2 (6 weeks): ContainerStackInspector click-to-view modal, BerthScheduleDrawer with vessel ETA/ETD, offline assignment queue replay, Tauri auto-updater from internal HTTP endpoint. Phase 3 (8 weeks): Multi-berth canvas spanning full terminal, vessel call planning integration, shift handover PDF report generated from audit_log aggregation.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust: clippy with deny warnings, no unwrap() in production paths, all TOS API errors mapped to typed AppError enum. TypeScript: strict mode, Konva layer components as named exports, Zustand selectors memoized with shallow equality. Testing: Rust unit tests for LDAP bind mock and TOS event parser, React Testing Library for Zustand store reducers, Playwright for drag-and-drop assignment E2E on TOS sandbox. PR requires one senior review. Assignment correlation logic is covered by dedicated integration test simulating assignment_ack and assignment_nack events.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: TOS WebSocket instability during vessel operations peaks. Mitigation: Axum reconnect with exponential backoff, offline SQLite queue for assignment submissions, dispatcher UI shows disconnected banner. Risk: Konva canvas performance degradation when yard has 200+ simultaneous trucks. Mitigation: TruckLayer markers batched in single batchDraw call, 10 FPS cap, position interpolation smooths jumpy updates. Tradeoff: Tauri over Electron reduces memory footprint from ~300 MB to ~50 MB for a dispatcher workstation with limited RAM. Tradeoff: SQLite over no persistence accepts a 5 MB/day write rate for audit log in exchange for offline queue reliability.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Konva.js chosen over plain SVG for canvas rendering to handle 200+ animated markers without DOM node overhead. SQLite WAL mode chosen over journal mode for concurrent read safety between Axum writer and diagnostic reader. TOS assignment correlation via correlation_id UUID generated client-side to avoid server round-trip for optimistic UI. LDAP bind on session start only, not per-request, to respect TOS rate limit budget.

metadata.notes: Port terminal dispatcher desktop app with Tauri+Rust, three-layer Konva canvas, and correlation-id optimistic rollback resolved in round 2.

profile.domain: Trade Compliance
