previous_plan.title: Regional Parcel Carrier Tracking Site - Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A static Astro 4 site on Vercel Edge Network for consumer parcel tracking lookups and merchant widget embedding, fetching live status from the carrier tracking API client-side.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Astro 4 static build deployed to Vercel CDN. Tracking page fetches carrier API directly from browser. Vanilla JS widget bundled separately for merchant embed.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: GDPR cookie consent banner displayed. GA4 analytics. Content Security Policy headers. CORS dependency on carrier API not addressed.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: Vercel auto-deploy on merge to main. Preview deploys on PRs. No bundle size check in CI pipeline.

target_output.thinking_summary: Fixing ISS-001 by introducing a Vercel Edge Function as a thin proxy for carrier API calls, eliminating the CORS dependency while keeping the static site architecture intact. Fixing ISS-002 by explicitly documenting that GA4 gtag.js load is deferred until the consent callback fires. The static_website constraint means no server-side database or backend runtime; the Edge Function is a fetch proxy only, not a stateful backend. Widget bundle size enforcement in CI also addressed per audit recommendation.

fix_report.action_taken: Added a Vercel Edge Function at /api/track that proxies browser requests to the carrier tracking API server-side, eliminating CORS dependency. Edge Function adds carrier API key from Vercel environment variable, not exposed to browser. Documented in architecture_overview and system_components.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Tracking lookups work from any browser origin regardless of carrier API CORS policy; API key not exposed in browser requests

fix_report.action_taken: Explicitly documented that GA4 gtag.js script tag is not loaded until the consent accept callback fires; consent state stored in localStorage; page load proceeds without GA4 for users who decline or have not been prompted. Documented in security_and_compliance and workflows sections.
fix_report.expected_outcome: GA4 is functionally blocked before consent, satisfying GDPR ePrivacy requirements for EU visitors

target_output.title: Regional Parcel Carrier Tracking Site
target_output.executive_summary: A static Astro 4 website deployed to Vercel Edge Network that gives consumers a live parcel tracking timeline and delivers an embeddable vanilla JS widget for e-commerce merchants to display carrier tracking status inline on their order confirmation pages. Tracking data is fetched via a lightweight Vercel Edge Function proxy that handles the carrier API key server-side, eliminating CORS dependencies. GDPR consent gates GA4 analytics initialization for EU visitors. The widget bundle is enforced under 50KB gzipped by CI bundlesize checks.
target_output.architecture_overview: Astro 4 generates a fully static HTML/CSS build at deploy time, served from Vercel Edge Network with global CDN distribution and automatic HTTPS. Consumer tracking lookups submit a tracking number to a client-side fetch call targeting a Vercel Edge Function at /api/track, which proxies the request to the regional carrier tracking API with the API key injected from a Vercel environment variable, returning normalized JSON to the browser. This eliminates any CORS dependency on the external carrier API. The merchant embed widget is a separate vanilla JS bundle loaded as a script tag; it calls the same /api/track Edge Function using the merchant carrier account token passed as a data attribute on the script tag. Google Analytics 4 is loaded only after explicit consent is received via the consent callback, not on initial page load.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Astro 4 with Tailwind CSS for static pages; vanilla JavaScript ES2022 for embeddable widget. Proxy: Vercel Edge Function (Web API compatible, no Node.js runtime) for carrier API proxy. Analytics: Google Analytics 4 with deferred consent-gated initialization. Build tooling: Rollup for widget bundle with bundlesize enforcement. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with Vercel CLI and Lighthouse CI. Hosting: Vercel Edge Network with automatic global CDN and HTTPS.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Tracking Lookup Page: consumer submits tracking number via search form, page fetches /api/track Edge Function, renders DeliveryTimeline component with event markers, current location, and ETA window; exception banners render for delay, failed delivery attempt, and address issue events. Merchant Embed Widget: script tag with data-token and data-branding attributes loads tracking-widget.js; widget renders inline tracking timeline using same /api/track proxy with merchant account token; supports custom logo URL and accent color via data attributes. GDPR Consent Banner: displayed on first visit to EU locale visitors; accepts or declines analytics; defers GA4 script injection until accept callback. Shareable Tracking URL: /track/{number} route renders pre-populated tracking page for direct link sharing from notification emails.
target_output.system_components: Vercel Edge Network: global CDN serving Astro static build; automatic SSL; handles 10M+ monthly requests on Enterprise plan. Vercel Edge Function /api/track: Web API fetch proxy adding carrier API key header from environment, normalizing carrier response to canonical tracking event schema, returning JSON. Carrier Tracking API (external): regional carrier REST API providing shipment status events and ETA data; accessed only from Edge Function server-side. Astro 4 Static Build: pre-rendered HTML pages and asset bundles deployed to Vercel on merge to main. Widget Bundle (tracking-widget.js): Rollup-built vanilla JS under 50KB gzipped; fetches /api/track with merchant token; renders inline HTML timeline. Google Analytics 4: loaded via dynamic script injection only after consent callback; event tracking for lookup and widget interactions.
target_output.workflows: Consumer Tracking Lookup: consumer types tracking number into search form on home page; browser submits GET /api/track?number={n}&carrier={c} to Vercel Edge Function; Edge Function adds API key header and fetches carrier REST endpoint; normalizes response to {tracking_number, carrier, origin, destination, current_status, estimated_delivery_window, events[]}; browser renders DeliveryTimeline Astro island with event markers and exception banner if delay_type is present. Merchant Widget Embed: merchant adds script tag with src=/tracking-widget.js?token={account_token} to order confirmation page; widget JS runs on DOMContentLoaded, reads data-tracking-number from parent element, fetches /api/track?number={n}&token={account_token}; renders inline timeline HTML with merchant branding from data-logo-url and data-accent-color attributes. GDPR Consent Flow: on first page load for EU locale visitors (detected via Accept-Language), consent banner injects into DOM; if user clicks Accept, localStorage.setItem('ga_consent', 'granted') and GA4 gtag.js script tag is dynamically appended to document head; if user declines, localStorage.setItem('ga_consent', 'denied') and no analytics script is loaded for the session.
target_output.data_model: No server-side data model. Client-side data structures: TrackingResponse {tracking_number: string, carrier: string, origin_city: string, destination_city: string, current_status: enum in_transit/out_for_delivery/delivered/exception/pending, estimated_delivery_window: {earliest: ISO8601, latest: ISO8601}, events: TrackingEvent[]}. TrackingEvent {timestamp: ISO8601, location_city: string, location_state: string, event_code: string, event_description: string, is_exception: boolean}. MerchantEmbedConfig {account_token: string, logo_url: string | null, accent_color: string, locale: string} -- passed as script data attributes, never stored. GA4 event schema: tracking_lookup_submitted {source: direct|merchant_embed, number_length: int}, tracking_result_viewed {has_exception: bool, carrier_name: string}, widget_loaded {merchant_domain: string}.
target_output.api_design: Vercel Edge Function: GET /api/track?number={tracking_number}&carrier={carrier_code}&token={optional_merchant_token}. Response: 200 {tracking_number, carrier, current_status, estimated_delivery_window, events[]}. 400 {error: 'invalid_tracking_number'} if format validation fails. 404 {error: 'not_found'} if carrier API returns no record. 429 rate limit response if IP exceeds 60 requests per minute. No authentication for consumer lookups; merchant token passed as query param for account-scoped rate limiting at carrier API. No other API endpoints; all other pages are static HTML served from CDN.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Carrier API key stored as Vercel environment variable, injected into Edge Function at runtime; never exposed to browser. Content Security Policy header: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'nonce-{deploy-nonce}' https://www.googletagmanager.com; connect-src 'self'; preventing XSS script injection. GDPR compliance: GA4 gtag.js script tag is NOT present in the static HTML; it is dynamically injected via JavaScript only when localStorage ga_consent equals 'granted' after explicit accept callback; consent banner displayed on first load for Accept-Language headers matching EU locales. Merchant account tokens are low-privilege identifiers scoped to rate limiting in the carrier API; token rotation documented in merchant onboarding guide. HTTPS enforced by Vercel Edge for all origins; HSTS header set with max-age 31536000.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: on every PR, run bun run build to produce Astro static output, run Rollup to build tracking-widget.js, enforce bundlesize check (widget < 50KB gzipped), run Lighthouse CI against Vercel preview URL with CWV regression gates (LCP < 2.5s, CLS < 0.1). Vercel auto-deploys preview per PR and production on merge to main. Vercel environment variables: CARRIER_API_KEY (production), CARRIER_API_KEY_STAGING (staging). Rollback: Vercel instant rollback to prior deployment via CLI or dashboard. Monitoring: Vercel Analytics for CWV, GA4 for user interactions; synthetic tracking lookup test via Vercel cron every 5 minutes using known valid tracking number, alerting on non-200 response from /api/track.
target_output.observability: Vercel Analytics: real-time Core Web Vitals per page (LCP, CLS, FID); geographic request distribution. Google Analytics 4 (consent-gated): custom events tracking_lookup_submitted, tracking_result_viewed (has_exception dimension), widget_loaded (merchant_domain dimension), consent_accepted, consent_rejected. Vercel Edge Function logs: tracking API response latency, carrier API error rates, 429 rate limit events per IP. Synthetic monitoring: Vercel cron job every 5 minutes calls /api/track with a test tracking number and alerts on non-200 or response latency over 2 seconds via webhook to Slack.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vercel Pro plan at $20/month supports 1M Edge Function invocations and 100GB bandwidth, covering expected consumer traffic. Widget adoption by multiple merchants may push Edge Function invocations toward the included limit; Vercel scales automatically with pay-per-invocation pricing above included tiers. GA4 is free. Carrier API costs governed by the carrier's API pricing contract, not this project. No database or server infrastructure costs.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (3 weeks): Tracking lookup page with /api/track Edge Function proxy, DeliveryTimeline component rendering event list and exception banner, GDPR consent banner with GA4 deferred init, Vercel deploy pipeline with bundlesize CI check. Phase 2 (2 weeks): Embeddable merchant widget with branding configuration (logo, accent color), shareable /track/{number} URL, Lighthouse CI CWV gates, synthetic monitoring cron. Phase 3 (ongoing): Merchant onboarding documentation, A/B testing of timeline UI variants via Vercel Edge config feature flags, support for additional carrier codes in /api/track proxy normalization.
target_output.development_guidelines: Astro component islands used sparingly: only TrackingTimeline uses client-side hydration; all other components are server-rendered at build time. Widget bundle enforces zero-dependency policy: no npm packages in widget entry point; date formatting via Intl.DateTimeFormat, icons as inline SVG. All /api/track Edge Function changes require integration test against staging carrier API before merge. GDPR consent logic reviewed by legal on any changes to GA4 initialization flow. Vercel environment variables documented in project README with staging/production naming convention.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Carrier tracking API rate limits affecting consumer lookup throughput if request volume spikes. Mitigation: Vercel Edge Function can implement response caching with short TTL (30 seconds) for repeated lookups of the same tracking number. Tradeoff: Vercel Edge Function proxy adds 20-50ms latency compared to direct browser fetch; acceptable given it eliminates CORS dependency and hides API key. Risk: Carrier API schema changes breaking the normalization layer in the Edge Function. Mitigation: integration test suite validates against known tracking responses on every CI run. Risk: Widget merchant token enumeration by third parties. Mitigation: tokens are rate-limited at carrier API; token rotation capability provided to merchants.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Vercel Edge Function proxy over direct browser fetch to eliminate CORS dependency; edge function adds negligible latency. Decided against Astro SSR mode to maintain pure static architecture with CDN benefits. Selected vanilla JS for widget over a Web Component to maximize browser compatibility including older merchant site environments.

metadata.notes: Static website with Vercel Edge Function proxy resolving CORS issue; GDPR consent gate explicitly blocking GA4 init; strong revision addressing all focus issues.

profile.domain: Distributed VRP Solver Benchmarking Research Prototype

project_goal.value: Benchmark genetic algorithms against reinforcement learning agents for the vehicle routing problem as fleet size scales from 50 to 5,000 vehicles, identifying algorithmic scalability breakpoints and producing a research report
project_goal.rationale: Core research objective: determine which algorithm family degrades first under urban same-day delivery fleet scale

target_users.value: Logistics research team benchmarking VRP solver algorithms for an upcoming academic publication
target_users.rationale: Internal research consumers; not production-facing

project_class.rationale: Experimental benchmarking prototype; not production deployment; disposable after research publication

capabilities.rationale: Ray-distributed algorithm execution, DuckDB result storage, MLflow experiment tracking, batch benchmark runs

complexity_level.rationale: Distributed Ray execution across EC2 spot instances; RL agent training at scale; statistical significance testing

risk_level.rationale: Research prototype; no production traffic; data loss means re-running experiments, not customer impact

data_sensitivity.rationale: Synthetic routing scenarios; no real customer addresses, driver PII, or commercial route data

external_exposure.rationale: Runs on internal AWS account; results shared via MLflow and DuckDB exports to research team only

access_model.value: AWS IAM roles for EC2 and S3; no user authentication; research team accesses MLflow UI via VPN
access_model.rationale: Internal research tool with no external user access

feature_scope.value: Configurable benchmark runner for GA and RL agents, fleet size sweep 50-5000 vehicles, DuckDB result storage, MLflow experiment tracking, statistical significance analysis, scalability breakpoint charts
feature_scope.rationale: Complete feature set for research publication quality results

mvp_scope.value: GA benchmark runner at fleet sizes 50, 500, 5000; DuckDB result storage; basic scalability charts
mvp_scope.rationale: Validates infrastructure before RL agent integration

security_baseline.value: AWS IAM roles; no public endpoints; S3 bucket private; EC2 instances in private subnet
security_baseline.rationale: Internal research compute with standard AWS security hygiene

frontend_stack.rationale: No UI; researchers access MLflow via its built-in web UI; charts generated as static PNG/HTML via matplotlib and Plotly

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Ray 2.9 for distributed execution; custom benchmark runner scripts
backend_stack.rationale: Ray provides distributed task scheduling across EC2 spot fleet with fault tolerance

data_platform.value: DuckDB for benchmark results; S3 for raw run artifacts and checkpoints; MLflow for experiment tracking
data_platform.rationale: DuckDB provides fast in-process analytics on benchmark datasets without a running database server

hosting_target.value: AWS EC2 spot instances (c5.4xlarge) managed by Ray autoscaler
hosting_target.rationale: Spot instances minimize cost for compute-intensive benchmark runs; Ray autoscaler handles instance lifecycle

privacy_retention_policy.value: Benchmark results retained in S3 for 1 year after publication; no personal data processed
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Research reproducibility requires result retention; no privacy obligations given synthetic data

future_scope.value: Extended to test metaheuristic variants (simulated annealing, ant colony); potential open-source release of benchmark framework
future_scope.rationale: Post-publication roadmap

constraints.value: Total benchmark compute budget under $3,000; all experiments must be reproducible via seed-based RNG; RL agent implementation must use stable-baselines3 or RLlib, not custom RL from scratch
constraints.rationale: Budget and reproducibility constraints for academic publication standards

observability_baseline.value: MLflow experiment tracking for all run metrics; structured JSON logs per benchmark run; no production monitoring required
observability_baseline.rationale: Research observability focused on experiment reproducibility and result comparison

execution_preference.value: Iterative: GA benchmark first, RL integration second, statistical analysis third
execution_preference.rationale: Reduces risk by validating distributed infra before adding RL complexity

llm_integration.value: LLM (GPT-4o) used for automated analysis of RL agent policy trajectories: summarizes why solution quality degrades at fleet size breakpoints by interpreting reward curves and policy gradient norms
llm_integration.rationale: LLM interpretability analysis supplements quantitative metrics with qualitative policy behavior summaries for the research paper

project.goal: Distributed VRP algorithm scalability benchmarking for research publication

backend.framework: Ray 2.9 for distributed benchmark execution; RLlib for RL agents; DEAP for genetic algorithms

security.auth: AWS IAM roles
security.network: EC2 in private subnet; S3 bucket private

reasoner_reviews.product: The research scope is well-defined: fleet size sweep from 50 to 5000 with both algorithm families is a credible methodology for identifying scalability breakpoints. The LLM policy analysis is an interesting addition but must not substitute for quantitative statistical significance testing in the publication. Result reproducibility via seeded RNG is non-negotiable for academic credibility.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Ray on EC2 spot is appropriate for distributed benchmark execution but spot instance preemption is a real risk for long-running RL training runs at fleet size 5000. Ray's fault tolerance requires checkpointing RL agent state to S3 at configurable intervals so preempted workers can resume. The DuckDB approach is correct for in-process analytics on benchmark results but the database file must be on EBS or S3-backed storage, not ephemeral instance storage.
reasoner_reviews.security: Research prototype with no external exposure and synthetic data has minimal security requirements. Standard AWS hygiene: IAM roles not keys, private S3 bucket, EC2 in private subnet. MLflow UI exposed on internal VPN only.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Budget of $3,000 is achievable with spot instances but requires careful fleet size sweep scheduling: run cheapest configurations first to validate pipeline, then scale up. RL training at fleet size 5000 is the most expensive run and should be last.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Rounds 1 and 2 both failed to specify how DuckDB benchmark results are persisted across spot instance preemption. The current plan implies DuckDB runs on the Ray head node with local file storage, which would lose all results on preemption. This was flagged in ISS-001 two rounds ago and still appears unresolved in the data model section.

backend.service_design: Ray remote functions for benchmark task distribution: each fleet-size/algorithm/seed combination dispatched as a Ray task to a worker; results returned as structured dicts and written to DuckDB on the head node. LLM analysis invoked as a post-processing step after all runs for a fleet size tier complete.
backend.background_jobs: Fleet size sweep orchestrated by a main benchmark runner script: iterates over fleet_sizes=[50,100,250,500,1000,2500,5000] x algorithms=[genetic_algorithm, rl_ppo, rl_dqn] x seeds=[0..9], dispatches Ray tasks, collects results, writes to DuckDB, and triggers LLM analysis summary per tier.
backend.failure_handling: Ray actor checkpointing for RL agents every 100 training episodes to S3; GA state checkpointed after each generation batch. On spot preemption Ray head node detects worker failure and reschedules tasks on replacement workers using stored checkpoints.
backend.scaling_notes: Ray autoscaler configured with min 1 head node (on-demand c5.2xlarge) and 0-20 spot c5.4xlarge workers; scales out on pending task queue; scales in after 10 minutes idle. Budget guardrail: boto3 script checks monthly EC2 spend before each fleet size tier and halts if approaching $3,000 limit.

security.auth_design: AWS IAM role assigned to EC2 head node for S3 read/write and CloudWatch log write; worker nodes assume same role via instance profile. No user-facing authentication.
security.secrets_management: OpenAI API key for GPT-4o LLM analysis stored in AWS Secrets Manager; fetched by head node at runtime via boto3; not stored in code or environment variables in plaintext.
security.security_risks: Spot instance preemption can interrupt RL training runs, losing in-flight state if checkpointing interval is too long; mitigated by 100-episode checkpoint cadence.

data.entities: optimization_runs (run_id UUID, algorithm ENUM genetic_algorithm/rl_ppo/rl_dqn, fleet_size INT, seed INT, iteration INT, solution_quality FLOAT, wall_clock_seconds FLOAT, cpu_core_seconds FLOAT, converged BOOL, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). benchmark_scenarios (scenario_id, fleet_size, num_deliveries, depot_location, delivery_coordinates_json, demand_weights_json, seed INT). llm_analysis_results (fleet_size, algorithm, analysis_text TEXT, model_version, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). experiment_metadata (run_group_id, git_commit, ray_version, algorithm_hyperparams_json, created_at).
data.storage_design: DuckDB file stored on EBS gp3 volume mounted to head node at /data/benchmark.duckdb for persistence across spot replacements of workers; head node on on-demand instance to avoid DuckDB file loss. S3 bucket for RL agent checkpoints (rl-checkpoints/{run_id}/episode_{n}.pkl), GA population snapshots, and final result exports (Parquet).
data.analytics_events: Post-run analysis queries: per-algorithm solution quality vs fleet_size curve, wall_clock_seconds regression by fleet_size tier, convergence rate by algorithm at each fleet size, statistical t-tests between GA and RL quality distributions per fleet_size.

devops.deployment_topology: Ray cluster: 1 on-demand c5.2xlarge head node running MLflow server, DuckDB on EBS, and benchmark orchestrator; 0-20 spot c5.4xlarge workers. Ray autoscaler config in YAML checked into repo.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: run pytest unit tests for benchmark runner, GA fitness function, and result storage on every PR. No CD pipeline; researchers deploy manually via ray up cluster.yaml.
devops.observability_stack: MLflow tracking server on head node logs: run_id, algorithm, fleet_size, seed, per-iteration solution_quality and wall_clock_seconds. DuckDB query outputs exported as CSV for statistical analysis scripts. CloudWatch logs for Ray head node and worker stderr.
devops.rollback_strategy: No deployment rollback needed; failed experiments re-run from checkpointed state. DuckDB file backed up to S3 daily via cron on head node.

ISS-001.title: DuckDB persistence not protected against head node loss
ISS-001.detail: Rounds 1 and 2 described DuckDB on the head node without specifying whether the file is on persistent EBS or ephemeral instance storage. If the head node is spot instance and preempted, all benchmark results are lost. Head node should be on-demand and DuckDB on EBS.

history.note: Identified: DuckDB storage medium unspecified
Round 2 plan still did not specify EBS vs ephemeral storage for head node

ISS-002.title: RL agent checkpoint interval not specified
ISS-002.detail: Both prior rounds mentioned spot preemption risk but did not specify the RL agent checkpoint interval or the S3 path structure for checkpoint storage, leaving the recovery mechanism undefined.

history.note: Checkpoint interval unspecified
Still no checkpoint interval documented

ISS-003.title: Budget guardrail mechanism not implemented
ISS-003.detail: Budget constraint of $3,000 noted in contract but no mechanism specified for halting runs before budget is exhausted.

history.note: No budget guardrail described in deployment or backend sections

focus_issues.title: DuckDB persistence not protected against head node loss
focus_issues.detail: DuckDB file storage medium unspecified across two rounds; head node must be on-demand with EBS volume.

focus_issues.title: RL agent checkpoint interval not specified
focus_issues.detail: Spot preemption recovery depends on checkpoint interval and S3 path structure, neither documented.

target_output.title: Distributed VRP Solver Benchmarking Prototype
target_output.executive_summary: A Ray-distributed research prototype running on AWS EC2 that benchmarks genetic algorithm and reinforcement learning approaches to the vehicle routing problem across fleet sizes from 50 to 5,000 urban delivery vehicles. Benchmark results are stored in DuckDB on a persistent EBS gp3 volume on the on-demand Ray head node, with RL agent state checkpointed every 100 training episodes to S3 for spot preemption recovery. MLflow tracks all experiment runs for reproducibility. A GPT-4o LLM analysis step interprets RL policy gradient behavior at scalability breakpoints for the research report.
target_output.architecture_overview: Ray 2.9 cluster with one on-demand c5.2xlarge head node and 0-20 spot c5.4xlarge worker nodes managed by the Ray autoscaler. The benchmark orchestrator runs on the head node, dispatching fleet_size x algorithm x seed combinations as Ray remote tasks. Workers execute GA generations using DEAP or RL training episodes using RLlib PPO/DQN. RL workers checkpoint model state every 100 episodes to s3://benchmark-artifacts/rl-checkpoints/{run_id}/episode_{n}.pkl; on worker respawn Ray restores from the latest checkpoint. Completed run results are written to DuckDB at /data/benchmark.duckdb on the head node's EBS gp3 50GB volume. MLflow tracking server runs on the head node and logs per-iteration solution quality, wall-clock time, and hyperparameters. Post-run LLM analysis queries OpenAI GPT-4o with reward curve summaries and policy gradient norms to generate interpretability commentary for the research report. Note: this architecture incorrectly states the total compute budget as $2,500 rather than the contracted $3,000.
target_output.technology_stack: Distributed execution: Python 3.12 with Ray 2.9 and Ray autoscaler on EC2. Genetic algorithm: DEAP 1.4 with configurable population size and crossover operators. Reinforcement learning: RLlib 2.9 with PPO and DQN agents; stable-baselines3 as fallback for single-node runs. Result storage: DuckDB 0.10 on EBS gp3 volume. Experiment tracking: MLflow 2.12 on head node. LLM analysis: OpenAI GPT-4o via Python openai SDK; API key in AWS Secrets Manager. Visualization: matplotlib and Plotly for scalability breakpoint charts. CI: GitHub Actions with pytest.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Benchmark Runner: iterates fleet_sizes=[50,100,250,500,1000,2500,5000] x algorithms=[genetic_algorithm,rl_ppo,rl_dqn] x seeds=range(10); dispatches Ray tasks; collects structured results; writes to DuckDB optimization_runs table. GA Engine: DEAP-based implementation with configurable population, crossover rate, mutation rate; tournament selection; returns best tour cost and convergence generation per run. RL Engine: RLlib PPO and DQN agents training on per-fleet VRP environment; checkpoints every 100 episodes to S3; returns final solution quality and training wall-clock time. Statistical Analysis: DuckDB queries computing mean, std, and 95% CI for solution quality per algorithm x fleet_size tier; t-tests between GA and RL distributions. LLM Policy Analysis: GPT-4o receives reward curve CSV and policy gradient norm summary per fleet-size tier; returns structured interpretability text logged to llm_analysis_results table. Budget Guardrail: boto3 Cost Explorer query before each fleet-size tier; halts runner if monthly EC2 spend approaches $2,800.
target_output.system_components: Ray Head Node (on-demand c5.2xlarge): benchmark orchestrator process, MLflow tracking server, DuckDB at /data/benchmark.duckdb on EBS gp3 50GB, boto3 budget check script, daily S3 backup cron for DuckDB file. Ray Spot Workers (c5.4xlarge, 0-20): execute GA and RL benchmark tasks; RL workers checkpoint to S3 every 100 episodes; workers respawn from checkpoint on preemption. S3 Bucket benchmark-artifacts: RL checkpoints at rl-checkpoints/{run_id}/episode_{n}.pkl, GA population snapshots, Parquet result exports, daily DuckDB backups. MLflow Server: localhost:5000 on head node, accessible via SSH tunnel or VPN. AWS Secrets Manager: OPENAI_API_KEY fetched at runtime by LLM analysis step.
target_output.workflows: Fleet Size Sweep: benchmark_runner.py iterates tier by tier; before each tier invokes boto3 Cost Explorer check and aborts if spend >= $2,800; for each algorithm x seed dispatches Ray remote task; GA tasks run DEAP optimization loop returning best_cost, generations_to_converge, wall_clock_s; RL tasks run RLlib training loop with 100-episode S3 checkpoints, returning final_solution_quality, training_episodes, wall_clock_s; results written to DuckDB optimization_runs. Preemption Recovery: spot worker preempted; Ray head node detects failure; autoscaler spawns replacement worker; RL Ray actor resumes from latest S3 checkpoint at rl-checkpoints/{run_id}/episode_{max_n}.pkl; GA task re-dispatched from last population snapshot. LLM Analysis: after all runs for a fleet_size tier complete, analysis_runner.py queries DuckDB for reward curves and policy gradient norm stats per algorithm, formats CSV prompt, sends to GPT-4o with system prompt asking for scalability interpretation, stores response in llm_analysis_results table with model_version and token count.
target_output.data_model: optimization_runs (run_id UUID PK, algorithm ENUM genetic_algorithm/rl_ppo/rl_dqn, fleet_size INT, seed INT, iteration INT, solution_quality FLOAT, wall_clock_seconds FLOAT, cpu_core_seconds FLOAT, converged BOOL, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ) stored in DuckDB on EBS. benchmark_scenarios (scenario_id UUID, fleet_size INT, num_deliveries INT, depot_lat FLOAT, depot_lon FLOAT, delivery_coords_json JSON, demand_weights_json JSON, seed INT). llm_analysis_results (analysis_id UUID, fleet_size INT, algorithm VARCHAR, analysis_text TEXT, model_version VARCHAR, prompt_tokens INT, completion_tokens INT, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). Note: experiment_metadata table tracking git_commit and hyperparameter versioning per run_group is absent from this schema, creating a reproducibility gap for academic publication requirements.
target_output.api_design: No external API. Internal CLI interface: python benchmark_runner.py --fleet-sizes 50,500,5000 --algorithms ga,rl_ppo --seeds 0-9 --output-db /data/benchmark.duckdb. python analysis_runner.py --fleet-size 5000 --algorithm rl_ppo --run-group-id {uuid} invokes LLM analysis for a completed tier. MLflow UI accessible on head node port 5000 via SSH tunnel. DuckDB result files exported to Parquet via python export_results.py --output-dir s3://benchmark-artifacts/results/ for sharing with research team.
target_output.security_and_compliance: AWS IAM instance profile on head node grants S3 read/write to benchmark-artifacts bucket and Secrets Manager read for OPENAI_API_KEY; principle of least privilege. EC2 instances in private subnet with no inbound internet access; outbound via NAT gateway for AWS API calls and OpenAI API. S3 bucket private with no public ACLs; bucket policy restricts access to benchmark IAM role. SSH key-based access for researchers via bastion host or AWS Systems Manager Session Manager. No user PII or sensitive commercial data processed; synthetic routing scenarios generated from parameterized distributions.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Ray cluster deployment: ray up cluster.yaml provisions head node (on-demand c5.2xlarge) with EBS gp3 50GB volume at /data; workers configured as spot c5.4xlarge max 20. Ray autoscaler scales workers based on pending task queue; idles workers after 10 minutes of no tasks. DuckDB backup: daily cron on head node copies /data/benchmark.duckdb to s3://benchmark-artifacts/backups/benchmark_$(date +%Y%m%d).duckdb. MLflow server started on head node via screen session. Budget guardrail: before each fleet-size tier, boto3 queries AWS Cost Explorer for current month EC2 spend; if spend >= $2,800 benchmark runner logs warning and exits cleanly. Researchers run experiments manually via SSH; no CD pipeline. GitHub Actions runs pytest on every PR to catch benchmark runner and result storage regressions.
target_output.observability: MLflow experiment tracking: each optimization_run logged as an MLflow run with fleet_size, algorithm, seed tags and solution_quality, wall_clock_seconds, converged metrics per iteration. DuckDB query scripts produce scalability breakpoint charts as Plotly HTML files exported to S3. CloudWatch Logs: Ray head node and worker stderr captured via CloudWatch agent for debugging preemption recovery. Budget tracker: boto3 Cost Explorer query results logged to DuckDB cost_tracking table after each tier for post-hoc spend analysis. LLM analysis token counts logged to llm_analysis_results for API cost monitoring.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated total compute: $2,500 for the full sweep (note: this figure contradicts the $3,000 contract budget constraint). Spot c5.4xlarge at approximately $0.25/hour; full 7-tier x 3-algorithm x 10-seed sweep estimated at 8,000 core-hours. Budget guardrail halts execution at $2,800 monthly spend. On-demand head node at $0.17/hour for 90 days approximately $370. S3 storage and data transfer negligible. OpenAI GPT-4o analysis estimated at $50-100 for all tiers.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (3 weeks): GA benchmark runner with DEAP at fleet sizes 50, 500, 5000; DuckDB on EBS with daily S3 backup; MLflow tracking; basic scalability charts. Phase 2 (4 weeks): RL agents (RLlib PPO and DQN) with 100-episode S3 checkpoint recovery; budget guardrail; full 7-tier fleet size sweep. Phase 3 (3 weeks): Statistical significance testing (t-tests and confidence intervals); LLM policy analysis via GPT-4o; Plotly scalability breakpoint chart generation; Parquet result export for publication data supplement.
target_output.development_guidelines: All benchmark runs use seed-based RNG: numpy.random.seed(run_seed) and torch.manual_seed(run_seed) set at task start for reproducibility. DuckDB writes use INSERT OR IGNORE with run_id UUID to handle Ray task retry duplicates without corrupting result counts. RL checkpoints versioned by episode number; recovery logic selects max episode checkpoint present in S3. DEAP fitness function unit-tested with fixed-seed scenarios to detect regression across code changes. OpenAI API calls wrapped with tenacity retry for rate limit and timeout handling.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: RL training at fleet_size=5000 may exceed $2,800 budget guardrail before completing all seeds. Mitigation: schedule fleet_size=5000 runs last; if budget runs out, reduce seed count from 10 to 5 for this tier only, with statistical power analysis confirming adequacy. Risk: Spot instance availability in chosen AWS region insufficient for 20 concurrent workers during high-demand periods. Mitigation: Ray autoscaler configured with fallback to c5.2xlarge if c5.4xlarge capacity unavailable. Tradeoff: DuckDB on a single EBS volume is a single point of failure for result persistence; mitigated by daily S3 backup but up to 24 hours of results could be lost if EBS fails between backups. Risk: GPT-4o policy interpretation may produce inconsistent analysis across runs if prompt is not tightly structured; mitigation: standardized CSV format prompt with explicit output JSON schema.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected RLlib over stable-baselines3 for RL agents due to native Ray integration enabling distributed rollout workers at fleet_size 5000. Selected DuckDB over SQLite for result storage due to 10x faster analytical query performance on optimization_runs table with 700K rows (7 tiers x 3 algorithms x 10 seeds x 3000 iterations). Decided GPT-4o over GPT-4-turbo for LLM analysis due to larger context window accommodating full reward curve CSV inputs.

metadata.notes: Intentionally flawed: data_model omits experiment_metadata table required for git_commit reproducibility tracing; cost_and_scaling states $2,500 budget contradicting the $3,000 contract constraint.

profile.domain: Small Freight Broker Transportation Management Web App

project_goal.value: Automate carrier selection and load tendering for small freight brokers, replacing manual email and phone tendering workflows to reduce operating cost per load and compress carrier response time from hours to minutes
project_goal.rationale: Core operational efficiency goal: automating tender dispatch eliminates dispatcher hours spent on phone and email

target_users.value: Freight brokers (dispatchers, operations managers) and carrier representatives confirming or declining tenders
target_users.rationale: Dispatchers are primary power users; carriers are partner-facing recipients of electronic tender offers

project_class.rationale: Browser-based TMS for dispatchers with partner-facing carrier tender portal

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, analytics, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Full-stack web app with EDI integrations, margin analytics, and broker admin panel

complexity_level.rationale: Core load management and EDI tender dispatch with analytics; no AI or real-time streaming

risk_level.rationale: Financial transaction data for load payments; FMCSA carrier authority verification required

data_sensitivity.rationale: Load payment amounts, carrier rate confirmations, and broker margin data are financial records

external_exposure.rationale: Carrier tender response portal is partner-facing; only authenticated broker users and verified carriers access the system

access_model.value: JWT authentication for broker users; carrier DUNS-linked accounts with email-verified registration; RBAC: dispatcher, operations manager, admin roles for broker side
access_model.rationale: Multi-tenant with strict carrier data isolation; broker roles have different load management permissions

feature_scope.value: Load entry and management, automated carrier ranking by lane history, EDI 204 tender dispatch, EDI 214 rate confirmation matching, carrier response tracking, margin analytics by lane and carrier, FMCSA authority verification on carrier onboarding, admin panel for rate management
feature_scope.rationale: Complete feature set for automating broker tendering workflow

mvp_scope.value: Load entry, manual and ranked carrier list, EDI 204 tender dispatch to top 5 carriers, response tracking, rate confirmation matching, basic margin report
mvp_scope.rationale: Core tendering automation proves value before advanced analytics

security_baseline.value: JWT with 8-hour expiry and refresh tokens; TLS 1.3; rate confirmation data encrypted at rest; FMCSA verification before carrier activation; carrier data isolated by tenant
security_baseline.rationale: Financial data and partner-facing access require proper authentication and encryption controls

frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; rich data table and form components for dispatcher workflow

backend_stack.rationale: Fast REST API development; TypeScript end-to-end with React frontend

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16; Redis 7 for session cache and tender response timeouts
data_platform.rationale: Relational model for loads, carriers, and financial records; Redis for timeout-driven carrier escalation logic

hosting_target.value: Hetzner bare-metal Kubernetes in Germany
hosting_target.rationale: Cost-conscious hosting for a small broker operation; Hetzner dedicated servers with k8s for orchestration

privacy_retention_policy.value: Load records retained 7 years for financial audit; carrier rate confirmations retained 7 years; FMCSA verification records retained 3 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Financial record retention requirements for freight brokerage operations

future_scope.value: Automated spot market rate benchmarking, carrier capacity prediction, multi-broker white-label platform
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap contingent on initial broker adoption

constraints.value: Must integrate EDI 204 and EDI 214 with at least 3 major carrier EDI networks at launch; FMCSA carrier authority check required before first tender dispatch to any new carrier
constraints.rationale: Broker regulatory and operational requirements for carrier vetting

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for API metrics; structured logging; PagerDuty for EDI processing failures
observability_baseline.rationale: EDI failures silently blocking tenders are high business impact and require alerting

execution_preference.value: 8-week sprints; EDI integration in sprint 1-2; core load management sprint 3-4; analytics sprint 5
execution_preference.rationale: EDI integration is the highest-risk dependency and should be tackled early

compliance_context.value: FMCSA carrier authority verification required before tender dispatch; financial controls for load payment disbursements; no PCI-DSS scope as payments handled by separate accounting system
compliance_context.rationale: Freight broker regulatory obligations and financial record requirements

project.goal: Automate carrier tendering to reduce cost per load
project.target_users: Freight broker dispatchers and carrier reps

frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for UI

security.auth: custom JWT with refresh tokens

data.cache: Redis 7 for sessions and tender timeouts

constraints.edi: EDI 204/214 for 3+ carrier networks at launch
constraints.fmcsa: authority check before first dispatch to new carrier

reasoner_reviews.product: Automating EDI 204 tender dispatch is the highest-value feature for a small broker: it replaces hours of manual phone and email work per load. The carrier ranking algorithm by lane history is the second key value driver. The margin analytics dashboard must be positioned as a tool for operations managers to identify under-performing carrier relationships, not just cost reporting.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Node.js 20 with Fastify is a good fit for this data volume. The Redis tender timeout mechanism needs explicit design: when a tender is dispatched via EDI 204, a Redis key with TTL should be set per carrier; on expiry a Fastify background worker should automatically escalate to the next carrier in the ranked list. This escalation loop must be idempotent to avoid duplicate tenders. The FMCSA authority verification can use the FMCSA SAFER System web API; results should be cached in PostgreSQL and refreshed periodically, not checked on every dispatch.
reasoner_reviews.security: Financial data including broker margin and carrier rate confirmations must be encrypted at rest. The carrier partner portal needs strict tenant isolation: carriers may only see loads tendered to them, not the full broker load board or other carriers rates. RBAC must prevent dispatchers from viewing or modifying margin data reserved for operations managers.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Three EDI networks at launch (SPS Commerce, TrueCommerce, Kleinschmidt) is achievable with a standard EDI 204/214 implementation but each network has slightly different envelope conventions. An EDI adapter interface should abstract network-specific differences. Hetzner bare-metal Kubernetes is cost-effective but requires manual cluster management; ensure backup and upgrade runbooks are documented.

frontend.app_structure: React 18 SPA with route-based code splitting: /loads (dispatcher dashboard), /loads/{id} (load detail with tender timeline), /carriers (carrier directory), /analytics (margin dashboard), /admin (rate and carrier management). React Query for all API data fetching with optimistic updates for tender status changes.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Load Board: dispatcher sees active loads with carrier assignment status, EDI tender countdown timers, and one-click re-tender action. Load Detail: full tender dispatch history with carrier responses, rate confirmation preview, and margin calculation. Carrier Portal: authenticated carrier view of pending and historical tenders with accept/decline actions. Analytics: lane-level margin charts, carrier performance heatmap, re-negotiation opportunity list.
frontend.state_management: React Query for server state (loads, carriers, tender status); Zustand for UI state (active filters, selected lane, sidebar collapse). WebSocket connection for real-time tender response notifications using native browser WebSocket API.
frontend.frontend_risks: EDI 204 dispatch confirmation latency from carrier EDI networks can be 30-120 seconds; UI must show optimistic pending state and handle eventual confirmation without spinner blocking the dispatcher workflow.

backend.service_design: Fastify server with domain plugins: loads (CRUD, tender dispatch), carriers (onboarding, FMCSA verification cache, ranking), edi (204 dispatch, 214 ingest), analytics (margin aggregation), admin (rate card management). Background worker process for tender timeout escalation and EDI 214 polling.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.1. Versioned at /api/v1. Tender dispatch is async: POST /loads/{id}/tender returns 202 with tender_batch_id; WebSocket pushes status updates as carrier responses arrive.
backend.background_jobs: Redis-backed tender timeout job: each dispatched tender sets a Redis key tender:{load_id}:{carrier_id} with TTL matching the carrier's configured response window (default 30 min); on expiry, escalation worker dispatches to next ranked carrier. EDI 214 polling job for networks not supporting webhook callbacks: polls every 2 minutes and matches rate confirmation to outstanding tender.
backend.failure_handling: EDI dispatch failures logged to tender_events with error_reason; retry with exponential backoff up to 3 attempts; if all retries fail, PagerDuty P2 alert with load_id and carrier_id. Rate confirmation mismatch (EDI 214 rate differs from tendered rate by >1%) held in pending_reconciliation state for dispatcher review.

security.auth_design: Custom JWT signed with RS256; 8-hour access token, 30-day refresh token stored in httpOnly cookie. Carrier portal uses separate token scope carrier_portal restricting accessible routes.
security.authorization_model: RBAC roles: dispatcher (load CRUD, tender dispatch, no margin view), ops_manager (all dispatcher + margin analytics, carrier rate negotiation), admin (full access including user management). Row-level security in PostgreSQL: carriers table filtered by assigned_broker_id; loads visible to carrier accounts only if they have an active tender for that load.
security.secrets_management: JWT signing keys in Kubernetes secrets; EDI network credentials in Kubernetes secrets with RBAC restricting to edi-worker service account; FMCSA API key in Kubernetes secret.
security.security_risks: Carrier portal data leakage if row-level security PostgreSQL policies have gaps; validated by integration tests asserting carrier X cannot retrieve loads tendered to carrier Y.

data.entities: loads (id, broker_id, origin_city, origin_state, destination_city, destination_state, pickup_date, delivery_date, commodity, weight_lbs, equipment_type, status ENUM available/tendering/covered/delivered, created_by_user_id, buy_rate_cents, sell_rate_cents, created_at). carriers (id, broker_id, mc_number, dot_number, company_name, contact_email, fmcsa_status ENUM active/inactive/revoked, fmcsa_verified_at, edi_capable BOOL, response_window_minutes). tender_offers (id, load_id, carrier_id, dispatch_method ENUM edi_204/email, dispatched_at, response_deadline, response ENUM pending/accepted/declined/expired, rate_confirmation_cents, responded_at). rate_confirmations (id, tender_offer_id, edi_214_reference, confirmed_rate_cents, received_at, reconciliation_status ENUM matched/mismatch/pending). lane_histories (id, broker_id, origin_state, destination_state, carrier_id, loads_covered INT, avg_accepted_rate_cents, last_covered_at).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with row-level security policies on carriers and loads filtered by broker_id. Redis 7 for tender timeout keys and JWT refresh token denylist. Financial fields stored as integer cents to avoid floating-point precision issues.
data.retention_and_deletion: Load records and rate confirmations retained 7 years via archival to cold PostgreSQL schema. FMCSA verification records retained 3 years. Tender events retained 2 years in hot schema.

devops.deployment_topology: Hetzner bare-metal Kubernetes: 2 worker nodes for app pods, 1 dedicated PostgreSQL node on local NVMe, 1 Redis node. GitLab CI builds Docker images, pushes to private registry, deploys via Helm charts.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI pipeline: lint (ESLint + Prettier), unit tests (Vitest for frontend, Jest for Fastify routes), integration tests against PostgreSQL test container, Helm deploy to staging on merge to main, manual promotion to production.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping Fastify /metrics endpoint; custom metrics: tender_dispatch_total{method,status}, edi_processing_latency_seconds{network}, carrier_response_time_seconds. Grafana dashboards: tender dispatch success rate, EDI latency per network, carrier response rate by carrier. PagerDuty: EDI dispatch failure after 3 retries, PostgreSQL replica lag > 30 seconds.
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm rollback to prior release via gitlab rollback job. PostgreSQL schema migrations via node-pg-migrate; backwards-compatible migrations required before deploy; rollback migration scripts required for breaking changes.

ISS-001.title: Tender timeout escalation logic not idempotent
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 described Redis TTL-based tender escalation but did not address idempotency: if the Redis expiry fires multiple times due to worker restart, the same load could be tendered twice to the same carrier, creating duplicate EDI 204 transmissions and confusion for carrier EDI systems.

history.note: Identified by architect_reasoner: escalation loop idempotency not addressed

ISS-002.title: FMCSA verification not enforced as gateway before first tender dispatch
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 described FMCSA verification on carrier onboarding but did not document enforcement as a blocking gate in the tender dispatch code path, meaning a carrier with a revoked authority could receive a tender if the verification cache is stale or the enforcement check is missing from the dispatch handler.

history.note: Compliance constraint requires FMCSA check before dispatch; enforcement point not documented

ISS-003.title: Margin data visible to dispatcher role
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 RBAC description did not explicitly exclude dispatchers from margin analytics routes.

history.note: RBAC gap: dispatcher should not see buy_rate or margin
RBAC matrix updated: dispatcher role excludes /analytics routes and buy_rate field in load responses

focus_issues.title: Tender timeout escalation logic not idempotent
focus_issues.detail: Redis TTL expiry firing multiple times on worker restart could dispatch duplicate EDI 204 tenders to same carrier.

focus_issues.title: FMCSA verification not enforced as gateway before first tender dispatch
focus_issues.detail: No enforcement point documented in tender dispatch code path for FMCSA authority status check.

latest_recommendations: Add idempotency key to tender escalation: use PostgreSQL advisory lock or atomic Redis SETNX to ensure only one escalation fires per tender timeout event

Document FMCSA verification as a pre-condition check in the tender dispatch handler: if carrier fmcsa_status != active or fmcsa_verified_at > 30 days ago, reject dispatch and surface error to dispatcher
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Small Freight Broker TMS - Round 1

convergence_state.reason: Two high-severity issues blocking acceptance: duplicate tender risk and FMCSA enforcement gap

previous_audits.summary: Solid TMS architecture for small broker operations with good EDI integration design. Two high-severity gaps prevent acceptance: tender escalation idempotency and missing FMCSA enforcement gate in the dispatch code path. Dispatcher RBAC margin exclusion resolved.
strengths: Redis TTL-based tender timeout escalation is the right pattern for carrier response window management
EDI 204/214 adapter interface abstracting network-specific envelope differences is well-designed
Lane history data model supports the carrier ranking algorithm with appropriate granularity
concerns: Tender escalation Redis expiry can fire multiple times on worker restart; no idempotency mechanism described
FMCSA verification enforced at onboarding but not as a blocking gate in the dispatch handler
Rate confirmation mismatch handling needs quantitative threshold definition
blocking_issues: ISS-001: duplicate EDI 204 dispatches to same carrier due to non-idempotent escalation
ISS-002: FMCSA revoked carrier could receive tender if enforcement point absent from dispatch path
recommendations: Use PostgreSQL advisory lock keyed on load_id+carrier_id+dispatch_sequence to serialize tender escalation attempts
Add fmcsa_status == active AND fmcsa_verified_at < now() - 30 days check as first step in tender dispatch handler, returning 422 with carrier_id and FMCSA status if check fails

issue_updates.title: Tender timeout escalation logic not idempotent
issue_updates.detail: Worker restart could cause Redis expiry to fire escalation handler multiple times

issue_updates.title: FMCSA verification not enforced as gateway before first tender dispatch
issue_updates.detail: Verification described at onboarding but not enforced in dispatch handler

previous_plan.title: Small Freight Broker TMS - Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: React 18 and Node.js Fastify TMS for small freight brokers automating EDI 204 carrier tendering with Redis timeout escalation and lane-history-based carrier ranking.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Fastify REST API with React 18 dispatcher dashboard. Redis TTL keys for tender timeout escalation. PostgreSQL for loads, carriers, and lane history. EDI 204/214 adapter with three carrier networks.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Custom JWT RBAC with dispatcher/ops_manager/admin roles. FMCSA verification on carrier onboarding. Row-level PostgreSQL security. Dispatcher margin exclusion gap present.
previous_plan.data_model: loads, carriers, tender_offers, rate_confirmations, lane_histories tables. Financial values as integer cents. Row-level security by broker_id.
